topics.title: Atherosclerosis Pathogenesis
topics.html:
Topic: Atherosclerosis Pathogenesis — Cardiology
Style: Sea Knowledge study infographic
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

1. Introduction

Atherosclerosis is a chronic inflammatory disease of large/medium elastic and muscular arteries (aorta, coronaries, carotids, iliofemoral). Underlies CAD, stroke, PAD. Develops over decades; clinically silent until plaque rupture.

2. Risk Factors (InterHEART)

  • Modifiable: HTN, DM, smoking, dyslipidaemia (↑LDL), obesity, sedentary, psychosocial
  • Non-modifiable: age, male, family history (first-degree <55 ♂ / <65 ♀), ethnicity
  • Emerging: hsCRP, Lp(a), homocysteine, air pollution

3. Pathogenesis — "Response to Injury" Hypothesis

Stage 1: Endothelial Dysfunction

  • Triggers: LDL, smoking, HTN, diabetes, oxidative stress
  • Loss of NO, PGI₂; ↑ endothelin-1, VCAM-1, ICAM-1
  • Increased permeability to LDL

Stage 2: Lipid Accumulation

  • LDL infiltrates subendothelium, oxidised → oxLDL
  • Monocytes adhere → M1 macrophages → foam cells
  • Fatty streak (earliest visible lesion, even in children)

Stage 3: Inflammatory Response

  • T-cells, macrophages, mast cells in intima
  • Cytokines: TNF-α, IL-1, IL-6, IFN-γ
  • Foam cells + extracellular matrix = atherosclerotic plaque

Stage 4: Plaque Progression

  • Smooth muscle migration from media → intima
  • Synthesis of collagen, elastin, proteoglycans = fibrous cap
  • Lipid core of cholesterol esters, foam cells, debris
  • Calcification (osteoblast-like cells, dystrophic)

Stage 5: Complicated Plaque

  • Fibrous cap thinning by MMPs (matrix metalloproteinases)
  • Plaque rupture (60–70%) or erosion (25–30%) → thrombosis
  • Calcified nodule (rare, elderly, dialysis) → ACS

4. Plaque Morphology

TypeDescription
Fatty streakLipid-laden macrophages, no clinical effect
FibroatheromaLipid core + fibrous cap
Thin-cap fibroatheroma (TCFA)Cap <65 μm, large lipid core — vulnerable
Fibrous plaqueDense collagen, calcified, stable
Calcified noduleEruptive calcification → ACS

5. Vulnerable Plaque

  • Large lipid core (>40% of plaque)
  • Thin fibrous cap (<65 μm)
  • Macrophage infiltration
  • Spotty calcification, neovascularisation
  • Positive remodelling (outward)
  • TCFA = the lesion that ruptures

6. Molecular Mechanisms

  • NF-κB — central inflammatory transcription factor
  • LOX-1 — oxLDL receptor on endothelium
  • MCP-1 — monocyte recruitment
  • TGF-β — SMC proliferation, collagen synthesis
  • MMPs (MMP-1, MMP-9) — cap thinning
  • Tissue factor — thrombogenicity on rupture
  • PCSK9 — LDL receptor degradation; PCSK9 inhibitors ↓ events

7. Stable vs Unstable Plaque

  • Stable: thick cap, small lipid core, calcified, slow stenosis
  • Unstable (vulnerable): thin cap, large lipid, inflamed, may not be flow-limiting (50–70% cause most MIs)

8. Regression

  • Statins ↓ LDL → ↓ lipid core, ↑ cap thickness, ↓ inflammation
  • PCSK9i — robust regression (GLAGOV, REVERSAL)
  • Anti-inflammatory: CANTOS (canakinumab ↓ events), LoDoCo2 (colchicine ↓ events)

9. Atherothrombosis

  • Plaque rupture → exposed lipid core + TF → platelet adhesion (vWF, GPVI)
  • Activation (TxA₂, ADP, thrombin) → aggregation (GP IIb/IIIa) → fibrin
  • STEMI if complete occlusion; NSTEMI/UA if partial

10. Localisation

  • Branching points, bifurcations, curvatures — low shear stress
  • LAD most affected; then RCA, LCx
Pearls
- Atherosclerosis = inflammatory disease of arteries (Ross, Libby)
- LDL is the key driver; oxLDL in intima is the trigger
- TCFA = thin cap (<65 µm) + large lipid + macrophages = vulnerable
- Most MIs from non-flow-limiting lesions (50–70% stenosis)
- Statins & PCSK9i can regress plaque (serial IVUS)

topics.title: Plaque Rupture And Thrombosis
topics.html:
Topic: Plaque Rupture & Thrombosis — Cardiology
Style: Sea Knowledge study infographic
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

1. Introduction

Plaque rupture (60–70%) and plaque erosion (25–30%) are the dominant mechanisms triggering ACS. The final common pathway is intracoronary thrombus — partial (NSTEMI/UA) or occlusive (STEMI).

2. Plaque Rupture

Pathogenesis

  • Vulnerable plaque (TCFA): thin fibrous cap (<65 μm), large necrotic core, macrophage-rich
  • Macrophage MMPs (MMP-1, MMP-2, MMP-9) degrade collagen
  • Apoptosis of SMCs in cap (IFN-γ from Th1) → cap thinning
  • Mechanical stress (HR, BP, vasospasm) + inflammation → fissure
  • Exposes lipid core + tissue factor to blood

Trigger Factors

  • Morning surge (sympathetic) — peak MI 6–12 am
  • Heavy exertion, emotional stress
  • Cocaine, methamphetamine
  • Infection (flu, sepsis)
  • Cold weather

3. Plaque Erosion

  • Intact fibrous cap (unlike rupture)
  • Endothelial apoptosis + neutrophil infiltration
  • More common in women, smokers, younger
  • Smooth muscle-rich plaque, less lipid core
  • Thrombus forms on denuded intima

4. Calcified Nodule

  • Eruptive calcification protrudes into lumen
  • Elderly, dialysis, diabetics
  • 5–10% of ACS
  • Distinct from rupture/erosion

5. Thrombotic Cascade

Platelet Adhesion

  • Subendothelial collagen + vWF → GPIb (platelet) binds vWF
  • GPVI binds collagen (most important)

Platelet Activation

  • TxA₂, ADP (P2Y₁₂), thrombin, serotonin, epinephrine
  • Inside-out signalling → GP IIb/IIIa (αIIbβ3) activated (binds fibrinogen)

Aggregation

  • Fibrinogen bridges activated GP IIb/IIIa
  • Platelet plug (primary haemostasis)

Coagulation

  • Tissue factor from plaque binds FVIIa
  • Extrinsic pathway → FXa → thrombin
  • Thrombin: fibrinogen → fibrin + activates FXIII (crosslinks)
  • Fibrin mesh stabilises thrombus

6. STEMI vs NSTEMI

  • STEMI = complete, occlusive thrombus (TIMI 0–1) → transmural ischaemia → ST elevation
  • NSTEMI/UA = partial, non-occlusive thrombus (TIMI 2–3) → subendocardial ischaemia
  • Distal embolisation of thrombus also contributes

7. Consequences

  • Myocardial ischaemia → angina
  • Infarction if sustained (STEMI >20–30 min)
  • Microvascular obstruction (MVO) — distal embolisation, spasm, oedema
  • Reperfusion injury — stunning, no-reflow, reperfusion arrhythmia
  • Remodelling — LV dilation, aneurysm, HF

8. Vulnerable Blood (Thrombogenicity)

  • ↑ fibrinogen, FVII, PAI-1
  • Smoking, OCP, dehydration, sepsis
  • Hypercoagulable states — Factor V Leiden, prothrombin gene, nephrotic syndrome

9. Prevention / Treatment Targets

  • Antiplatelet: aspirin (COX-1 → ↓TxA₂), P2Y₁₂ inhibitors (clopidogrel, ticagrelor, prasugrel)
  • Anticoagulant: heparin (UFH, enoxaparin, fondaparinux, bivalirudin)
  • Statins — pleiotropic (plaque stabilisation, ↓ inflammation)
  • ACEi / ARB — vascular protection
  • PCSK9i — robust LDL reduction
  • Colchicine (LoDoCo2) — anti-inflammatory

10. Plaque Stabilisation

  • Statins → ↑ collagen, ↓ MMPs, ↓ macrophages, ↓ lipid
  • Reduces acute events even before stenosis regression
Pearls
- Rupture 60–70%, erosion 25–30%, calcified nodule 5–10%
- TCFA = thin cap (<65 μm) + large necrotic core + macrophages
- GP IIb/IIIa (αIIbβ3) = final common pathway of aggregation
- STEMI = occlusive thrombus; NSTEMI = partial thrombus
- Morning surge (sympathetic) — peak MI 6–12 am
- Statins stabilise plaque independent of LDL reduction

topics.title: Acs Classification Stemi Nstemi Ua
topics.html:
Topic: ACS Classification — Cardiology
Style: Sea Knowledge study infographic
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

1. Introduction

ACS is the spectrum of acute myocardial ischaemia due to sudden reduction in coronary flow. Three entities, classified by ECG and biomarkers:

  • STEMI — ST elevation MI (transmural, complete occlusion)
  • NSTEMI — non-ST elevation MI (subendocardial, partial occlusion, troponin ↑)
  • UA — unstable angina (ischaemia, no necrosis, troponin normal)

2. Spectrum

FeatureSTEMINSTEMIUA
PathologyComplete occlusionPartial occlusionPartial / dynamic
ECGST elevationST depression, TWI, non-specificST depression, TWI, normal
Troponin↑↑↑Normal
PainAt rest, prolongedRest, prolongedRest, brief
RiskHighestIntermediateLower

3. STEMI — Universal Definition (2018, 4th UDMI)

  • ST elevation at J-point in two contiguous leads:
  • ♂ <40 yr: ≥2.5 mm V2–V3, ≥1 mm other
  • ♂ ≥40 yr: ≥2 mm V2–V3, ≥1 mm other
  • ♀: ≥1.5 mm V2–V3, ≥1 mm other
  • V7–V9 (posterior): ≥0.5 mm (♂ <40: ≥1 mm)
  • V3R, V4R (RV): ≥0.5 mm (esp. ♂ <30: ≥1 mm)
  • New LBBB + symptoms (no longer diagnostic alone — Sgarbossa criteria help)
  • Trop ↑ + clinical evidence (in absence of ST elevation)
  • Posterior MI: ST depression V1–V3 with ST elevation V7–V9 ≥0.5 mm

4. NSTEMI Definition

  • ACS without ST elevation
  • Troponin elevation (above 99th percentile) with rise/fall
  • ECG: ST depression, T-wave inversion, or non-specific changes
  • Type 1 (plaque rupture) vs Type 2 (supply-demand mismatch)

5. UA Definition

  • Rest angina (>20 min at rest)
  • New-onset severe angina (CCS III)
  • Crescendo angina (↑ frequency, duration, threshold)
  • Troponin normal (key distinction from NSTEMI)
  • Often progresses to NSTEMI if untreated

6. Type 1 vs Type 2 MI

  • Type 1 — primary coronary event (plaque rupture/erosion) → NSTEMI/STEMI
  • Type 2 — supply-demand mismatch (anaemia, AF with RVR, hypotension, anaesthetic) — common in hospitalised
  • Type 3 — sudden cardiac death with ischaemia (no biomarker)
  • Type 4a — PCI-related (trop >5x ULN + new ECG/Q)
  • Type 4b — stent thrombosis
  • Type 5 — CABG-related

7. Other ACS Mimics (Troponin ↑ but non-ACS)

  • Myocarditis, Takotsubo
  • Pulmonary embolism
  • Aortic dissection
  • Acute HF, tachyarrhythmia
  • Renal failure (chronic troponin elevation)
  • Cardiac contusion, cardioversion

8. Risk Stratification

  • GRACE score — in-hospital and 6-month mortality
  • TIMI score — 30-day death/MI/UR
  • HEART score — 6-week MACE
  • All incorporate age, haemodynamics, ECG, biomarkers

9. ECG Territory (STEMI)

LeadsArtery
V1–V4 (anteroseptal)LAD
I, aVL, V5–V6 (lateral)LCx
II, III, aVF (inferior)RCA (90%), LCx (10%)
V1–V2 ST depression (posterior)RCA / LCx
V3R, V4RRV branch of RCA
New LBBBLAD

10. Initial Management

  • STEMI — emergent reperfusion: primary PCI <90 min (or fibrinolysis if not feasible <120 min)
  • NSTEMI/UA — risk-stratify → early invasive (<24 h GRACE >140, refractory ischaemia, dynamic ST) vs ischaemia-guided
  • All: MONA-B (morphine, O₂, nitrate, aspirin, β-blocker) — modern adjust: O₂ only if SpO₂<90, nitrates cautiously
Pearls
- STEMI = occlusive; NSTEMI/UA = partial thrombus
- Trop distinguishes NSTEMI (↑) from UA (normal)
- Type 1 MI = plaque rupture; Type 2 = supply-demand mismatch
- Posterior MI = ST depression V1–V3 + ST elevation V7–V9
- New LBBB alone no longer diagnostic — use Sgarbossa criteria
- GRACE score best validated for in-hospital and 6-month mortality

topics.title: Cardiac Biomarkers Troponin Ck Mb Myoglobin
topics.html:
Topic: Cardiac Biomarkers — Cardiology
Style: Sea Knowledge study infographic
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

1. Introduction

Cardiac biomarkers are released into the circulation following myocardial injury. High-sensitivity troponin (hs-cTn) is the gold standard for diagnosing MI, with CK-MB and myoglobin as adjuncts (mostly historical).

2. Troponin

Structure

  • Cardiac troponin complex = TnC, TnT, TnI
  • Regulates actin-myosin interaction (calcium-mediated)
  • Cardiac isoforms: cTnT and cTnI are cardiac-specific (different from skeletal)

High-Sensitivity Troponin (hs-cTn)

  • Detects 10–100× lower concentrations than conventional assays
  • 99th percentile cutoff (sex-specific: ♀ <15.6 ng/L, ♂ <34.2 ng/L for hs-cTnI Abbott)
  • Coefficient of variation (CV) <10% at 99th percentile
  • Detectable in normal individuals (high sensitivity)

Kinetics

  • Rises 1 h post-injury; peak 24 h; remains elevated 7–14 days
  • Half-life cTnT ~120 min, cTnI ~120 min
  • Re-elevation after initial fall = re-infarction (especially within 48 h)
  • Single measurement at 0 h can rule out with some assays (limit of detection)

Diagnosis of MI

  • Rise and/or fall of troponin with at least one value >99th percentile
  • + clinical evidence of ischaemia (symptoms, ECG, imaging)
  • Universal definition (4th UDMI, 2018)

ESC 0/1-hour or 0/2-hour Algorithm

  • hs-cTnT/I at 0 h:
  • < LoD (limit of detection) → rule out (NPV >99%)
  • > Rule-in cutoff → rule in (MI)
  • Intermediate → repeat at 1 h or 2 h
  • Δ values (delta) at 1 h: rule-in if Δhs-cTnT ≥3 ng/L (early presenters) or ≥5–7 ng/L (late presenters)
  • Rule-out if Δ <3 ng/L + low baseline

Non-MI Causes of Troponin Elevation

  • Cardiac: myocarditis, Takotsubo, HF, AF with RVR, ablation, contusion, cardioversion, ACS-related (type 2), pericarditis
  • Non-cardiac: PE, aortic dissection, sepsis, renal failure (chronic elevation), SAH, stroke, anaemia, hypoxia, drug toxicity (5-FU, anthracyclines)
  • Renal failure — chronic troponin elevation, but a rise is still significant

3. CK-MB

  • Creatine kinase, muscle-brain isoform
  • Rises 3–6 h, peaks 12–24 h, normalises 48–72 h
  • Mass assay preferred over activity
  • Less specific than troponin (skeletal muscle, brain, smooth muscle, renal)
  • Used for: re-infarction (falls faster than troponin), infarct size estimation, post-PCI/CABG
  • CK-MB/total CK ratio >5% suggests cardiac source

4. Myoglobin

  • Heme protein in skeletal and cardiac muscle
  • Rises 1–2 h, peaks 6–9 h, normalises 24 h
  • Earliest to rise but non-specific (rhabdomyolysis, exercise, renal failure)
  • Low sensitivity/specificity — replaced by troponin
  • May be useful in early rule-out when combined with troponin and ECG

5. Other Markers (Mostly Historical)

  • AST, LDH, total CK — historical
  • BNP / NT-proBNP — HF (rule in/out), prognostic in MI
  • CRP, hs-CRP — inflammatory; prognostic
  • D-dimer — PE rule-out
  • Lp(a), LDL, HDL — risk assessment

6. Point-of-Care Troponin

  • Rapid (<20 min) troponin I/T assays
  • Sensitivity 80–90% (less than central hs-cTn)
  • Used in ED triage for early rule-out
  • Negative POC + low risk can discharge (HEART score)

7. Special Situations

  • Renal failure — chronic elevation of troponin; rise/fall still diagnostic
  • Post-PCI — type 4a MI: cTn >5x ULN (stable) or >10x ULN (if baseline elevated) + new ECG/Q
  • Post-CABG — type 5 MI: cTn >10x ULN + new Q/LBBB/LV dysfunction
  • Stroke — central catecholamine surge → troponin leak (not ACS)
Pearls
- hs-cTn = gold standard for MI diagnosis (sex-specific 99th percentile)
- 0/1 h or 0/2 h algorithm rules out MI rapidly (NPV >99%)
- Troponin ↑ is NOT specific for ACS — must have rise AND fall + clinical context
- CK-MB useful for re-infarction (faster fall than troponin)
- Renal failure — chronic troponin elevation, but rise/fall still significant
- Type 4a MI post-PCI: cTn >5x ULN + new Q/ischaemia

topics.title: Risk Scores Grace Timi Heart
topics.html:
Topic: ACS Risk Scores — Cardiology
Style: Sea Knowledge study infographic
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

1. Introduction

Risk scores in ACS stratify prognosis and guide management (early invasive vs conservative, ICU admission, length of stay, secondary prevention). Most used: GRACE, TIMI, HEART.

2. GRACE Score (Global Registry of Acute Coronary Events)

Variables (8)

  • Age (years)
  • HR (bpm)
  • SBP (mmHg)
  • Creatinine (mg/dL or μmol/L)
  • Killip class (I–IV)
  • Cardiac arrest at admission (yes/no)
  • ST deviation (yes/no)
  • Elevated cardiac biomarkers (yes/no)

Outcomes Predicted

  • In-hospital mortality
  • 6-month mortality
  • 1-year mortality
  • 3-year mortality (GRACE 2.0)

Risk Categories (In-hospital mortality)

  • Low: GRACE <109 → mortality <1%
  • Intermediate: 109–140 → 1–3%
  • High: >140 → >3%

Use

  • Best validated for ACS prognosis
  • Class I recommendation (ESC, AHA/ACC) for risk stratification
  • Guides timing of invasive strategy (NSTEMI)
  • GRACE >140 → early invasive <24 h
  • GRACE 109–140 → invasive <72 h
  • GRACE <109 → ischaemia-guided

3. TIMI Score (Thrombolysis in Myocardial Infarction)

For NSTEMI/UA (7 variables, 0–7 points)

  • Age ≥65 (1)
  • ≥3 CAD risk factors (FH, HTN, DM, smoking, dyslipidaemia) (1)
  • Known CAD (≥50% stenosis) (1)
  • Aspirin use in last 7 days (1)
  • Severe angina (≥2 episodes in 24 h) (1)
  • ST deviation ≥0.5 mm (1)
  • Elevated cardiac biomarkers (1)

Interpretation

  • 0–1: 4.7% MACE at 14 days
  • 2: 8.3%
  • 3: 13.2%
  • 4: 19.9%
  • 5–7: ≥26.2%

STEMI TIMI Score (0–14)

  • Age, DM, HTN, exam, weight, SBP, HR, Killip, anterior MI, time to Rx

Use

  • Simple, bedside
  • Less accurate than GRACE

4. HEART Score (History, ECG, Age, Risk factors, Troponin)

Variables (0–10)

  • History: 0 (slightly suspicious) / 1 (moderately) / 2 (highly suspicious) (3 pts)
  • ECG: 0 (normal) / 1 (non-specific, LBBB, LVH, repol changes) / 2 (significant ST deviation not due to LVH/LBBB) (2 pts)
  • Age: <45 (0), 45–64 (1), ≥65 (2)
  • Risk factors (HTN, DM, dyslipidaemia, obesity, smoking, FH, atherosclerosis): 0 (none) / 1 (1–2) / 2 (≥3 or hx of atherosclerosis)
  • Troponin: 0 (≤normal) / 1 (1–3× normal) / 2 (>3× normal)

Interpretation

  • 0–3 — low risk (MACE 1.7%) — discharge
  • 4–6 — moderate risk (MACE ~16%) — observe/admit, stress test
  • 7–10 — high risk (MACE 50–65%) — early invasive

Use

  • ED triage of undifferentiated chest pain
  • Validated, widely used in EDs
  • Modified HEART (incorporates hs-cTn)

5. PURSUIT, PREDICT, AMIS, ZWOLLE Scores

  • Less commonly used
  • PURSUIT — NSTEMI/UA, similar to GRACE
  • Zwolle — primary PCI mortality in STEMI

6. Comparison

ScoreBest ForStrengthWeakness
GRACENSTEMI/UA, STEMIBest validated, accurateNeeds bloods, more variables
TIMINSTEMI/UA, STEMISimpleLess accurate
HEARTED chest pain triageEasy, incorporates troponinLess granular
PURSUITNSTEMI/UAOlderSupplanted by GRACE

7. Bleeding Risk Scores

  • CRUSADE — NSTEMI bleeding risk (in-hospital major bleeding)
  • ACUITY / HORIZONS — for NSTEMI PCI
  • Used to balance ischaemic vs bleeding risk (e.g., DAPT duration, access site)

8. Other Risk Markers

  • LVEF — strongest single predictor of long-term mortality
  • hs-CRP — inflammation (CANTOS)
  • BNP / NT-proBNP — HF, prognosis
  • Renal function (eGFR) — strong predictor
  • Time to reperfusion (STEMI) — door-to-balloon <90 min
  • Cumulative risk factors — DM, CKD, age, prior MI

9. Risk-Based Management

  • NSTEMI:
  • GRACE >140 → early invasive <24 h
  • GRACE 109–140 → invasive <72 h
  • GRACE <109 → ischaemia-guided
  • STEMI — primary PCI regardless of score
  • Discharge — HEART ≤3, low TIMI/GRACE
Pearls
- GRACE >140 = high-risk NSTEMI → invasive <24 h
- HEART ≤3 = low risk → safe to discharge from ED
- TIMI 5–7 = high-risk NSTEMI → early invasive
- GRACE best validated; HEART best for ED triage
- LVEF is the strongest single long-term predictor
- Use bleeding risk (CRUSADE) and ischaemic risk to balance DAPT

topics.title: Stemi Ecg Criteria St Elevation Hyperacute T Waves Reciprocal Changes
topics.html:
Topic: STEMI ECG Criteria — Cardiology
Style: Sea Knowledge study infographic
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

1. Introduction

STEMI = ST elevation MI — transmural ischaemia from complete coronary occlusion. Defined by ST elevation at the J-point in ≥2 contiguous leads (4th Universal Definition, 2018). "Time is muscle" — early recognition saves myocardium.

2. ST Elevation Thresholds (J-point)

Lead Group♂ <40 yr♂ ≥40 yr♀
V2–V3≥2.5 mm≥2.0 mm≥1.5 mm
All other leads≥1.0 mm≥1.0 mm≥1.0 mm
V7–V9 (posterior)≥1.0 mm (♂ <40)≥0.5 mm≥0.5 mm
V3R, V4R (RV)≥1.0 mm (♂ <30)≥0.5 mm≥0.5 mm

3. Classic Evolution (Stage by Stage)

StageTimeECGPathology
Hyperacute T<30 minTall, broad, peaked T (early)Reversible ischaemia
ST elevationmin–hoursConcave-up ST ↑, often "tombstone"Injury (transmural)
Q wavehours–daysPathological Q (>40 ms, >25% R)Necrosis
T inversiondays–weeksSymmetric TWIPeri-infarct
Normalised STweeksQ persists, T normalisesScar

4. Hyperacute T Waves

  • Tall, broad, "tented" T waves, often in same leads that subsequently elevate
  • Earliest sign of STEMI (first 30 min)
  • Tall anterior T in V2–V4 = early anterior STEMI
  • May be subtle — compare to previous ECG

5. ST Elevation Patterns

  • Concave up (often early, less specific)
  • Convex / tombstone (more specific for transmural injury)
  • ≥1 mm in two contiguous leads (or as above)
  • Reciprocal ST depression (opposite leads) — increases specificity (e.g., inferior ST↑ + lateral ST↓)

6. Pathological Q Waves

  • Width ≥40 ms (1 small box)
  • Depth ≥25% of R wave
  • Develops hours to days
  • Persists indefinitely
  • Not all Q waves are pathological — III, aVL, V1, V2 (septal) can be normal

7. Reciprocal Changes

  • ST depression in leads opposite to ST elevation
  • Inferior STEMI (II, III, aVF) + ST↓ in I, aVL = high specificity
  • Anterior STEMI (V1–V4) + ST↓ in inferior leads = indicates proximal LAD, larger infarct
  • Absence of reciprocal change does NOT rule out STEMI

8. Coronary Territory

LeadsArteryWall
V1–V2Septal (LAD)Septal
V3–V4Anterior (LAD)Anterior
V5–V6, I, aVLLateral (LCx / diagonal)Lateral
II, III, aVFInferior (RCA 90%, LCx 10%)Inferior
V7–V9Posterior (LCx / RCA)Posterior
V3R, V4RRV (RCA)RV

9. Posterior MI

  • ST depression in V1–V3 (mirror image)
  • Tall, broad R wave in V1–V2 (true posterior Q)
  • Confirm with V7–V9: ST elevation ≥0.5 mm
  • Usually from LCx or distal RCA

10. Right Ventricular MI

  • ST elevation ≥1 mm in V3R, V4R (most specific)
  • Inferior MI + hypotension → think RV infarct
  • Confirm with V4R (RCA territory)
  • Avoid nitrates and diuretics (preload-dependent)

11. STEMI Equivalents

  • New LBBB with Sgarbossa criteria
  • de Winter T waves (upsloping ST depression V1–V6 + tall T) — proximal LAD
  • Wellens syndrome (deep symmetric TWI V2–V3) — critical proximal LAD
  • Posterior MI (V1–V3 ST depression)
  • Hyperacute T waves
  • South African flag sign (persistent ST elevation after MI = LV aneurysm)

12. Sgarbossa Criteria (LBBB / Paced rhythm)

  • ≥1 mm concordant ST elevation in leads with positive QRS (5 pts)
  • ≥1 mm concordant ST depression in V1–V3 (3 pts)
  • ≥5 mm discordant ST elevation in leads with negative QRS (2 pts)
  • Modified Sgarbossa (Smith-modified): ST/S ratio ≤−0.25 = positive
  • Total ≥3 = diagnostic of STEMI in LBBB

13. Localisation from ECG

  • LAD proximal — ST↑ aVR, aVL, new RBBB, anterior + septal
  • LAD mid — anterior + septal
  • LAD distal — apical, ± V7–V9
  • RCA proximal — inferior + RV (V4R)
  • LCx — lateral, posterior
Pearls
- ≥1 mm ST elevation in 2 contiguous leads = STEMI (use sex/age cutoffs for V2–V3)
- Reciprocal changes ↑ specificity (e.g., inferior + lateral ST↓)
- Hyperacute T waves = earliest sign (tall, broad, tented)
- V1–V3 ST depression = posterior MI (get V7–V9)
- Inferior MI + hypotension → V4R for RV infarct; avoid nitrates
- Sgarbossa ≥3 = STEMI in LBBB; modified Sgarbossa ST/S ratio better

topics.title: Stemi Mimics Lvh Lbbb Pericarditis Brugada Early Repolarization
topics.html:
Topic: STEMI Mimics — Cardiology
Style: Sea Knowledge study infographic
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

1. Introduction

Many conditions cause ST elevation without acute coronary occlusion. Accurate differentiation prevents unnecessary thrombolysis or PCI. Always combine ECG with history, troponin, echo (WMA in vascular territory).

2. Common Mimics

Left Ventricular Hypertrophy (LVH)

  • High QRS voltage + ST elevation in V1–V3
  • ST elevation in V1–V3 is discordant to deep S wave
  • Discordant ST elevation >5 mm or concordant = abnormal (STEMI in LVH — "Sgarbossa-equivalent")
  • Strain pattern: ST depression with TWI in lateral leads

Left Bundle Branch Block (LBBB)

  • Wide QRS ≥120 ms, monomorphic V1 (rS or QS), broad R in I, aVL, V6
  • ST elevation in V1–V3 is expected (discordant to negative QRS)
  • Use Sgarbossa (or modified) to diagnose STEMI in LBBB
  • Modified Sgarbossa — ST/S ratio ≤−0.25 — more sensitive

Pericarditis

  • Diffuse concave-up ST elevation (most leads, except aVR, V1)
  • PR depression (highly specific)
  • PR elevation in aVR (knuckle sign)
  • No reciprocal changes (vs STEMI)
  • Spodick sign — downsloping TP segment
  • Stage progression: ST↑ → TWI → normalisation (vs Q in STEMI)

Brugada Syndrome

  • Type 1 (coved): ST elevation V1–V2 ≥2 mm with negative T ("Brugada sign") — diagnostic
  • Type 2 (saddleback): ST ≥0.5 mm with positive/biphasic T
  • More common in ♂, Asian, family history SCD
  • Sodium channel blockers (ajmaline, flecainide) unmask
  • Risk of VF → ICD if symptomatic

Early Repolarisation

  • J-point notching/slurring in lateral/inferior leads
  • Concave-up ST elevation with tall T waves
  • Common in young, healthy, ♂, athletes, Black
  • Generally benign — but inferior early repol linked to idiopathic VF
  • Distinguish from STEMI by absence of dynamic change, symptoms, troponin

Benign Early Repolarisation vs STEMI

FeatureBERSTEMI
ST morphologyConcaveConvex (tombstone)
Reciprocal changeNoYes
Dynamic changeNoYes
TroponinNormalElevated
PainNoYes
T waveTall, asymmetricHyperacute/tented

Hyperkalaemia

  • Peaked T waves, wide QRS, sine wave (severe)
  • ST elevation uncommon but can occur

Takotsubo (Apical Ballooning)

  • ST elevation in precordial leads (often anterior)
  • Anterior WMA on echo (apical ballooning) — but non-vascular distribution
  • Coronary angiography normal
  • ↑ troponin (mild); BNP disproportionately high
  • Trigger: emotional/physical stress, ♀ postmenopausal

Other Mimics

  • LV aneurysm — persistent ST elevation (after MI), Q waves, no dynamic change, echo confirms
  • Takotsubo — see above
  • Cardiac contusion, myocarditis, PE (especially anterior with RV strain)
  • Hypothermia — Osborn (J) wave
  • Hypercalcaemia — short QT
  • Paced rhythm, WPW — abnormal depolarisation → ST/T changes

3. How to Differentiate

  1. Clinical context — chest pain, risk factors, duration
  2. Serial ECG — dynamic change favours STEMI
  3. Compare to old ECG — if old ST elevation, may be chronic
  4. Troponin — rise/fall favours MI
  5. Echocardiogram — WMA in vascular territory = MI; global = myopericarditis
  6. Sgarbossa / modified in LBBB
  7. Coronary angiography — gold standard (when needed)

4. Red Flags (Concerning for True STEMI)

  • Convex ST elevation ("tombstone")
  • Reciprocal ST depression
  • Dynamic ST change on serial ECG
  • Concordant ST elevation in LBBB (Sgarbossa)
  • New Q wave
  • Elevated troponin
  • WMA in coronary territory on echo
Pearls
- Diffuse ST elevation + PR depression = pericarditis (not STEMI)
- Sgarbossa ≥3 = STEMI in LBBB; modified ST/S ratio better
- BER = concave ST elevation, tall T, no reciprocal change, no pain
- Brugada Type 1 = coved ST in V1–V2 with negative T (diagnostic)
- LV aneurysm = persistent ST elevation post-MI, no dynamic change
- When in doubt, echo (WMA in vascular territory) or coronary angio

topics.title: Primary Pci Protocol Door To Balloon Stent Choice Anticoagulation
topics.html:
Topic: Primary PCI Protocol — Cardiology
Style: Sea Knowledge study infographic
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

1. Definition

Primary PCI = emergent percutaneous coronary intervention in STEMI (or new LBBB with symptoms) without prior fibrinolysis. Goal: restore antegrade flow in the infarct-related artery (IRA). Preferred reperfusion strategy when achievable within 120 min of first medical contact (FMC).

2. Indications

  • STEMI with symptoms ≤12 h (Class I)
  • Cardiogenic shock irrespective of time window
  • New LBBB with symptoms (controversial — Sgarbossa-positive)
  • Persistent ischaemia 12–24 h (Class IIa)
  • Late presentation >24 h with occluded IRA — generally no PCI (OAT trial)

3. Time Targets

MetricTarget
FMC → ECG≤10 min
Door-in → Door-out (non-PCI hospital)≤30 min
FMC → Device activation≤90 min (PCI-capable)
FMC → Device activation≤120 min (transfer)
Door → Balloon (direct admission)≤60 min
Delays >120 min → consider fibrinolysis if no contraindications.

4. Pre-procedure Medications

  • Aspirin 150–300 mg chewed (loading)
  • P2Y12 inhibitor loading:
  • Ticagrelor 180 mg (preferred)
  • Prasugrel 60 mg (if no stroke/TIA, age <75, weight >60 kg)
  • Clopidogrel 600 mg (only if others unavailable)
  • Anticoagulant (one):
  • Unfractionated heparin (UFH) 70–100 U/kg bolus
  • Enoxaparin 0.5 mg/kg IV bolus
  • Bivalirudin 0.75 mg/kg bolus + infusion (if heparin contraindicated)

5. Stent Choice

  • Drug-eluting stent (DES) — standard of care (lower restenosis vs BMS)
  • Newer-generation everolimus- or zotarolimus-eluting stents preferred
  • BMS reserved for: large STEMI with need for dual antiplatelet interruption, bleeding risk, surgery planned
  • Bifurcation lesions — provisional stenting (main vessel first)
  • Aspiration thrombectomy — NOT routine (TOTAL trial); consider in large thrombus burden

6. Periprocedural Anticoagulation

  • UFH — most common; activated clotting time (ACT) target 250–300 s (300–350 with GP IIb/IIIa)
  • Bivalirudin — non-inferior to UFH; lower bleeding; transient ACS mortality signal in early trials mitigated by prolonging infusion
  • Enoxaparin — non-inferior to UFH (ATOLL trial)
  • Fondaparinux — avoid as sole anticoagulant in STEMI (catheter thrombosis risk)
  • GP IIb/IIIa inhibitors (abciximab, eptifibatide, tirofiban) — bail-out only (high bleeding, no routine benefit)

7. Vessel Access

  • Radial preferred (lower bleeding, mortality benefit in ACS — RIVAL, MATRIX)
  • Femoral if radial fails, hemodynamic instability, or anatomy unsuitable

8. Culprit-Only vs Multivessel PCI

  • Culprit-only at index procedure (default in shock: CULPRIT-SHOCK)
  • Staged non-culprit PCI within 1–6 weeks for residual disease (COMPLETE, FUTURE)
  • Immediate multivessel PCI in stable patients: supported by recent evidence but still debated

9. Post-PCI Care

  • DAPT: aspirin + P2Y12 (ticagrelor/prasugrel) ≥12 months
  • Anticoagulation: stop periprocedural agent (unless other indication)
  • Statin, beta-blocker (within 24 h if stable), ACEi/ARB (within 24 h)
  • Risk-stratify for ICD if LVEF ≤35% at 40 days
Pearls
- FMC-to-device ≤90 min (PCI hospital) / ≤120 min (transfer) — every minute = myocyte loss
- DES > BMS in STEMI (lower restenosis, similar safety)
- Ticagrelor or prasugrel preferred over clopidogrel
- Radial access > femoral in ACS
- Avoid fondaparinux monotherapy (catheter thrombosis)
- Culprit-only in shock; staged complete revascularisation if multivessel disease

topics.title: Fibrinolysis Indications Contraindications Agents Rescue Pci
topics.html:
Topic: Fibrinolysis in STEMI — Cardiology
Style: Sea Knowledge study infographic
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

1. Definition

Fibrinolysis = pharmacologic reperfusion using thrombolytic agents to dissolve the occlusive coronary thrombus in STEMI. Used when primary PCI cannot be performed within 120 min of first medical contact (FMC).

2. Indications

  • STEMI with symptoms ≤12 h (Class I)
  • No contraindications to fibrinolysis
  • PCI not available within 120 min of FMC
  • ECG shows ST elevation ≥1 mm in ≥2 contiguous leads (or new LBBB with Sgarbossa)
  • True posterior MI (ST depression V1–V3 with confirmed posterior STEMI)

3. Absolute Contraindications

  • Prior intracranial haemorrhage (any time)
  • Known cerebral vascular malformation (e.g., AVM, aneurysm)
  • Known intracranial neoplasm
  • Ischaemic stroke within 3 months (excluding acute stroke <4.5 h)
  • Active internal bleeding (excluding menses)
  • Suspected aortic dissection
  • Significant closed-head or facial trauma within 3 months
  • Intracranial or intraspinal surgery within 2 months

4. Relative Contraindications

  • BP >180/110 mmHg (must lower first)
  • Recent major surgery (<3 weeks)
  • Recent GI bleed (<1 month)
  • Pregnancy
  • Active peptic ulcer
  • Prolonged CPR (>10 min)
  • Current anticoagulation with elevated INR
  • Non-compressible vascular puncture
  • Prior allergic reaction to fibrinolytic agent

5. Time Targets

  • Door → Needle ≤30 min
  • Best outcomes if given <3 h of symptom onset
  • Benefit diminishes but still present up to 12 h
  • Beyond 12 h → PCI preferred (or no reperfusion)

6. Fibrinolytic Agents

AgentDoseFibrin SpecificityNotes
Tenecteplase (TNK)Single bolus (weight-adjusted)HighPreferred — easiest, single bolus
Reteplase (rPA)2 boluses, 30 min apartHighDouble-bolus regimen
Alteplase (tPA)90-min infusionHighFront-loaded regimen
Streptokinase1.5 MU over 30–60 minLowCheap, antigenic, hypotension

7. Adjunctive Therapy

  • Aspirin 150–325 mg chewed at presentation, then indefinitely
  • P2Y12 inhibitor (clopidogrel 300 mg loading, then 75 mg daily)
  • Anticoagulation for ≥48 h (or up to 8 days):
  • Enoxaparin 1 mg/kg SC q12h (preferred over UFH in fibrinolysis — ExTRACT)
  • UFH 60 U/kg bolus → 12 U/kg/h infusion
  • Fondaparinux 2.5 mg SC daily (effective, OASIS-6)
  • Statin, beta-blocker (avoid if shock/HF), ACEi

8. Monitoring Post-Fibrinolysis

  • ECG 60–90 min post-bolus — look for >50% ST resolution (success)
  • Cardiac biomarkers (troponin peaks earlier)
  • Reperfusion arrhythmias (idioventricular rhythm, AF, VT — usually transient)
  • Bleeding watch (intracranial, GI, puncture sites)

9. Successful vs Failed Reperfusion

Success (clinical):

  • ST resolution ≥50% at 60–90 min
  • Resolution of chest pain
  • Reperfusion arrhythmia
  • Idioventricular rhythm (AIVR)

Failure (consider rescue PCI):

  • Persistent ST elevation
  • Persistent pain
  • Hemodynamic instability
  • → Rescue PCI within 12 h (REACT, MERLIN trials — better outcomes than repeat fibrinolysis or conservative)

10. Rescue PCI

  • Urgent PCI after failed fibrinolysis
  • Indicated for: persistent ST elevation, chest pain, hemodynamic instability
  • Performed at the PCI-capable centre after transfer
  • Avoid repeat fibrinolysis (increased bleeding, no benefit)
  • Routine early PCI (3–24 h after successful fibrinolysis) — strategy of choice (CARESS-in-AMI, TRANSFER-AMI)

11. Bleeding Complications

  • Intracranial haemorrhage — most feared (0.5–1%, higher with TNK/rPA vs SK)
  • Major bleeding 1–2%
  • Management: stop fibrinolytic, cryoprecipitate, FFP, platelets, antifibrinolytics (tranexamic acid)
  • Avoid further antiplatelets/anticoagulants until bleeding controlled
Pearls
- Door-to-needle ≤30 min if PCI unavailable within 120 min
- Tenecteplase (TNK) = preferred (single bolus, weight-adjusted)
- Clopidogrel is the P2Y12 of choice (ticagrelor/prasugrel not studied with fibrinolysis — risk of catastrophic bleeding)
- Enoxaparin > UFH as adjunct (ExTRACT-TIMI 25)
- ST resolution ≥50% at 90 min = success
- Failed lysis → rescue PCI (do not repeat fibrinolysis)
- Routine early PCI 3–24 h after successful lysis improves outcomes

topics.title: Stemi Complications Mechanical Arrhythmic Hf Pericarditis
topics.html:
Topic: STEMI Complications — Cardiology
Style: Sea Knowledge study infographic
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

1. Overview

STEMI complications can be grouped as:

  • Arrhythmic (most common)
  • Mechanical (highest mortality)
  • Inflammatory (pericarditis)
  • Hemodynamic (HF, shock)
  • Thromboembolic (LV thrombus)

2. Arrhythmic Complications

ArrhythmiaTimingManagement
VF / pulseless VT<24 h (most common <1 h)Defibrillation, ACLS, β-blocker reduces risk
Accelerated idioventricular rhythm (AIVR)Reperfusion markerObservation (usually transient)
Sinus bradycardiaInferior MI (RCA)Atropine, pacing if symptomatic
AV blockInferior MI (AV node ischaemia)Atropine, temporary pacing
New BBB / RBBBProximal LADHigh mortality; consider pacing
AF5–20%Rate control, anticoagulation
Sustained VTLate (post-MI)ICD workup, antiarrhythmics
  • Lidocaine / amiodarone for VT (prophylaxis no longer routine)
  • β-blocker within 24 h reduces VF and mortality

3. Mechanical Complications (1–7 days)

ComplicationVesselClinicalDiagnosisManagement
Free wall ruptureLADSudden death, tamponadeEcho (pericardial effusion)Emergent surgery (often fatal)
VSR (ventricular septal rupture)LAD/RCANew harsh holosystolic murmur, thrill, biventricular failureEcho with colour Doppler, PA catheter (step-up)Surgical repair (mortality >50%); IABP bridge
Papillary muscle ruptureRCA (posteromedial)Acute severe MR, pulmonary oedemaEcho (TEE)Emergency surgery; IABP, diuretics, vasodilators
  • Most occur 3–7 days post-MI (coincides with macrophage-mediated collagen degradation)
  • Risk factors: advanced age, female, hypertension, late reperfusion

4. Heart Failure & Cardiogenic Shock

  • LV failure → acute pulmonary oedema
  • Cardiogenic shock — typically within 24 h; mortality 50–80%
  • SCAI stages A (at risk) → E (extremis)
  • Management: inotropes (dobutamine, norepinephrine), IABP, Impella, VA-ECMO; revascularisation (CULPRIT-SHOCK — culprit-only)

5. Pericarditis

Early (peri-infarction pericarditis):

  • Within <4 days of MI
  • From transmural inflammation adjacent to pericardium
  • Treatment: high-dose aspirin (avoid NSAIDs/steroids — impair healing, increase rupture risk)

Late (Dressler's syndrome):

  • 2–10 weeks post-MI (autoimmune)
  • Fever, pleuritic chest pain, pericardial effusion
  • Treatment: NSAIDs, colchicine; rarely steroids
  • Higher risk of ventricular aneurysm formation

6. Left Ventricular Aneurysm

  • True aneurysm (after transmural MI, scarred)
  • Persistent ST elevation after MI ("South African flag sign")
  • Apex most common (LAD territory)
  • Risk of LV thrombus → anticoagulation
  • Treat heart failure, consider surgical repair if symptomatic

7. LV Thrombus

  • 5–20% of anterior MIs
  • High risk in anterior STEMI with apical akinesis
  • Diagnosis: echo (thrombus in apex), contrast echo or cardiac MRI
  • Anticoagulation for 3–6 months (warfarin or DOAC)
  • Repeat echo to confirm resolution

8. Recurrent Ischaemia / Reinfarction

  • Chest pain + new ST changes post-MI
  • Causes: stent thrombosis, recurrent plaque rupture, incomplete revascularisation
  • Urgent angiography ± PCI

9. Right Ventricular Infarction

  • Proximal RCA occlusion
  • Triad: hypotension, clear lung fields, raised JVP
  • Avoid nitrates and diuretics (preload-dependent)
  • IV fluids + inotropes
  • ST elevation in V4R
Pearls
- VF — most common cause of early death; β-blocker reduces risk
- Mechanical complications (free wall, VSR, papillary rupture) peak at 3–7 days
- VSR → LAD/RCA territory; papillary rupture → RCA (posteromedial papillary muscle has single blood supply)
- Free wall rupture = surgical emergency (often fatal)
- Dressler's = autoimmune, weeks later; peri-infarction = early, treat with high-dose aspirin
- Avoid NSAIDs/steroids in early peri-infarction pericarditis (impaired healing, ↑rupture)
- Anterior MI + apical akinesis → anticoagulate for LV thrombus

topics.title: Nstemi Vs Ua Differentiation
topics.html:
Topic: NSTEMI vs UA — Cardiology
Style: Sea Knowledge study infographic
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

1. Definitions

Acute Coronary Syndromes (ACS) encompass:

  • STEMI — ST elevation, transmural ischaemia, complete occlusion
  • NSTEMI — non-ST elevation MI (subendocardial), partial occlusion, troponin elevation
  • Unstable angina (UA) — ischaemia without troponin rise

UA vs NSTEMI = presence or absence of myocardial necrosis (determined by troponin).

2. Pathophysiology Differences

FeatureUANSTEMI
MechanismPlaque rupture + incomplete thrombusPlaque rupture + transient complete thrombus / microembolisation
NecrosisNoneSubendocardial necrosis present
TroponinNormalElevated (above 99th percentile)
ECGST depression, T inversion, or normalST depression, T inversion (similar to UA)
Cardiac enzymesNormalElevated (troponin I/T)
Time courseSymptoms may resolveSymptoms often more prolonged

3. Diagnosis — Troponin (Universal Definition)

  • >99th percentile upper reference limit = MI
  • High-sensitivity troponin (hs-cTn): rule-out algorithm at 0 h and 1 h or 0 h and 3 h
  • Delta change (rise and/or fall pattern) differentiates acute from chronic elevation
  • Sex-specific cutoffs for hs-cTn (women lower)

4. ECG Patterns

  • ST depression ≥0.5 mm in ≥2 contiguous leads (most specific)
  • T wave inversion (deep, symmetric) — especially in V1–V4 (anterior ischaemia) or inferior
  • Transient ST elevation (<20 min) — NSTEMI (not sustained STEMI)
  • Wellens' syndrome — deep symmetric TWI V2–V3 = critical proximal LAD
  • de Winter T — upsloping ST depression V1–V6 + tall T (proximal LAD, treat as STEMI)

5. Biomarker Profile

  • hs-cTn rise within 1–3 h of symptom onset
  • CK-MB rises later (4–9 h, peaks 24 h, normalises 48–72 h) — useful for reinfarction diagnosis
  • Persistent elevation of troponin beyond expected peak → differential (chronic troponin elevation in CKD, myocarditis, Tako-tsubo, PE)

6. Clinical Presentation (Both NSTEMI & UA)

  • Rest angina (>20 min, new onset)
  • Crescendo angina (more frequent/severe)
  • Post-MI angina
  • New-onset severe angina (CCS III/IV)
  • Atypical: dyspnoea, fatigue, epigastric pain (esp. women, elderly, diabetics)

7. Risk Stratification (GRACE Score)

  • In-hospital mortality predictors: age, HR, SBP, Killip class, creatinine, cardiac arrest, ST deviation, troponin
  • GRACE >140 = high risk → early invasive (<24 h)
  • GRACE 109–140 = intermediate → invasive (<72 h)
  • GRACE <109 = low risk → selective invasive

8. TIMI Score (Alternative)

  • Age ≥65, ≥3 risk factors, prior CAD, ST deviation, severe angina, ASA use, ↑cardiac markers
  • TIMI 0–2 = low; 3–4 = intermediate; 5–7 = high risk

9. Why Differentiate?

  • UA: managed medically; invasive strategy not always needed
  • NSTEMI: usually requires invasive (especially high-risk features)
  • Diagnosis may be revised as troponin evolves (initial UA → NSTEMI)

10. Mimics of NSTEMI / UA

  • Aortic dissection
  • Pulmonary embolism
  • Pericarditis / myocarditis
  • Tako-tsubo cardiomyopathy
  • MINOCA
  • Oesophageal spasm / reflux
  • Musculoskeletal chest pain
Pearls
- Troponin is the discriminator between UA (normal) and NSTEMI (elevated)
- hs-cTn — 0/1 h or 0/3 h algorithm; rise/fall pattern = acute injury
- GRACE score guides timing of invasive strategy
- Sex-specific troponin cutoffs for hs-cTn (women lower)
- ST depression ≥0.5 mm in 2 contiguous leads = hallmark of NSTEMI/UA
- de Winter T waves = STEMI-equivalent (urgent angiography)
- Wellens' syndrome = critical proximal LAD; admission + angiography

topics.title: Early Invasive Vs Conservative Strategy
topics.html:
Topic: Early Invasive vs Conservative Strategy in NSTE-ACS — Cardiology
Style: Sea Knowledge study infographic
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

1. Definition

Early invasive strategy = routine coronary angiography ± revascularisation within 24–72 h of admission in NSTE-ACS. Conservative (ischaemia-guided) strategy = medical therapy first, with angiography reserved for recurrent ischaemia or high-risk findings.

2. Strategy Comparison

FeatureEarly InvasiveConservative
Angiography timingWithin 24–72 hOnly if recurrent ischaemia or failed medical therapy
Recurrent ischaemiaLowerHigher
Length of stayShorter (if uncomplicated)Longer (often)
Bleeding riskHigher (procedural)Lower
Mortality benefitYes (high-risk)Less clear
RevascularisationHigher rateLower rate

3. Who Benefits Most (Class I) — Early Invasive

  • GRACE score >140 (high risk)
  • Recurrent/refractory ischaemia at rest
  • Dynamic ST changes
  • Elevated cardiac biomarkers
  • Haemodynamic instability (shock, pulmonary oedema)
  • Life-threatening arrhythmia (VF, sustained VT)
  • Post-MI angina
  • New LBBB
  • High TIMI risk score (≥3)
  • Diabetes mellitus
  • CKD (eGFR 30–60) (cautiously)
  • LVEF <40%

4. Delayed vs Immediate Invasive

TimingIndicationTrials
<2 h (immediate)Refractory ischaemia, shock, mechanical complicationClinical urgency
<24 h (early)GRACE >140, dynamic ST change, high troponinTIMACS, VERHEGGEN
<72 hGRACE 109–140, intermediate riskStandard
>72 h / non-invasiveGRACE <109, low risk, women (controversial)ICTUS, RITA-3

5. Conservative Strategy — When Appropriate

  • GRACE <109 (low risk)
  • No recurrent ischaemia
  • Normal troponin
  • Significant comorbidities where invasive risk outweighs benefit
  • Patient preference
  • No high-risk features

6. Special Populations

  • Elderly (>75): benefit maintained but higher bleeding — careful selection
  • Women: less robust benefit (ICTUS); not worse with invasive if high-risk
  • CKD: benefit may be greater, but contrast risk; weigh carefully
  • Diabetes: clear benefit (FREEDOM, but in stable CAD)
  • Post-CABG: consider graft disease; native vessel PCI often preferred

7. Trial Evidence

  • FRISC-II, TACTICS-TIMI 18, RITA-3, ICTUS — early invasive
  • TIMACS — early (<24 h) best in GRACE >140
  • VERHEGGEN / ABOARD — immediate not superior to early (unless refractory)

8. Practical Pathway

  1. Diagnose NSTE-ACS (ECG + troponin)
  2. Risk-stratify (GRACE / TIMI)
  3. Initiate DAPT + anticoagulation (see other notes)
  4. High-risk → early invasive (<24 h)
  5. Intermediate → invasive within 72 h
  6. Low-risk → stress test or CTCA; invasive if positive
  7. Angiography → PCI, CABG, or medical therapy based on anatomy

9. Complications of Invasive Strategy

  • Access site bleeding (radial < femoral)
  • Contrast nephropathy (esp. CKD, diabetics)
  • Stent thrombosis / restenosis
  • Stroke (rare, ~0.1%)
  • Periprocedural MI
Pearls
- GRACE >140 = early invasive (<24 h); GRACE <109 = conservative
- Recurrent ischaemia, dynamic ST change, shock = urgent (<2 h) angiography
- Routine early invasive reduces death/MI in high-risk NSTE-ACS
- Conservative strategy reserved for low-risk patients
- Radial access preferred (lower bleeding, MATRIX trial)
- CKD/diabetics benefit from invasive; bleeding risk higher

topics.title: Medical Therapy Dual Antiplatelet Anticoagulant Beta Blocker Acei Statin
topics.html:
Topic: Medical Therapy in NSTE-ACS — Cardiology
Style: Sea Knowledge study infographic
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

1. Overview

NSTE-ACS management relies on antiplatelet + anticoagulant therapy for acute thrombotic protection, plus secondary prevention (statin, ACEi, beta-blocker). The choice of agent and duration depends on the chosen strategy (invasive vs conservative) and bleeding/ischaemic risk.

2. Dual Antiplatelet Therapy (DAPT)

P2Y12LoadingMaintenanceNotes
Ticagrelor180 mg90 mg BDPreferred (PLATO); reversible, dyspnoea
Prasugrel60 mg10 mg dailyAvoid if stroke/TIA, age >75, weight <60 kg
Clopidogrel300–600 mg75 mg dailyUse if fibrinolysis given, or other unavailable
  • Aspirin 150–300 mg loading → 75–100 mg daily
  • DAPT duration: ≥12 months after NSTEMI (Class I)
  • PPI (e.g., omeprazole) for gastroprotection

3. Anticoagulation (choose one, switch if PCI planned)

AgentDoseNotes
Fondaparinux2.5 mg SC dailyFirst choice in NSTE-ACS (OASIS-5)
Enoxaparin1 mg/kg SC BDRenally cleared; reduce in CKD
UFH60 U/kg bolus + 12 U/kg/hUse if PCI planned, or CKD
BivalirudinPer protocolPeriprocedural; lower bleeding
  • Fondaparinux + UFH (for PCI) is the standard combination
  • Stop anticoagulant after PCI (unless other indication)

4. Beta-Blocker

  • Start within 24 h if no contraindications
  • Target HR 50–60 bpm
  • Oral (not IV in first 24 h — increased cardiogenic shock risk; COMMIT)
  • Continue indefinitely post-MI
  • Contraindications: HR <60, SBP <100, signs of HF, AV block, asthma (relative)

5. ACE Inhibitor / ARB

  • Start within 24 h in STEMI / NSTE-ACS (especially LVEF <40%, anterior MI, HF)
  • Ramipril, enalapril, perindopril (HOPE, EUROPA)
  • If ACEi-intolerant → ARB (valsartan, losartan)
  • Avoid in: hypotension, hyperkalaemia, bilateral RAS, angioedema history
  • Continue indefinitely

6. Statin

  • High-intensity (atorvastatin 40–80 mg or rosuvastatin 20–40 mg) for all ACS patients
  • Start regardless of LDL level
  • Reduces mortality (PROVE-IT, IMPROVE-IT)
  • Target LDL <1.8 mmol/L (<70 mg/dL) — even <1.4 mmol/L very-high-risk

7. Additional Therapies

DrugIndication
Mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist (MRA)LVEF ≤40% + HF or DM (EPHESUS)
Nitrates (IV)Persistent ischaemia, hypertension, pulmonary oedema
MorphineSevere pain (but slow absorption of clopidogrel — caution)
OxygenOnly if SpO2 <90% (AVOID trial)
Calcium channel blockersIf β-blocker contraindicated (verapamil/diltiazem — avoid if LV dysfunction)

8. Duration of Therapy

  • DAPT: 12 months (then assess ischaemic vs bleeding risk)
  • Anticoagulant: until PCI (or discharge)
  • Statin / ACEi / beta-blocker: indefinite
  • PPI: while on DAPT, especially if bleeding risk

9. Bleeding Risk Assessment (CRUSADE / ARC-HBR)

  • High bleeding risk: consider shorter DAPT, radial access, fondaparinux
  • Score variables: CrCl, HR, SBP, sex, CHF, prior vascular disease, DM
Pearls
- DAPT = aspirin + ticagrelor (or prasugrel) ≥12 months
- Fondaparinux = preferred anticoagulant in NSTE-ACS; add UFH for PCI
- Beta-blocker (oral) within 24 h, avoid IV in first 24 h
- ACEi within 24 h in anterior MI, LV dysfunction, or HF
- High-intensity statin for all (target LDL <1.4–1.8 mmol/L)
- PPI for gastroprotection during DAPT
- Oxygen only if SpO2 <90%

topics.title: Coronary Angiography Timing
topics.html:
Topic: Coronary Angiography Timing in NSTE-ACS — Cardiology
Style: Sea Knowledge study infographic
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

1. Definition

Coronary angiography is the definitive diagnostic test in NSTE-ACS to identify the culprit lesion and plan revascularisation (PCI or CABG). The timing of angiography is risk-driven.

2. Risk-Based Timing (2023 ESC / 2025 ACC)

Risk CategoryTimingExamples
Immediate / very urgent<2 hRefractory ischaemia, cardiogenic shock, mechanical complication, life-threatening arrhythmia
Early<24 hGRACE >140, dynamic ST changes, high troponin, high TIMI, post-PCI/CABG
Standard<72 hGRACE 109–140, diabetes, CKD, LVEF <40%
Selective / conservativeBased on non-invasive testingGRACE <109, low risk, no recurrent ischaemia

3. High-Risk Features Mandating Early Angiography

  • Haemodynamic instability (hypotension, shock, pulmonary oedema)
  • Sustained ventricular arrhythmia (VT/VF)
  • Recurrent/refractory ischaemia at rest
  • Dynamic ST changes (especially with ongoing pain)
  • High GRACE score (>140)
  • High TIMI score (≥3)
  • Significant troponin rise
  • New/worsening mitral regurgitation
  • Diabetes mellitus
  • LVEF <40%
  • Post-CABG (suggesting graft failure)

4. Routine Early vs Delayed Trials

TrialFinding
FRISC-IIEarly invasive
TACTICS-TIMI 18Early invasive reduces death/MI
RITA-3Early invasive better at 5 yr (high-risk)
ICTUSNo benefit in low-risk NSTEMI
TIMACSEarly (<24 h) best if GRACE >140
ABOARDImmediate (within 2 h) not better than early (<24 h)

5. Pre-Angiography Preparation

  • DAPT (aspirin + P2Y12) — given once decision made (ticagrelor 180 mg or clopidogrel 600 mg)
  • Anticoagulation (fondaparinux 2.5 mg SC, enoxaparin, or UFH)
  • Hydration if CKD (avoid contrast nephropathy)
  • NPO if radial (no NPO needed for radial)
  • Consent, dual access (radial preferred)

6. Access Choice

  • Radial — preferred (lower bleeding, mortality in ACS — RIVAL, MATRIX)
  • Femoral — haemodynamic instability, large catheters (IABP, Impella), anatomy
  • Brachial — rare alternative

7. Findings on Angiography

  • Culprit lesion: complex, irregular, thrombus, haziness
  • TIMI flow: 0 (no flow), 1 (penetration), 2 (slow), 3 (normal)
  • TIMI blush: myocardial reperfusion grade
  • Multivessel disease in ~50% of NSTEMI
  • No critical lesion — consider MINOCA, SCAD, vasospasm, thrombus resolved

8. Subsequent Management Based on Anatomy

  • Single-vessel / focal → PCI with DES
  • Multivessel, simple → PCI (single or staged)
  • Multivessel, complex (3VD / LM / DM) → Heart Team discussion (CABG vs PCI)
  • Non-obstructive (<50% stenosis) → medical therapy, consider MINOCA workup

9. Special Considerations

SituationApproach
Cardiogenic shockImmediate PCI; culprit-only (CULPRIT-SHOCK)
CKD (eGFR <30)Hydration, hold metformin, consider no PCI or staged
Anticoagulated (VKA, DOAC)Time procedure; reversal if UFH needed
Lactation / pregnancyLimit radiation, abdominal shield
Recent fibrinolysisWait 2–24 h if stable; urgent if refractory

10. When to Defer Angiography

  • Active bleeding
  • Severe anaemia (transfuse first)
  • Stroke within 3 months
  • Uncontrolled hypertension
  • Severe contrast allergy (premedicate)
  • Patient refusal / palliative
Pearls
- Risk-stratify first (GRACE, TIMI) — determines timing
- Refractory ischaemia, shock, VT/VF → <2 h
- GRACE >140 → <24 h
- GRACE 109–140 → <72 h
- Radial access preferred (lower bleeding, mortality)
- DAPT before PCI; fondaparinux + UFH is standard
- Multivessel disease in 50%; Heart Team for LM/3VD

topics.title: Revascularization Pci Vs Cabg
topics.html:
Topic: Revascularisation PCI vs CABG — Cardiology
Style: Sea Knowledge study infographic
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

1. Definitions

  • PCI (Percutaneous Coronary Intervention): catheter-based, balloon + stent
  • CABG (Coronary Artery Bypass Graft): surgical, LIMA/RIMA + vein grafts

The choice depends on anatomy, comorbidities, risk, and patient preference, decided by the Heart Team.

2. Indications for CABG (Preferred)

PatternReasoning
Left main disease (≥50%)SYNTAX, NOBLE, EXCEL — CABG preferred for >3VD + LM, lower LM only with low SYNTAX
Three-vessel disease (3VD)SYNTAX: CABG > PCI in 3VD, esp. with DM
2VD with proximal LADSurgery superior (anatomic LAD territory)
DM + multivesselFREEDOM: CABG > PCI for mortality, MI, repeat revascularisation
LV dysfunction + multivesselCABG + medical (STICH)
Inability to take DAPTSurgical

3. Indications for PCI (Preferred)

PatternReasoning
Single-vessel diseasePCI first-line
2VD without proximal LADPCI reasonable
Left main + low SYNTAX (≤22)EXCEL — PCI non-inferior for composite at 5 yr
High surgical riskAge, frailty, EF <30%, COPD, CKD
Acute STEMIPrimary PCI
Recurrent ischaemia, shockUrgent PCI
Patient preferenceFaster recovery with PCI

4. SYNTAX Score

Anatomic scoring system to grade lesion complexity (1–3 vessels, bifurcation, length, calcification, etc.). Guides revascularisation:

  • Low (0–22): PCI or CABG
  • Intermediate (23–32): discussion; consider CABG
  • High (≥33): CABG preferred

5. Key Trials

TrialComparisonOutcome
SYNTAX3VD / LM: PCI vs CABGCABG superior for 3VD, MACE
EXCELLM: PCI vs CABGPCI non-inferior (5 yr composite)
NOBLELM: PCI vs CABGCABG superior (5 yr MACE)
FREEDOMDM + 3VD: PCI vs CABGCABG > PCI (mortality, MI)
FAME-2 / FAME-3FFR-guided PCIPCI in LM/3VD
STICHLV dysfunction + CAD: CABG + medicalCABG > medical alone (10-yr)

6. Diabetes

  • Diabetics with multivessel disease → CABG preferred (FREEDOM)
  • DES + aggressive medical therapy still inferior to CABG in DM
  • SGLT2i / GLP-1 RA improve outcomes irrespective of strategy

7. Surgical Risk Assessment

  • STS score, EuroSCORE II — operative mortality
  • High risk: elderly, frail, prior CABG, severe COPD, EF <30%
  • CABG with cardiopulmonary bypass (on-pump) vs OPCAB (off-pump) — no clear mortality difference

8. PCI Practicalities

  • DES (drug-eluting) standard
  • Intravascular imaging (IVUS, OCT) for LM, complex lesions
  • FFR / iFR for borderline lesions
  • Radial access preferred
  • DAPT required ≥6–12 months

9. CABG Practicalities

  • LIMA → LAD (gold standard, >90% patency at 10 yr)
  • Vein grafts (SVG) → other targets (50% occlusion at 10 yr)
  • Cardiopulmonary bypass vs OPCAB
  • Avoid aspirin cessation (continue)

10. Post-Revascularisation Care

  • PCI: DAPT 6–12 months, then aspirin indefinitely
  • CABG: aspirin 75–100 mg indefinitely (continue through surgery)
  • Statin, ACEi, beta-blocker — both strategies
  • Cardiac rehab — both strategies

11. Hybrid Approach

  • LIMA–LAD (CABG) + PCI to non-LAD lesions — for high-risk surgical patients
  • Limited adoption, specialised centres
Pearls
- CABG preferred in: 3VD, LM + high SYNTAX, DM + multivessel, LV dysfunction + 3VD
- PCI preferred in: 1VD, 2VD non-proximal LAD, LM low SYNTAX, STEMI, surgical high-risk
- Heart Team decides (surgeon, interventionalist, non-invasive)
- LIMA → LAD = gold standard in CABG
- SYNTAX score quantifies lesion complexity
- Diabetics with 3VD = CABG (FREEDOM)
- Always DES in PCI

topics.title: Cardiogenic Shock Iabp Impella Va Ecmo
topics.html:
Topic: Cardiogenic Shock — Cardiology
Style: Sea Knowledge study infographic
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

1. Definition

Cardiogenic shock (CS) = inadequate tissue perfusion from primary cardiac pump failure. Hallmark: SBP <90 mmHg (or vasopressors needed), signs of hypoperfusion (cold, clammy, oliguria, altered mental status, ↑lactate), and elevated filling pressures (PCWP >15 mmHg, low cardiac index <2.2 L/min/m²).

2. Aetiology

  • Acute MI (most common — ~80%)
  • Mechanical complications (VSR, papillary rupture, free wall rupture)
  • Acute decompensated heart failure (chronic HF, fulminant myocarditis)
  • Tako-tsubo with apical rupture
  • Pericardial tamponade
  • Arrhythmias (VT storm, bradycardia, high-degree AV block)
  • Post-cardiotomy
  • PE with RV failure

3. SCAI Classification (Stages A → E)

StageDescriptionFindings
A — At riskLarge MI, no shockNormal BP, well perfused
B — BeginningHypotension, no hypoperfusionSBP <90, HR >100
C — ClassicHypotension + hypoperfusionCold, oliguria, lactate >2
D — DeterioratingFailing to respondEscalating vasopressors
E — ExtremisCardiac arrest, CPRRefractory

4. Hemodynamic Profile

  • CI <2.2 L/min/m²
  • PCWP >15 mmHg (LV failure) or elevated RA pressure (RV failure)
  • SVR normal or high (vs septic shock where SVR low)
  • Mixed venous O2 sat <60%

5. Initial Management

  • Oxygen + ventilation (intubate if needed)
  • IV access + arterial line
  • Foley catheter (urine output)
  • Fluid challenge (small bolus 250 mL) if not in pulmonary oedema
  • Norepinephrine first-line (less arrhythmia than dopamine — SOAP II)
  • Dobutamine for inotropy if SBP adequate
  • Revascularisation if ACS (urgent PCI/CABG)

6. Mechanical Circulatory Support (MCS)

DeviceMechanismSupportUse
IABP (Intra-aortic balloon pump)Counterpulsation (augments diastole, ↓afterload)~0.5–1 L/minClass III (no mortality benefit — IABP-SHOCK II); bail-out for bridge
Impella (2.5, CP, 5.0, RP)Continuous axial flow pump (LV→aorta or RA→PA)2.5–5 L/minLV unloading; cardiogenic shock, high-risk PCI, VT storm
VA-ECMO (veno-arterial)Cardiopulmonary bypass; oxygenation + flow4–6 L/minCardiac arrest, biventricular failure, severe CS
TandemHeartLA→femoral artery bypass4–5 L/minCentres with experience

7. IABP Details

  • Inflates in diastole (augments coronary perfusion), deflates in systole (↓afterload)
  • Requires stable rhythm, no severe AR, no aortic dissection
  • IABP-SHOCK II: no mortality benefit in CS; not routinely recommended (Class III)
  • Useful in: mechanical complications, bridge to surgery, high-risk PCI (controversial)

8. Impella Details

  • Impella 2.5 / CP — percutaneous, femoral
  • Impella 5.0 — surgical cut-down, full LV support
  • Impella RP — right ventricular support
  • Unloads LV (reduces wall stress, oxygen demand)
  • Used in: high-risk PCI, cardiogenic shock, VT ablation, bridge to recovery/transplant
  • Complications: limb ischaemia, haemolysis, bleeding

9. VA-ECMO Details

  • Femoral vein → femoral artery (or central)
  • Provides oxygenation + circulation
  • Complications: limb ischaemia, bleeding, LV distension (use Impella to unload), stroke
  • Contraindications: severe aortic regurgitation, irreversible CNS injury, advanced age/frailty
  • E-CPR: ECMO during cardiac arrest (improves outcomes in selected patients)

10. Choosing MCS Device

ScenarioDevice
Isolated LV failureImpella
Biventricular / pulmonary oedema + hypoxiaVA-ECMO + LV venting
RV failure (RCA MI)Impella RP, VA-ECMO
Bridge to recoveryImpella, VA-ECMO
High-risk PCIImpella, IABP
Cardiac arrestVA-ECMO (E-CPR)

11. Culprit-Only PCI in Shock

  • CULPRIT-SHOCK trial: PCI of culprit lesion only at index procedure
  • Staged non-culprit PCI improves survival vs immediate multivessel PCI

12. Escalation Pathway

  1. Vasopressors/inotropes (norepi ± dobutamine)
  2. Refractory → MCS (Impella or VA-ECMO)
  3. Wean as tolerated or bridge to durable VAD / transplant
  4. Palliative care if no recovery and not a candidate
Pearls
- SCAI staging A–E (at risk → extremis)
- Norepinephrine first-line vasopressor
- IABP no longer routine in CS (Class III in ESC)
- Impella / VA-ECMO for refractory CS
- VA-ECMO can cause LV distension → add Impella to vent
- Culprit-only PCI in shock (CULPRIT-SHOCK)
- Lactate, mixed venous O2 sat, urine output = perfusion markers

topics.title: Mechanical Complications Vsr Papillary Muscle Rupture Free Wall Rupture
topics.html:
Topic: Mechanical Complications of MI — Cardiology
Style: Sea Knowledge study infographic
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

1. Overview

Mechanical complications of MI occur in 0.5–5% of patients, typically 3–7 days post-infarct (when macrophage-mediated collagen degradation peaks). They are surgical emergencies with high mortality (50–90%).

2. Free Wall Rupture

  • Incidence: 1–3% of MIs (decreasing with reperfusion)
  • Timing: 3–14 days (often 5–7 days)
  • Site: Anterior (LAD) — LV free wall
  • Risk factors: Advanced age, female, hypertension, late presentation, no reperfusion
  • Clinical: Sudden collapse, PEA, pulselessness; hemopericardium + tamponade
  • Diagnosis: Echo (pericardial effusion with tamponade)
  • Treatment: Emergent pericardiocentesis + surgical repair (mortality >90%)

3. Ventricular Septal Rupture (VSR)

  • Incidence: 0.2–0.5% (decreasing with PCI)
  • Timing: 3–5 days
  • Site: Anteroseptal (LAD) or posteroseptal (RCA/LCx)
  • Clinical: New harsh holosystolic murmur at LLSB, palpable thrill, biventricular failure
  • Diagnosis:
  • Echo with colour Doppler (jet from LV → RV)
  • PA catheter: step-up in O2 sat (RA → RV) >5%
  • Left-to-right shunt Qp/Qs >1.5
  • Management:
  • Stabilise: IABP, diuretics, vasodilators, inotropes
  • Urgent surgical repair (within 24–48 h if haemodynamically unstable)
  • Percutaneous VSD closure in selected cases
  • Mortality: 40–80% (higher with cardiogenic shock)

4. Papillary Muscle Rupture

  • Incidence: 0.5% of MIs
  • Timing: 3–5 days
  • Site: Posteromedial papillary muscle (single blood supply from PDA → RCA in 90%); anterolateral is dual supply (LAD + LCx)
  • Clinical: Acute severe mitral regurgitation, pulmonary oedema, hypotension
  • Auscultation: New apical pan-systolic murmur (may be soft due to rapid equalisation of pressures)
  • Diagnosis: Echo (TEE) — flail leaflet, severe MR
  • Management:
  • Stabilise: IV diuretics, vasodilators (nitroprusside), IABP
  • Emergency surgery: mitral valve repair or replacement
  • Mortality: 30–50%

5. Differences Between VSR and Papillary Rupture

FeatureVSRPapillary Rupture
MurmurHarsh holosystolic at LLSBApical pan-systolic (often soft)
Step-up in O2 satRA → RV (L→R shunt)None
EchoShunt LV → RVSevere MR, flail leaflet
Echocardiographic clueColour jet across septumColour jet in LA
SurgicalPatch closureMV repair/replace

6. Right Ventricular Rupture

  • Rare; usually fatal
  • Occurs in posteroseptal infarction
  • Presents as tamponade + shock

7. LV Pseudoaneurysm

  • Incomplete rupture contained by pericardium/adhesions
  • Neck is narrower than the body (true aneurysm has wider neck)
  • High rupture risk → surgical repair even if asymptomatic
  • Echo and MRI for diagnosis

8. LV True Aneurysm

  • Outpouching of scarred myocardium (LAD territory — apex)
  • Persistent ST elevation post-MI ("South African flag sign")
  • Complications: HF, thrombus, arrhythmia, rarely rupture
  • Treatment: medical (HF, anticoagulation); surgical repair if symptomatic/large

9. Pre-Operative Stabilisation

  • IABP — first-line bridge (reduces afterload, augments perfusion)
  • Inotropes (dobutamine, norepinephrine)
  • Diuretics for pulmonary oedema
  • Avoid vasodilators if hypotensive

10. Outcomes

  • Free wall rupture: 90% mortality (often pre-hospital)
  • VSR: 30–50% surgical mortality
  • Papillary rupture: 20–30% surgical mortality
  • All have better outcomes with early surgical repair
Pearls
- Mechanical complications occur 3–7 days post-MI (macrophage digestion)
- Free wall rupture = tamponade + PEA → pericardiocentesis + surgery
- VSR = harsh holosystolic murmur at LLSB + step-up O2 sat (RA → RV)
- Papillary rupture = posteromedial (RCA) = severe MR + pulmonary oedema
- Posteromedial papillary muscle = single blood supply (PDA) — more vulnerable
- IABP as bridge; urgent surgery is definitive
- Pseudoaneurysm = narrow neck; true aneurysm = wide neck

topics.title: Arrhythmias Af Vt Vf Heart Block
topics.html:
1. Definition

Arrhythmias occur in >75% of MI patients, especially in the first 48 h. Mechanisms: ischemia, reperfusion, autonomic surge, structural change. Can be supraventricular (AF, SVT) or ventricular (VT/VF, bradyarrhythmia/AV block).

2. Key Features

  • VF / sustained VT: most common in first 24–48 h (ischaemia/reperfusion); primary VF (no HF/shock) ≠ long-term ICD indication
  • Accelerated idioventricular rhythm (AIVR): benign, common post-reperfusion
  • AF: 5–20% of MI; worse prognosis; from atrial ischaemia, ↑atrial pressure, pericarditis
  • Sinus bradycardia/AV block:
  • Inferior MI → AV node ischaemia (RCA territory) → 1st/2nd type I/3rd block; usually resolves
  • Anterior MI → septal necrosis → infranisian block (RBBB + LAFB, Mobitz II, 3rd) → permanent pacing often needed
  • Long QT / Torsades: from antiarrhythmics, electrolyte disturbance

3. Management

  • VF/pulseless VT: ACLS — defibrillation, amiodarone, lidocaine
  • Sustained VT (stable): amiodarone, lidocaine; cardioversion if unstable
  • AF: rate control (β-blocker, CCB); anticoagulate per CHA2DS2-VASc; cardioversion if unstable
  • Bradyarrhythmias: atropine, temporary pacing; permanent pacemaker for persistent high-degree block (especially anterior MI)
  • Correct: K⁺ >4.0, Mg²⁺ >2.0
  • ICD: only for secondary prevention or late (>48 h) ventricular arrhythmia + LVEF ≤35%
Pearls
- Primary VF in first 48 h ≠ ICD indication (low recurrence)
- Inferior MI + AV block: usually transient (RCA)
- Anterior MI + AV block: high mortality, needs permanent pacing
- Keep K⁺ >4.0, Mg²⁺ >2.0 in MI

topics.title: Post Mi Pericarditis Acute Dresslers
topics.html:
1. Definition

Pericarditis after MI has two distinct forms: (1) Early / peri-infarction pericarditis (within days) from transmural necrosis-induced inflammation; (2) Dressler's syndrome (weeks-months later) from autoimmune mechanism.

2. Key Features

  • Early peri-infarction pericarditis (1–3 days post-MI):
  • Transmural MI → inflammation of adjacent pericardium
  • Pleuritic chest pain, pericardial rub (~10–20%)
  • ECG: persistent ST↑, may have PR depression
  • Treatment: high-dose aspirin (avoid NSAIDs/steroids — impair healing, ↑ risk of LV rupture/anastomotic leak)
  • Dressler syndrome (2–10 weeks post-MI):
  • Autoimmune; serum sickness-like
  • Fever, pleuritic chest pain, pericardial effusion, ↑ESR, leukocytosis
  • Risk ↓ with reperfusion (rare in PCI era)
  • Treatment: NSAIDs (ibuprofen) ± colchicine; steroids if refractory
  • Differential: recurrent ischaemia, pulmonary embolism, aortic dissection

3. Management

  • Early: aspirin 750–1000 mg q6h, taper over 1–2 weeks; avoid indomethacin/steroids
  • Dressler: NSAIDs + colchicine; rarely steroids
  • Large effusion/tamponade: pericardiocentesis (rare)
  • Anticoagulation: cautious if effusion (risk of haemopericardium)
  • Recurrent Dressler: may need pericardial window
Pearls
- Early post-MI pericarditis → high-dose aspirin (avoid NSAIDs/steroids)
- Dressler = weeks later, autoimmune, treat with NSAIDs
- Persistent ST↑ post-MI may be pericarditis, not re-infarction
- Dressler is rare in the reperfusion era

topics.title: Left Ventricular Aneurysm And Thrombus
topics.html:
1. Definition

LV aneurysm: transmural scar → thin, dyskinetic bulge (true aneurysm) — typically apical, anterior. Develops weeks to months after STEMI. LV thrombus often forms within the aneurysm (stasis).

2. Key Features

  • True aneurysm: wall contains all layers (scar); mouth = neck; rarely ruptures
  • Pseudoaneurysm (false): contained rupture, neck smaller than cavity; high rupture risk; needs urgent surgery
  • Aneurysm complications: HF, mural thrombus → embolisation (stroke, peripheral), arrhythmia (VT), angina (ischaemia in residual viable myocardium)
  • ECG: persistent ST↑ in infarct territory
  • Echo: dyskinetic, thinned segment; thrombus = echogenic mass adjacent to aneurysm
  • CMR: gold standard for tissue characterisation, thrombus detection
  • LV thrombus: risk highest in anterior STEMI with apical akinesis

3. Management

  • Anticoagulation for LV thrombus: warfarin (INR 2–3) for 3–6 months, repeat imaging
  • DOACs are increasingly used (off-label); trials ongoing
  • Aneurysm: medical therapy for HF, ICD if LVEF ≤35%, surgical resection if refractory arrhythmia/HF
  • Pseudoaneurysm: urgent surgery
  • Aspirin + statin; consider ACEi/ARB
  • Anticoagulation generally not needed for aneurysm without thrombus
Pearls
- True aneurysm rarely ruptures; pseudoaneurysm ruptures → urgent surgery
- Anterior STEMI + apical akinesis = high thrombus risk
- Warfarin 3–6 months for LV thrombus, then reimage
- Persistent ST↑ post-MI ≠ re-MI; consider aneurysm

topics.title: Secondary Prevention Dual Antiplatelet Duration Statin Acei Arb Beta Blocker Mra
topics.html:
1. Definition

Secondary prevention after ACS = lifelong strategies to reduce recurrent CV events. Components: antiplatelet therapy, lipid-lowering, RAAS blockade, β-blockade, MRA, SGLT2i, lifestyle, cardiac rehab.

2. Key Features

  • DAPT (aspirin + P2Y12):
  • Aspirin: 75–100 mg lifelong
  • P2Y12: ticagrelor or prasugrel (preferred over clopidogrel) for 12 months in ACS; clopidogrel if high bleeding risk
  • DAPT duration: 12 months standard; shorter (1–6 mo) if high bleeding risk; longer (>12 mo) for high ischaemic/low bleeding risk
  • Statin: high-intensity (atorvastatin 40–80 mg or rosuvastatin 20–40 mg) — target LDL <1.4 mmol/L (<55 mg/dL)
  • ACEi/ARB: all patients with LV dysfunction, HTN, diabetes; consider in all
  • β-blocker: 1st year post-MI; lifelong if LVEF ≤40% or HFrEF
  • MRA: if LVEF ≤40% + HF/diabetes (similar to HFrEF GDMT)
  • SGLT2i/GLP-1 RA: emerging in non-diabetic post-MI (EMPA-TROP, SELECT)
  • Lifestyle: smoking cessation, Mediterranean diet, exercise, weight, alcohol moderation
  • Cardiac rehab: structured exercise programme

3. Management

  • All post-MI patients should be on: aspirin + P2Y12 + high-intensity statin + ACEi + β-blocker
  • Address BP, LDL, HbA1c, smoking, weight
  • Annual flu vaccine; pneumococcal
  • Depression screening post-MI
  • Cardiac rehab improves outcomes
  • Imaging surveillance: echo at 3–6 months
Pearls
- Ticagrelor or prasugrel preferred over clopidogrel in ACS
- LDL target <1.4 mmol/L (or <1.0 in very high risk)
- β-blocker lifelong if LVEF ≤40%
- DAPT duration personalised: ischaemic vs bleeding risk

topics.title: Cardiac Rehabilitation
topics.html:
1. Definition & Indications

Comprehensive secondary prevention program post-MI, post-PCI, post-CABG, stable HF, stable angina. Class I (ESC 2021 CV Prevention).

2. Core Components

  • Exercise training — aerobic + resistance, ≥150 min/wk
  • Risk factor modification — BP, lipids, glucose, weight, smoking
  • Psychosocial support — depression/anxiety screening
  • Medication adherence education
  • Return to work/sexual activity counseling

3. Phases

  1. Phase 1 (inpatient): early mobilization
  2. Phase 2 (early outpatient, 2–12 wk): supervised
  3. Phase 3 (maintenance, lifelong): self-directed

4. Benefits

  • 20–30% all-cause mortality reduction
  • ↓ recurrent MI ~25%
  • ↑ VO₂ max ~20%
  • ↑ QoL, ↓ readmission
Pearls — Refer before discharge post-MI. Women, elderly, lower-SES under-referred. Use Mediterranean diet. Combine with smoking cessation.

topics.title: Post Mi Risk Stratification Lvef Nsvt Inducible Vt
topics.html:
1. Definition & Background

Non sustained VT NSVT: Ventricular Tachycardia (VT) — wide complex tachycardia (>120ms QRS, typically >140ms) of ventricular origin, ≥3 consecutive beats, rate >100 bpm (typically 150-250). Sustained VT ≥30s or requires intervention. Non-sustained (NSVT): 3 beats to <30s. Monomorphic (single morphology) vs polymorphic (variable). ICD-terminated in ICD patients (VT/VF zone).

2. Etiology & Pathophysiology

Etiology & Pathophysiology

Mechanisms: (1) Re-entry (scar from MI, cardiomyopathy, after surgery), (2) Abnormal automaticity (ischaemia, hypoxia, electrolyte), (3) Triggered activity (early/late afterdepolarisations — torsades, digitalis). Substrate: post-MI scar, fibrosis (DCM, HCM, ARVC), infiltrative disease, RV dysplasia.

3. Classification

Clinical Features

Palpitations, dyspnoea, chest pain, syncope, presyncope, fatigue. Haemodynamic instability in 50% (SBP<90, shock, ischaemia, pulmonary oedema). SCD in untreated sustained VT (10-20%/year in post-MI). NSVT: often asymptomatic. Examination: tachycardia, hypotension, signs of HF, cannon A waves (AV dissociation).

4. Clinical Features

Diagnostic Approach

12-lead ECG: wide complex QRS (>120ms), AV dissociation (P waves marching through), capture/fusion beats, concordance (all positive or all negative V1-V6), extreme axis (northwest), Brugada criteria (RS absent in precordials, RS interval >100ms, AV dissociation). Holter for NSVT. EP study for risk stratification and ablation. Echo (LVEF, scar, infiltrative). CMR (scar, fibrofatty infiltration — ARVC).

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

Severity Assessment & Risk Stratification

Haemodynamic instability is the immediate trigger for emergency cardioversion. Underlying substrate severity dictates long-term risk: post-MI scar >30% LV EF, NSVT, syncope → 20% SCD/year. EP study inducibility predicts risk. Brugada ECG pattern = high SCD risk.

Differential Diagnosis

SVT with aberrancy (RBBB, LBBB, pre-existing), SVT with pre-excitation (WPW antidromic), antidromic AVRT, paced rhythm, ventricular paced, hyperkalaemia, severe metabolic disturbance, drug toxicity (sodium channel blockers — TCA, cocaine, flecainide).

Investigations

12-lead ECG (during and after), Holter/event recorder (intermittent), echo (LVEF, scar, infiltrative), CMR (scar, infiltrative, ARVC, myocarditis), coronary angiography (ischaemia), EP study (inducibility, mapping, ablation), TFT, electrolyte, drug levels, genetic testing (LQT, Brugada, CPVT, ARVC).

6. Management

Management Algorithm

Stable VT: IV amiodarone 5mg/kg over 20min, then 15mg/kg/24h; or procainamide (preserved EF, monomorphic), lidocaine (ischaemia/CM). Unstable/VF: DCCV synchronised 100-200J biphasic. Pulseless VT/VF: VF algorithm, shock + CPR. Recurrent/electrical storm: intubate, deep sedation, amiodarone, overdrive pacing, sympathetic blockade (esmolol, stellate ganglion block).

Special Populations

Post-MI: most common in first year, especially with LVEF<35% — ICD primary prevention. Bradycardia-dependent (pause-dependent) torsades: pacing, withdraw offending drug. Catecholaminergic (CPVT): beta-blocker, ICD, sympathetic denervation. Short-coupled Torsades: IV verapamil. Brugada: ICD, quinidine for arrhythmia suppression. ARVC: ICD, exercise restriction, ablation.

7. Complications & Prognosis

Complications & Prognosis

SCD. Cerebrovascular event (hypoperfusion, AF/VF). Heart failure (cardiomyopathy, post-shock). Post-shock complications (skin burns, lead displacement, infection, anxiety). Storms (≥3 VT/VF in 24h — 50% mortality). Cardiac arrest.

topics.title: Icd Indication Post Mi
topics.html:
1. Primary Prevention

  • LVEF ≤35% at ≥40 days post-MI on optimal GDMT (Class IA)
  • NYHA II–III, expected survival >1 year
  • Reassess LVEF at 6–12 wk post-revascularization

2. Secondary Prevention

  • Documented VF or hemodynamically significant VT >48 h post-MI
  • Cardiac arrest survivors with reversible cause treated

3. Bridge

  • WCD (LifeVest) — for 40-day waiting period or pending reassessment

4. Key Trials

  • MADIT-II (2002): LVEF ≤30% → ↓ mortality 31%
  • SCD-HeFT (2005): LVEF ≤35% ischemic CM → ↓ mortality 23%
Pearls — Don't implant <40 d post-MI (EF may recover). Don't in NYHA IV unless CRT-D/LVAD/HTx candidate. Optimize GDMT first.

topics.title: Acs In Elderly
topics.html:
1. Definition

Elderly ACS (≥75 y) accounts for >30% of MI. Atypical presentation, higher comorbidity, more complications, higher mortality. Under-represented in trials — treatment decisions often extrapolated.

2. Key Features

  • Atypical presentation: dyspnoea, fatigue, syncope, confusion, falls — less chest pain (~40%)
  • ECG: more nonspecific changes, baseline abnormalities
  • Biomarkers: troponin may be chronically elevated; serial Δ for diagnosis
  • Higher risk: more multivessel disease, prior MI, HF, CKD
  • Bleeding risk: high; PRECISE-DAPT score
  • Mortality: in-hospital 2–3× higher than younger
  • Type: NSTEMI more common with age; STEMI under-diagnosed

3. Management

  • Same evidence-based therapies — but dose-adjusted
  • Reperfusion in STEMI: PCI preferred; if not available, consider fibrinolysis (age alone not CI)
  • Drug choice: clopidogrel often preferred over ticagrelor (less bleeding); avoid prasugrel if age ≥75 or <60 kg
  • Anticoagulation: UFAR or enoxaparin (adjusted for CrCl); fondaparinux in NSTEMI
  • Statin: high-intensity if tolerated
  • Cautious: β-blocker (start low), ACEi, contrast load
  • Invasive strategy: benefit in NSTEMI, but balance against frailty, comorbidities
Pearls
- Atypical presentation common — dyspnoea, falls, confusion
- Clopidogrel preferred over ticagrelor in many elderly
- Avoid prasugrel if age ≥75 or weight <60 kg
- Treat the same but with dose adjustment and frailty assessment

topics.title: Acs In Women
topics.html:
1. Differences from Men

  • Atypical presentation — dyspnea, fatigue, epigastric/back pain
  • Older age at presentation (~7 years)
  • More comorbidities (HTN, DM, AF, CKD)
  • More MINOCA (~10% vs 5%)
  • SCAD much more common <50y

2. Diagnosis

  • hs-Troponin — same cutoff
  • ECG — same criteria
  • CTA — reasonable first-line in low-intermediate risk
  • MINOCA workup — IVUS, OCT, CMR, spasm testing

3. Management

  • PCI equally effective but underused
  • Same DAPT, statin targets
  • Pregnancy — avoid warfarin (teratogenic), ACEi/ARB, statins

4. Sex-Specific Risks

  • PCOS, preeclampsia, gestational HTN/DM, premature menopause, OCP + smoking
Pearls — Women 2× more likely to die from MI. Don't dismiss atypical symptoms. MINOCA needs workup of all 4 mechanisms (plaque, spasm, thromboembolism, microvascular).

topics.title: Acs In Ckd Dialysis
topics.html:
1. Epidemiology

  • CVD = leading cause of death in dialysis (~40% mortality)
  • CKD 4–5: ~50% have CAD; dialysis: ~80%
  • Accelerated atherosclerosis + vascular calcification

2. Diagnosis Challenges

  • Baseline troponin chronically elevated — interpret delta not absolute
  • ECG: baseline LVH may mask ischemia
  • Contrast-induced AKI risk — minimize contrast, IV hydration

3. Management

  • Primary PCI — preferred; use iso-osmolar contrast, IV saline
  • DAPT same; UFH not LMWH if CrCl <30
  • Statin modest benefit in dialysis (4D, AURORA) — start if eGFR >30
  • CABG > PCI in multivessel + DM (SYNTAX, FREEDOM)
  • ACEi/ARB if tolerated (monitor K+)

4. Avoid

  • Metformin (eGFR <30, lactic acidosis)
  • NSAIDs
  • Gadolinium (NSF)
Pearls — Don't withhold reperfusion for CKD. UFH not LMWH if CrCl <30. Use troponin delta in CKD. Mortality post-MI in dialysis ~50% at 1 year.
