topics.title: Spontaneous Coronary Artery Dissection Scad
topics.html:
1. Definition

SCAD = non-traumatic, non-atherosclerotic separation of coronary artery wall layers with intramural haematoma compressing the true lumen. Mostly affects young/middle-aged women without traditional CV risk factors.

2. Key Features

  • Population: ~90% female, mean age 45–55; few CV risk factors
  • Triggers: emotional/physical stress, intense exercise, Valsalva, pregnancy/post-partum
  • Predisposing conditions: fibromuscular dysplasia (FMD) (~70%), connective tissue disease (Marfan, Ehlers-Danlos, Loeys-Dietz), hormonal therapy, multiparity
  • Angiographic types (Saw classification):
  • Type 1: contrast staining of arterial wall
  • Type 2: diffuse stenosis (most common)
  • Type 3: focal/mimics atherosclerosis
  • Presentation: ACS, including STEMI; sometimes VF arrest
  • Multivessel in ~10–15%; recurrent SCAD in 10–20% at 5 y

3. Management

  • Conservative = first-line if haemodynamically stable (most heal spontaneously in weeks)
  • PCI only for ongoing ischaemia, refractory pain, haemodynamic instability (high complication rate — dissection can extend)
  • CABG rare; for left main or recurrent
  • Antiplatelet: aspirin ± clopidogrel (limited evidence; avoid DAPT unless stented)
  • Statin: controversial; usually avoided unless dyslipidaemia
  • β-blocker: associated with ↓ recurrence
  • Screen for FMD (CT/MR angiography of neck, head, abdomen, pelvis)
  • Pregnancy counselling: recurrence risk
  • Cardiac rehab with caution
Pearls
- SCAD = young woman + ACS + few risk factors → think FMD
- Conservative management first-line (heals spontaneously)
- PCI high-risk in SCAD (dissection can extend)
- β-blockers reduce recurrence

topics.title: Takotsubo Cardiomyopathy
topics.html:
1. Definition

Takotsubo (stress) cardiomyopathy = acute, transient LV systolic dysfunction (apical ballooning or other patterns) mimicking ACS, in absence of obstructive CAD, typically triggered by emotional/physical stress. Predominantly post-menopausal women.

2. Key Features

  • Pathogenesis: catecholamine surge, microvascular spasm, myocardial stunning
  • Anatomic variants (apical is classic): apical (most common), mid-ventricular, basal (reverse), focal, biventricular
  • Diagnosis (Mayo criteria): (1) transient regional wall motion abnormality extending beyond single coronary territory; (2) absence of obstructive CAD/plaque rupture; (3) new ECG abnormalities or modest troponin; (4) absence of pheochromocytoma/myocarditis
  • Triggers: emotional stress (death of loved one, surprise), physical (asthma, surgery, sepsis), "broken heart"
  • ECG: ST↑ (often anterior), TWI, QT prolongation; can mimic STEMI
  • Echo: apical akinesis, hypercontractile base; LVEF ↓
  • CMR: LGE typically absent (helps differentiate from MI/myocarditis)
  • Complications: LV outflow obstruction, MR, cardiogenic shock, VT, mural thrombus, ventricular rupture (rare)

3. Management

  • Supportive — most recover in days–weeks
  • GDMT for HFrEF if reduced EF; β-blocker and ACEi during recovery
  • Anticoagulation if apical thrombus
  • Avoid: inotropes (catecholamines), diuretics; mechanical support (IABP, Impella) if shock
  • β-blockers continued long-term may prevent recurrence
  • Avoid triggers; psychological support
  • Prognosis: good overall; in-hospital mortality 5%; recurrence 5% per year
Pearls
- Apex balloons, base contracts — pathognomonic
- Post-menopausal women most common
- CMR: no LGE (vs MI has LGE)
- Catecholamines can worsen; avoid inotropes if possible

topics.title: Myocardial Infarction With Non Obstructive Coronary Arteries Minoca
topics.html:
1. Definition

MINOCA = MI by Universal Definition (troponin + clinical evidence) with non-obstructive CAD (<50% stenosis in culprit artery) on angiography. Diagnosis of exclusion. Workup needed to identify underlying cause.

2. Key Features

  • Prevalence: 5–10% of all MI; more common in women, young, fewer risk factors
  • Working diagnosis: requires (1) AMI by criteria, (2) non-obstructive CAD, (3) no overt alternative cause
  • Mechanisms:
  • Plaque disruption (ulceration, erosion) — IVUS/OCT detects
  • Thromboembolism — AF, hypercoagulable, PFO
  • Spasm — variant angina, cocaine, triptans
  • SCAD
  • Microvascular dysfunction (INOCA)
  • Myocarditis (CMR differentiates)
  • Takotsubo (CMR differentiates)
  • CMR within days = key diagnostic tool (LGE pattern differentiates MI vs myocarditis vs takotsubo)
  • Outcomes: not benign — similar mortality to obstructive MI

3. Management

  • Address underlying cause:
  • Plaque disruption: DAPT, statin (overt MI)
  • Spasm: CCB, nitrates; avoid β-blockers (can worsen)
  • Thromboembolism: anticoagulation
  • Myocarditis: avoid NSAIDs in early phase; standard HF
  • Standard MI secondary prevention (DAPT, statin) for atherosclerotic cause
  • CMR within 1–2 weeks for diagnosis
  • OCT/IVUS if available during angiography
  • Cardiac rehab appropriate
  • Reassess risk factors (esp. smoking, hypertension)
Pearls
- MINOCA ≠ normal coronaries; needs workup
- CMR is the key diagnostic test (LGE pattern)
- Most common causes: plaque disruption, spasm, myocarditis, takotsubo
- β-blockers can worsen coronary spasm — use CCB instead

topics.title: Aortic Dissection Stanford A B Debakey Management
topics.html:
Topic: Aortic Dissection — Cardiology
Style: Sea Knowledge study infographic
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

1. Definition

Intimal tear → blood enters media → splits into true + false lumen. Propagates antegrade or retrograde. Mortality 1–2% per hour in untreated Type A.

Risk Factors

  • Hypertension (most important, 70%)
  • Connective tissue: Marfan, Loeys-Dietz, Ehlers-Danlos type IV
  • Bicuspid aortic valve (BAV)
  • Atherosclerosis, vasculitis (Takayasu, GCA)
  • Pregnancy (3rd trimester), cocaine, trauma, iatrogenic (catheter, IABP)
  • Cocaine, amphetamines, weightlifting

2. Classification

Stanford (clinical, drives treatment)

TypeInvolvementTreatment
AAscending aorta ± arch ± descendingEmergency surgery
BDescending only (distal to LSCA)Medical ± TEVAR if complicated

DeBakey (anatomical)

  • I — Ascending + arch + descending
  • II — Ascending only
  • III — Descending only

3. Clinical Features

  • Sudden "tearing / ripping" chest or back pain
  • Type A: anterior chest; Type B: interscapular
  • Pulse deficit (30% Type A), BP differential > 20 mmHg
  • Acute AR murmur (40% Type A)
  • Neurological: stroke, syncope, paraplegia
  • Hypotension / tamponade (rupture into pericardium)

4. Management

Type A — Emergency Open Repair

  • Bentall procedure (root + valve + re-implant coronaries)
  • Valve-sparing David procedure if valve OK
  • Immediate surgery — mortality 1–2%/h untreated

Type B — Medical First

  • HR < 60 with IV beta-blocker (esmolol, labetalol)
  • SBP 100–120 mmHg (add nitroprusside after HR controlled)
  • Analgesia (morphine / fentanyl)
  • TEVAR if: malperfusion, rupture, refractory pain / HTN, expansion

5. Key Pearls

Pearls
  • Type A = surgery (1–2% mortality per hour)
  • Type B = medical ± TEVAR
  • HR < 60 with IV BB first, then SBP 100–120
  • Never thrombolyse a Type A (worsens rupture)
  • Long-term: BB + ACEi/ARB + statin, lifelong BP control

Sea Knowledge · Cardiology · Aortic Diseases · v1.0 · For FCPS / MRCP exam prep

topics.title: Aortic Aneurysm Thoracic Abdominal Surveillance Repair Thresholds
topics.html:
Topic: Aortic Aneurysm — Cardiology
Style: Sea Knowledge study infographic
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

1. Definition

Localised dilatation > 1.5× normal aortic diameter. True (all 3 layers) vs false (contained rupture). Risk of rupture, dissection, compression.

Risk Factors

  • Age, male sex, smoking (strongest modifiable)
  • Hypertension, atherosclerosis
  • Connective tissue disease (Marfan, Loeys-Dietz, Ehlers-Danlos)
  • Bicuspid aortic valve (BAV)
  • Family history, vasculitis (Takayasu, GCA, IgG4)

2. Classification by Location

  • TAA (thoracic): ascending > root > arch > descending
  • AAA (abdominal): infrarenal (90%) > juxtarenal
  • Thoracoabdominal (Crawford I–V)

3. Clinical Features

  • Usually asymptomatic — incidental on CXR / US / CT
  • Expanding: deep, boring pain (chest / back / abdomen)
  • Compression: SVC, RLN (hoarseness), bronchi
  • AAA: pulsatile epigastric mass, hypotension if ruptured
  • Rupture: hypotension, abdominal / back pain — mortality 80–90% pre-hospital

4. Repair Thresholds

LocationThresholdRepair
Ascending / root (general)≥ 55 mmOpen (Bentall / valve-sparing)
BAV without risk≥ 55 mmOpen
Marfan≥ 50 mm (45 if FHx / rapid growth)Open
Loeys-Dietz≥ 42 mmOpen (more aggressive)
Descending thoracic≥ 60 mm (55 if risk)TEVAR
AAA (infrarenal)≥ 5.5 cm (men), 5.0 (women)EVAR (if anatomy suitable) / open
Rapid growth> 5 mm/yearConsider earlier repair

5. Surveillance

  • AAA: US every 2–3 y if 3.0–3.9 cm; annually if 4.0–4.9 cm; repair at 5.5 cm
  • TAA: CT / MRI at 6–12 month intervals
  • Screening: USPSTF — one-time US in men 65–75 who ever smoked

6. Key Pearls

Pearls
  • AAA ≥ 5.5 cm (men) → repair; 5.0 cm (women)
  • EVAR preferred for AAA if anatomy suitable (lower peri-op mortality)
  • Marfan → 50 mm; Loeys-Dietz → 42 mm (more aggressive)
  • BAV → 55 mm (independent of valve severity)
  • AAA screening: men 65–75, ever-smokers (one-time US)

Sea Knowledge · Cardiology · Aortic Diseases · v1.0 · For FCPS / MRCP exam prep

topics.title: Aortic Intramural Hematoma Imh
topics.html:
Topic: Aortic Intramural Haematoma — Cardiology
Style: Sea Knowledge study infographic
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

1. Definition

Variant of acute aortic syndrome — haemorrhage within the media without an intimal tear (no false lumen). Caused by rupture of vasa vasorum.

Distinguishing Features from Dissection

  • No intimal flap, no false lumen
  • Crescentic wall thickening on imaging
  • May evolve into dissection (10–30%)

2. Classification (Same as Dissection)

  • Type A (Stanford A): ascending aorta involvement → surgery
  • Type B (Stanford B): descending only → medical management

3. Risk Factors

  • Same as dissection: HTN (most common), age, atherosclerosis
  • Anticoagulation, antiplatelet therapy (controversial)
  • Connective tissue disease

4. Clinical Features

  • Similar to dissection: sudden severe chest / back pain
  • May be clinically indistinguishable
  • D-dimer ↑ (less than dissection)
  • Sometimes detected incidentally on CT

5. Imaging

  • CT / MRI: crescentic aortic wall thickening (>5 mm), no intimal flap
  • TEE: localised echolucent area in wall
  • MRI T1 fat-sat: better for subacute / chronic
  • Serial imaging for evolution (→ dissection, aneurysm, resolution)

6. Management

  • Type A IMH: surgical repair (most centres) — same as Type A dissection
  • Type B IMH: medical therapy (HR < 60, SBP 100–120) ± TEVAR if complicated
  • Some centres observe small Type B IMH with close imaging

Complicated Features (TEVAR)

  • Persistent pain despite therapy
  • Aortic expansion
  • Pleural / pericardial effusion
  • Malperfusion
  • Rupture

7. Key Pearls

Pearls
  • IMH = no intimal flap, crescentic wall thickening (vasa vasorum rupture)
  • Type A → surgery (same as dissection)
  • Type B → medical ± TEVAR if complicated
  • May evolve to dissection (10–30%) — serial imaging essential
  • D-dimer elevated (less than dissection)

Sea Knowledge · Cardiology · Aortic Diseases · v1.0 · For FCPS / MRCP exam prep

topics.title: Penetrating Aortic Ulcer Pau
topics.html:
Topic: Penetrating Aortic Ulcer — Cardiology
Style: Sea Knowledge study infographic
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

1. Definition

Atherosclerotic ulcer that erodes through the intima into the media — focal, usually in the descending thoracic aorta. Forms part of acute aortic syndrome spectrum.

Distinguishing from IMH and Dissection

  • Ulcer crater visible in aortic wall (vs no flap in IMH)
  • Localised, not propagated
  • Often multiple, in heavily calcified aorta

2. Risk Factors

  • Elderly hypertensive (typical patient)
  • Atherosclerosis (heavy aortic plaque burden)
  • Smoking, hyperlipidaemia
  • Less common in connective tissue disease

3. Clinical Features

  • Acute chest / back pain (similar to dissection)
  • Can be asymptomatic (incidental CT finding)
  • May cause haemothorax if ruptures
  • May progress to IMH, dissection, pseudoaneurysm, or rupture

4. Imaging

  • CT angiography: focal contrast-filled outpouching in aortic wall, often with surrounding haematoma
  • MRI: crater with surrounding high signal (oedema / blood)
  • TEE: useful in unstable patients

Follow-up

  • Serial imaging at 1, 3, 6, 12 months then yearly (small, asymptomatic)

5. Management

  • Asymptomatic, small: medical management + surveillance
  • Symptomatic, expanding, or large: TEVAR (endovascular repair)
  • Ruptured: emergent TEVAR or open repair
  • Medical therapy: HR < 60, SBP 100–120, statin, smoking cessation

Indications for TEVAR

  • Persistent pain
  • Rapid growth
  • Pleural effusion / haemothorax
  • Diameter > 20–25 mm, depth > 10 mm
  • Associated IMH with expansion

6. Key Pearls

Pearls
  • PAU = atherosclerotic ulcer crater in aortic wall (usually descending)
  • Elderly hypertensive with atherosclerosis
  • Small / asymptomatic → medical + surveillance
  • Symptomatic / expanding → TEVAR
  • May progress to IMH, dissection, rupture

Sea Knowledge · Cardiology · Aortic Diseases · v1.0 · For FCPS / MRCP exam prep

topics.title: Aortitis Takayasu Gca Infectious Igg4 Related
topics.html:
Topic: Aortitis — Cardiology
Style: Sea Knowledge study infographic
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

1. Definition

Inflammation of the aortic wall — may cause aneurysm, stenosis, dissection, or rupture. Classified by aetiology.

2. Large Vessel Vasculitis

Giant Cell Arteritis (GCA)

  • > 50 y, female > male
  • Headache, jaw claudication, scalp tenderness, vision loss (giant cell involvement of temporal artery)
  • Polymyalgia rheumatica (50%): shoulder / pelvic girdle pain
  • ↑↑ ESR, ↑ CRP, anaemia of chronic disease
  • Treat: high-dose prednisolone (40–60 mg); tocilizumab for relapse
  • Aortic involvement (aneurysm, dissection) — late complication

Takayasu Arteritis

  • Young female < 40 y, Asian descent
  • "Pulseless disease" — absent / weak upper limb pulses, BP discrepancy
  • Systemic: fever, weight loss, malaise
  • Claudication, carotidynia, syncope, renovascular HTN
  • Treatment: high-dose steroids ± methotrexate / tocilizumab / anti-TNF
  • Bypass / angioplasty for critical stenosis

3. Infectious Aortitis

  • Salmonella, Staph aureus, Strep, TB, syphilis
  • Pre-existing aneurysm / atherosclerosis risk factor
  • Mycotic aneurysm formation
  • Treatment: prolonged IV antibiotics + surgery

4. IgG4-Related Aortitis

  • Fibroinflammatory with storiform fibrosis, obliterative phlebitis
  • ↑ serum IgG4 (> 1.35 g/L)
  • Often periaortitis / retroperitoneal fibrosis
  • Steroid-responsive

5. Diagnosis

  • CTA / MRA: wall thickening, aneurysm, stenosis
  • PET-CT: active inflammation
  • ESR / CRP elevated
  • Biopsy (GCA: temporal artery)
  • IgG4 levels, ANA, ANCA, syphilis serology, blood cultures

6. Key Pearls

Pearls
  • GCA: elderly, headache, jaw claudication, ↑ESR, high-dose steroids
  • Takayasu: young Asian female, "pulseless disease", BP discrepancy
  • Infectious: Salmonella on aneurysm; IV antibiotics + surgery
  • IgG4: ↑IgG4, steroid-responsive
  • Image with CTA / MRA, PET-CT for activity

Sea Knowledge · Cardiology · Aortic Diseases · v1.0 · For FCPS / MRCP exam prep

topics.title: Palliative Care In Advanced Hf
topics.html:
1. Indications

NYHA III–IV despite optimal GDMT, recurrent hospitalizations, ICD shocks, cachexia, refractory symptoms, consideration of LVAD/HTx.

2. Symptom Management

  • Dyspnea — opioids (morphine 2.5–5 mg PO q4h PRN), oxygen, diuretics
  • Pain — acetaminophen, opioids; avoid NSAIDs
  • Nausea — haloperidol, ondansetron
  • Anxiety/depression — SSRIs, mirtazapine (also ↑ appetite)
  • Fatigue — energy conservation, treat anemia/sleep apnea
  • Cachexia — nutritional support, omega-3, treat depression

3. Advance Care Planning

  • Document goals of care early (not just at end of life)
  • Discuss ICD deactivation at end of life (shocks are distressing)
  • Code status, mechanical circulatory support preferences
  • Designate surrogate decision-maker

4. End-of-Life

  • Hospice referral when life expectancy <6 months
  • Discontinue drugs that don't provide symptomatic benefit
  • Continue symptom-focused drugs (diuretics, opioids)
  • Compassionate withdrawal of inotropes/ICD as appropriate
Pearls — Palliative care is not end-of-life care only. Start early in advanced HF. Deactivate ICD before death to prevent distressing shocks. Discuss goals of care at every hospitalization.

topics.title: Cardiogenic Shock Classification Scai Stages
topics.html:
1. SCAI Staging (2019)

StageDescriptionHemodynamics
A — At riskLarge MI, acute on chronic HFNormal
B — BeginningHypotension, no hypoperfusionSBP <90, no signs
C — ClassicHypoperfusion requiring interventionCI <2.2, PCWP >15, hypotension
D — DeterioratingNot responding to initial measuresWorsening despite drugs ± IABP
E — ExtremisCardiac arrest, refractory shockCPR, ECMO candidates

2. Etiology

  • Acute MI with LV failure (~80%)
  • Mechanical complications (VSD, papillary rupture, free wall)
  • Acute decompensated chronic HF
  • Myocarditis, takotsubo
  • Post-cardiotomy

3. Initial Management

  • Inotropes — dobutamine, milrinone, norepinephrine
  • Fluid — cautious; avoid in RV infarct if not preload-dependent
  • Mechanical circulatory support — escalate by stage (see MCS topic)

4. Mortality

  • Stage C: 30–40%
  • Stage D: 50–60%
  • Stage E: >80%
Pearls — Norepinephrine preferred over dopamine (less arrhythmia). Escalate to MCS early in D/E. Address mechanical complications (echo!). Multidisciplinary shock team improves outcomes.

topics.title: Temporary Mcs Iabp Impella Va Ecmo
topics.html:
1. IABP (Intra-Aortic Balloon Pump)

  • Counterpulsation — inflates in diastole, deflates in systole
  • ↑ coronary perfusion, ↓ afterload
  • Limited support (~0.5 L/min ↑ CO)
  • Use: cardiogenic shock, bridge to PCI/CABG
  • Contraindications: severe AR, aortic dissection

2. Impella (2.5, CP, 5.0, 5.5)

  • Axial flow pump across aortic valve
  • Unloads LV, ↑ forward flow
  • 2.5 = 2.5 L/min; 5.0/5.5 = up to 6 L/min
  • Use: cardiogenic shock, high-risk PCI, VT ablation
  • Contra: LV thrombus, severe AS, mechanical AV

3. VA-ECMO

  • Veno-arterial extracorporeal circulation
  • Provides full cardiopulmonary support
  • Use: refractory shock, cardiac arrest (ECPR)
  • Complications: bleeding, limb ischemia, LV distension, Harlequin syndrome (differential hypoxia)
  • Weaning — requires adequate LV ejection (consider Impella for LV venting)

4. Selection by SCAI Stage

  • B/C: IABP, inotropes
  • C/D: Impella, VA-ECMO
  • E: VA-ECMO ± Impella, CPR/ECPR
Pearls — No mortality benefit proven for routine IABP in cardiogenic shock (IABP-SHOCK II). VA-ECMO ↑ LV afterload — add Impella for venting. Multidisciplinary shock team essential. Time to MCS is critical.

topics.title: Definition Lvef ≤40% Epidemiology Pathophysiology
topics.html:
Topic: Heart Failure with Reduced Ejection Fraction — Cardiology
Style: Sea Knowledge study note
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

1. Definition

HFrEF = clinical syndrome of HF with LVEF ≤40%. Two pillars of diagnosis: (1) symptoms/signs of HF (dyspnoea, fatigue, oedema) AND (2) objective evidence of cardiac dysfunction (reduced LVEF on echo, ↑BNP/NT-proBNP). ~50% of all HF cases.

2. Epidemiology

  • Prevalence ~1–2% of adults, >10% over 70 y
  • Lifetime risk ~20% in those aged ≥40
  • Commoner in men, African-Caribbean ethnicity
  • Ischaemic aetiology accounts for 60–70% of cases in the West
  • 5-year mortality ~50%; prognosis worse than many cancers

3. Etiology

CategoryExamples
IschaemicPrior MI, chronic ischaemia (most common)
Dilated cardiomyopathy (DCM)Idiopathic, genetic (TTN, LMNA), viral, toxic (alcohol, chemo — anthracyclines, trastuzumab, cyclophosphamide)
ValvularLate MR, AR, AS
Tachycardia-mediatedChronic AF with rapid VR, SVT
InflammatoryMyocarditis, peripartum, autoimmune
InfiltrativeAmyloid, sarcoid, haemochromatosis
MetabolicThyrotoxicosis, thiamine deficiency (wet beriberi)

4. Pathophysiology

Initial insult (ischaemia, toxin, infection) → myocyte loss → neurohormonal activation (RAAS, SNS, ADH, endothelin) → vasoconstriction, Na⁺/H₂O retention → ↑preload and afterload → adverse remodelling (eccentric hypertrophy, dilation, fibrosis, MR annular dilation) → progressive pump failure. Vicious cycle: ↓CO → RAAS/SNS → vasoconstriction, retention → ↑preload, afterload → further ↓CO.

Vicious cycle: Pump failure begets neurohormonal activation, which begets further pump failure.

5. Clinical Clues to Severity

  • NYHA III/IV symptoms, S3 gallop, elevated JVP
  • BNP/NT-proBNP ↑↑; troponin may be detectable
  • Echo: LVEF ≤40%, LV dilation, functional MR, ↑PASP
  • MAGGIC, Seattle Heart Failure Model for prognosis

6. Management Snapshot

  • 4 pillars of GDMT (all class I, mortality benefit): ARNI/ACEi/ARB + Beta-blocker + MRA + SGLT2i
  • Diuretics for congestion (no mortality benefit)
  • ICD if LVEF ≤35%, NYHA II–III, on GDMT ≥3 mo, life expectancy >1 y
  • CRT if LBBB + QRS ≥150 ms + LVEF ≤35% + NYHA II–IV
  • Treat cause: revascularisation, valve repair, abstinence, rate control
Pearls
- HFrEF is not just a low EF — must have symptoms/signs + objective evidence
- 4 GDMT pillars all reduce mortality and hospitalisation
- SGLT2i work across the full EF spectrum
- Ischaemic aetiology is the most common cause — always check coronaries
- ACEi/ARB + MRA → monitor K⁺ and renal function (hyperkalaemia in 5–10%)
CLINICAL SIGNIFICANCE — HFrEF is a chronic, progressive, lethal syndrome. Early initiation and up-titration of the 4 GDMT pillars is the single biggest advance in modern HF care. STRONG-HF (2022) showed rapid up-titration within 2 weeks of discharge reduces readmission and mortality.

topics.title: Gdmt Quadruple Therapy Arni Acei Arb Beta Blocker Mra Sglt2I
topics.html:
Topic: Guideline-Directed Medical Therapy — Cardiology
Style: Sea Knowledge study note
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

1. The 4 Pillars

All class I, evidence A in HFrEF (LVEF ≤40%, NYHA II–IV) — all reduce mortality and hospitalisation. Initiate early and up-titrate to target within 4–6 weeks (STRONG-HF 2022).

PillarDrug & Target DoseLandmark Trial
ARNISacubitril/valsartan 97/103 mg BD (or ACEi/ARB)PARADIGM-HF
Beta-blockerBisoprolol 10 mg · Carvedilol 25 mg BD · Metoprolol succ 200 mgCIBIS-II, COPERNICUS, MERIT-HF
MRASpironolactone 25 mg · Eplerenone 50 mgRALES, EMPHASIS-HF, EPHESUS
SGLT2iDapagliflozin 10 mg · Empagliflozin 10 mgDAPA-HF, EMPEROR-Reduced
All-cause mortality dropped ~60–70% across 4 pivotal trials when all 4 pillars combined.

2. Initiation Order

  • Inpatient / de novo HF — start ARNI + BB + MRA + SGLT2i all at low dose; up-titrate every 2 weeks
  • SGLT2i is the easiest first drug (no titration, no monitoring)
  • ARNI requires 36-h washout from ACEi (risk of angioedema)
  • BB — start once euvolaemic, not in acute decompensation

3. Class-Specific Notes

  • ARNI preferred over ACEi/ARB — replaces ACEi/ARB, do not combine
  • ACEi/ARB if ARNI not tolerated (cough, angioedema, cost). ARB if ACEi cough
  • BB — only bisoprolol, carvedilol, metoprolol succinate (NOT atenolol, NOT propranolol)
  • MRA — monitor K⁺ and creatinine at 1, 4, 8 weeks then 3-monthly; hold if K⁺ >5.5
  • SGLT2i — works in HFrEF and HFpEF, diabetes-independent, modest HbA1c effect

4. Where to Add Other Therapies

  • Diuretics (loop ± thiazide) — for symptom/congestion only (no mortality benefit)
  • Ivabradine — if sinus rhythm, HR ≥70 despite max BB (SHIFT)
  • Vericiguat — recently hospitalised, worsening HF (VICTORIA)
  • Hydralazine + ISDN — if ACEi/ARB/ARNI intolerant, especially African-Caribbean (A-HeFT)
  • Digoxin — symptomatic despite GDMT; reduces hospitalisation, not mortality (DIG)
  • IV iron (ferric carboxymaltose) — if ferritin <100 or TSAT <20% (AFFIRM-AHF, FAIR-HFpEF)
  • Omega-3 PUFA — modest benefit (REDUCE-IT excluded HF, GISSI-HF neutral)

5. Drugs to AVOID in HFrEF

  • Verapamil / diltiazem — negative inotrope
  • Class I antiarrhythmics (flecainide) — pro-arrhythmic in structural HD
  • Thiazolidinediones — fluid retention
  • NSAIDs — Na⁺ retention, antagonism of diuretics and ACEi
  • Saxagliptin, alogliptin — ↑HF hospitalisation

6. Monitoring

  • U&E, K⁺, creatinine at 1, 4, 8 weeks then 3-monthly on ACEi/ARB + MRA
  • BP, HR, weight, symptoms at every visit
  • Echo at 3 months after major change, then yearly
  • BNP/NT-proBNP for prognosis and adherence
  • Recheck iron studies periodically
Pearls
- GDMT = Guideline-Directed Medical Therapy; not a single drug
- ARNI replaces ACEi/ARB — never combine
- Washout 36 h ACEi → ARNI
- Beta-blocker in HFrEF = bisoprolol, carvedilol, or metoprolol succinate ONLY
- SGLT2i are the easiest drug to start — no titration, benefit within days
- Avoid verapamil/diltiazem in HFrEF
CLINICAL SIGNIFICANCE — Quadruple therapy within 4 weeks of HFrEF diagnosis is the standard of care. STRONG-HF (2022) showed early aggressive up-titration cuts 180-day HF readmission/mortality. SGLT2i are class I across the entire LVEF spectrum (HFrEF, HFmrEF, HFpEF).

topics.title: Device Therapy Icd Crt
topics.html:
Topic: ICD & Cardiac Resynchronisation Therapy — Cardiology
Style: Sea Knowledge study note
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

1. ICD (Implantable Cardioverter-Defibrillator)

Primary prevention of sudden cardiac death (SCD) in ischaemic and non-ischaemic cardiomyopathy.

Primary Prevention Criteria (ESC 2021 / ACC/AHA 2022)

  • LVEF ≤35% despite ≥3 months of optimal GDMT
  • NYHA II–III (or NYHA I with ischaemic aetiology)
  • Expected survival >1 year with good functional status
  • ≥40 days post-MI, ≥3 months post-revascularisation
  • Landmark trials: MADIT-II, SCD-HeFT

Secondary Prevention

  • Survivors of VF or sustained VT with haemodynamic compromise
  • Trials: AVID, CIDS, CASH
ICD does NOT improve EF or HF symptoms — it only aborts lethal arrhythmias.

2. CRT (Cardiac Resynchronisation Therapy)

Biventricular pacing to restore ventricular synchrony in patients with dyssynchronous LV contraction (broad LBBB). Improves symptoms, EF, and survival.

Indications (ESC 2021 — Class I)

SettingQRS / MorphologyLVEFNYHA
LBBB≥150 ms≤35%II–IV (ambulatory)
LBBB130–149 ms≤35%II–IV (ambulatory)
Non-LBBB≥150 ms≤35%II–IV (Class IIa)
CRT-D = CRT + ICD (preferred if ICD also indicated).
CRT-P = CRT alone (frail, advanced age, comorbidities).

Landmark Trials

  • COMPANION, CARE-HF, MADIT-CRT, REVERSE — mortality/morbidity benefit
  • Response: ~70% improve (NYHA, EF ↑, LVESV ↓); reverse remodelling
  • Non-response — extensive scar, non-LBBB, sub-optimal lead position, sub-optimal GDMT

3. When to Choose CRT-D vs ICD Alone

  • CRT-D if QRS ≥150 ms, LBBB morphology, AND LVEF ≤35% AND NYHA II–III AND ICD also indicated
  • ICD alone if narrow QRS or no LBBB morphology
  • CRT-P if advanced age, frailty, severe comorbidity (e.g. CKD on dialysis) where ICD benefit is questionable

4. Practical Issues

  • Pneumothorax, lead displacement, infection, haematoma (~1–2%)
  • Battery life 6–10 years; generator change at end of service
  • Driving restrictions (1 month post-implant for primary, 6 months for secondary prevention — DVLA/MVC rules)
  • MRI-conditional devices — most modern devices are
  • Remote monitoring for arrhythmia, lead parameters, battery

5. ECG After Device

  • LBBB pattern after RV pacing (avoid — use CRT if pacing-dependent)
  • Paced rhythm — wide QRS with pacing spike
  • Failure to capture — visible spike but no QRS — check lead, K⁺, threshold
  • Failure to sense — inappropriate shocks / no shock in VT
Pearls
- ICD prevents SCD; CRT improves pump function — different roles
- LBBB ≥150 ms + LVEF ≤35% + NYHA II–IV = strongest CRT indication
- Always optimise GDMT first and re-assess EF at 3 months before implanting ICD
- QRS <120 ms → no benefit from CRT (ECHO-CRT trial)
- Right ventricular pacing (e.g. AV block) can worsen HF — upgrade to CRT if EF drops
- LBBB only morphology — non-LBBB (RBBB, IVCD) responds poorly
CLINICAL SIGNIFICANCE — ICD reduces arrhythmic death but not HF death. CRT reduces both HF hospitalisation and all-cause mortality in LBBB ≥150 ms. Device therapy complements GDMT — it does not replace it. Always optimise medical therapy first and confirm LVEF after 3 months.

topics.title: Advanced Hf Therapies Lvad Transplant
topics.html:
Topic: LVAD & Heart Transplantation — Cardiology
Style: Sea Knowledge study note
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

1. Advanced HF — INTERMACS Profiles

ProfileDescriptionTime to Intervention
1Critical cardiogenic shockHours
2Progressive declineDays
3Stable but inotrope-dependentWeeks
4Resting symptoms, frequent admissionsMonths
5Exertion intolerant, comfortable at restVariable
6Advanced NYHA IIIVariable
7NYHA III, no recent decompensation—

2. LVAD (Left Ventricular Assist Device)

Mechanical pump that offloads the LV and returns blood to the aorta. Continuous-flow devices (HeartMate 3, HeartWare) are now standard.

Strategies

  • BTT — Bridge to Transplant
  • DT — Destination Therapy (inotrope-ineligible or transplant-ineligible)
  • BTC — Bridge to Candidacy / Bridge to Decision

Indications

  • NYHA IV despite optimal GDMT
  • LVEF ≤25%, CI <2.0 L/min/m²
  • On inotropes, or INTERMACS 1–4
  • Suitable candidate: age, no irreversible end-organ failure, psychosocial

Key Outcomes

  • HeartMate 3 (MOMENTUM 3): 2-year survival ~80%, near-elimination of pump thrombosis with fully magnetically levitated design
  • Improves functional capacity, 6MWD, QoL
  • Improves renal and hepatic function in selected patients

Complications

  • Pump thrombosis (reduced with HeartMate 3, ~1%/y)
  • Stroke (ischaemic and haemorrhagic, ~10%/y)
  • Driveline infection (most common, ~15–20%)
  • GI bleeding from arteriovenous malformations (angiodysplasia)
  • Right HF — LVAD can worsen RV failure
  • Aortic insufficiency — develop over time, may need valve repair

3. Heart Transplantation

Gold standard for end-stage refractory HF. ~4,000/year worldwide, ~3,500/year in US.

Indications

  • End-stage HF (NYHA IV, ACC/AHA stage D) despite GDMT, devices, surgery
  • Peak VO₂ <12 mL/kg/min (or <14 if on beta-blocker)
  • Refractory angina, recurrent VT, congenital heart disease not amenable to repair
  • Age typically <70 (relative)

Contraindications

  • Active malignancy (within 5 y, except some skin cancers)
  • Active infection, HIV with uncontrolled viraemia
  • Severe irreversible PVR >5 Wood units (or >2.5 with vasodilator challenge)
  • Severe renal/hepatic disease (consider combined transplant)
  • Substance misuse, severe psychosocial instability
  • BMI >35, severe peripheral/cerebrovascular disease

Immunosuppression

  • Induction — basiliximab, ATG (high-risk)
  • Maintenance triple — calcineurin inhibitor (tacrolimus > cyclosporine), mycophenolate mofetil, prednisolone (often weaned)
  • mTOR inhibitors (sirolimus, everolimus) for CNI-sparing or CAV

Rejection Surveillance

  • Endomyocardial biopsy — ISHLT grade (ACR 0–3R, AMR 0–3)
  • Surveillance schedule — weekly for 1 mo, 2-weekly 2–3 mo, monthly to 6 mo, then 3–6 monthly
  • Acute cellular rejection — high-dose methylpred, ATG
  • AMR — plasmapheresis, IVIG, rituximab, bortezomib

Long-Term Complications

  • Cardiac allograft vasculopathy (CAV) — leading cause of graft loss; surveillance with IVUS/angiography
  • Malignancy — skin cancers, PTLD (EBV-driven lymphoma)
  • Infection — CMV, fungal, opportunistic
  • Chronic kidney disease — CNI toxicity
  • Hypertension, diabetes, dyslipidaemia — from CNIs and steroids
Pearls
- HeartMate 3 is the current LVAD of choice (magnetic levitation, low thrombosis)
- LVAD improves survival in advanced HF but complications remain significant
- Transplant survival — 1 y 90%, 5 y 75%, 10 y 60%
- PVR >5 Wood units is a contraindication (RV will fail)
- CAV is the leading cause of late graft loss
- PTLD (post-transplant lymphoproliferative disorder) is EBV-associated — reduce immunosuppression
- Induction basiliximab or ATG; maintenance tacrolimus + MMF + prednisolone
CLINICAL SIGNIFICANCE — Both LVAD and transplant are reserved for INTERMACS 1–4 patients refractory to GDMT. HeartMate 3 (MOMENTUM 3) and modern immunosuppression have transformed outcomes. Timing of referral is crucial — late referrals have worse outcomes.

topics.title: Definitions Hfmref 41 49% Hfpef ≥50%
topics.html:
Topic: HF with Mid-Range / Preserved EF — Cardiology
Style: Sea Knowledge study note
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

1. LVEF-Based Classification (ESC 2021)

CategoryLVEFNotes
HFrEF≤40%Reduced EF — clear benefit from GDMT
HFmrEF41–49%"Mid-range" — benefits from SGLT2i, diuretics
HFpEF≥50%"Preserved" — SGLT2i + diuretics + comorbidity management
HFimpEFPreviously ≤40% → now >40%"Improved" — recovered EF on GDMT
Key concept: HF is a clinical syndrome — same diagnosis across the EF spectrum. EF-based categories predict response to therapies, not disease biology.

2. HFmrEF (41–49%)

  • ~15–20% of HF population
  • Sits on a spectrum between HFrEF and HFpEF
  • Treatment evidence: SGLT2i (class I, EMPEROR-Preserved benefit preserved at lower EF)
  • Beta-blocker, ACEi/ARB, MRA — modest benefit, no mortality signal in this range
  • Treat the cause — many HFmrEF have recovered EF (HFimpEF) or evolving HFrEF

3. HFpEF (≥50%)

  • ~50% of all HF cases; the dominant form in elderly, women, hypertensive, obese
  • Pathophysiology: diastolic dysfunction ± impaired systolic reserve, abnormal LV relaxation/stiffness, ↑filling pressures, atrial dysfunction, pulmonary HTN, RV dysfunction
  • Comorbidities drive the syndrome — HTN, obesity, DM, AF, CKD, sleep apnoea, anaemia, iron deficiency
  • H2FPEF score (0–9): Heavy (BMI >30), Hypertensive, F (atrial Fib), Pulmonary HTN, Elder (>60), Filling pressures — score ≥5 ⇒ HFpEF likely
  • HFA-PEFF algorithm (ESC) — stepwise: pre-test → echocardiographic + natriuretic → functional testing → final aetiology

4. Diagnostic Red Flags for HFpEF

  • BNP / NT-proBNP often modestly elevated (rule-out value intact)
  • Echo — LVEF ≥50%, LVH (concentric), LA enlargement, E/e' >14, PASP >35, septal e' <7
  • Strain imaging (GLS) — subclinical systolic dysfunction despite preserved EF
  • HFpEF can be difficult to diagnose — exercise testing (CPET, stress echo) often needed
  • Invasive haemodynamics — PCWP >15 mmHg at rest, >25 with exercise

5. Phenotypes of HFpEF

  • Obesity/metabolic (most common in West)
  • Hypertensive / elderly (concentric remodelling, ATTR amyloid common)
  • Pulmonary hypertension predominant
  • Right-heart predominant
  • Atrial functional (AF-driven, especially in elderly)
  • Amyloid / infiltrative (screen with bone scintigraphy + monoclonal screen)
Pearls
- HFpEF = ~50% of HF; obesity, HTN, women, elderly
- SGLT2i are the only disease-modifying drugs for HFpEF (EMPEROR-Preserved, DELIVER)
- H2FPEF score ≥5 = likely HFpEF
- Diagnose with HFA-PEFF stepwise algorithm (ESC)
- GLS on echo reveals subclinical dysfunction
- ATTR amyloid — common cause in elderly with LVH; screen with DPD/PYP scan
CLINICAL SIGNIFICANCE — HFpEF is increasingly common, often missed, and has no cure. SGLT2i (EMPEROR-Preserved, DELIVER) are the only disease-modifying therapy. Treat the phenotype — obesity, HTN, AF, iron deficiency, sleep apnoea. ATTR amyloid is a treatable mimicker — always consider in elderly LVH.

topics.title: Diagnosis Hfa Peff H2Fpef Scores
topics.html:
Topic: Diagnostic Algorithms for HFpEF — Cardiology
Style: Sea Knowledge study note
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

1. Why Diagnose HFpEF?

HFpEF is underdiagnosed because symptoms are non-specific (dyspnoea, fatigue) and LVEF is "normal". Diagnostic scores stratify probability and guide further testing.

2. H2FPEF Score (Mayo Clinic)

Simple, non-invasive — 6 variables, each scoring 1–3, total 0–9.

VariablePoints
H — Heavy (BMI >30 kg/m²)2
H — Hypertensive (≥2 antihypertensives)1
F — Atrial Fibrillation (paroxysmal or persistent)3
P — Pulmonary Hypertension (PASP >35 mmHg)1
E — Elder (age >60 y)1
F — Filling pressure (E/e' >9)1

Interpretation:

  • 0–1 — low probability
  • 2–5 — intermediate (further testing)
  • 6–9 — high probability

3. HFA-PEFF Algorithm (ESC 2021)

Stepwise, 4 domains — Pre-test, Echocardiographic, Functional (stress), Final aetiology.

Step 1 — P (Pre-test assessment)

  • HF symptoms/signs, comorbidities, BNP/NT-proBNP
  • Exclude other causes of symptoms

Step 2 — E (Echocardiographic & natriuretic peptides)

Major criteria (2 points each):

  • Septal e' <7, lateral e' <10, E/e' ≥15, TR velocity >2.8 m/s
  • LA volume index >34 mL/m², LV mass index ≥115 g/m² (men) / 95 g/m² (women), RWT >0.42
  • NT-proBNP ≥220 pg/mL (SR) or ≥660 (AF)
  • LV GLS absolute <16%

Minor criteria (1 point each): moderate abnormalities in same parameters

Score ≥5 → diagnostic; 2–4 → functional testing

Step 3 — F (Functional testing)

  • Diastolic stress echo — exercise E/e' ≥15 or TR velocity >2.8 mHg
  • Invasive haemodynamics — PCWP >15 mmHg at rest, >25 with exercise (gold standard)
  • CPET — peak VO₂ <20 mL/kg/min, ↑VE/VCO₂ slope

Step 4 — F (Final aetiology)

  • CMR — fibrosis, amyloid, sarcoid, iron
  • DPD/PYP scan for ATTR amyloid
  • Genetic testing, biopsy if indicated

4. Key Echo Markers of HFpEF

  • LVEF ≥50% (mandatory)
  • LVH (concentric) — LV mass index ↑
  • LA enlargement — LAVI >34 mL/m²
  • Diastolic dysfunction — E/e' ≥14, septal e' <7
  • PASP >35 mmHg
  • TAPSE <17 — RV dysfunction
  • GLS <16% — subclinical systolic dysfunction
  • Septal flash / apical rocking — dyssynchrony

5. Differentials to Exclude

  • ATTR cardiac amyloidosis (always consider in elderly LVH + low voltage)
  • Cardiac sarcoid (CMR with LGE, FDG-PET)
  • Haemochromatosis (iron studies, CMR T2*)
  • Constrictive pericarditis (respiratory septal shift, Kussmaul, pericardial thickness)
  • Hypertensive heart disease with severe diastolic dysfunction
  • High-output states (anaemia, AV fistula, thyrotoxicosis)
  • Lung disease (COPD, ILD) — pulmonary causes of dyspnoea
Pearls
- H2FPEF — quick bedside rule-out (BMI, HTN, AF, PASP, age, E/e')
- HFA-PEFF — ESC gold standard, stepwise
- Invasive PCWP with exercise is the gold standard for diagnosis
- ATTR amyloid must be excluded in elderly with LVH (PYP/DPD scan)
- GLS is more sensitive than EF for early dysfunction
- NT-proBNP ≥220 (SR) or ≥660 (AF) is the ESC threshold
CLINICAL SIGNIFICANCE — HFpEF is missed in up to 50% of cases. HFA-PEFF and H2FPEF scores guide further workup. Always exclude ATTR amyloid in elderly HFpEF with LVH — it's treatable (tafamidis). Invasive exercise haemodynamics (PCWP >25) remains the gold standard for difficult cases.

topics.title: Management Diuretics Sglt2I Bp Control Treat Comorbidities
topics.html:
Topic: Management of HFpEF — Cardiology
Style: Sea Knowledge study note
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

1. Treatment Principles

HFpEF has no single magic bullet. The four pillars are:

  1. Decongestion (diuretics)
  2. Disease-modifying drug — SGLT2i
  3. Treat comorbidities aggressively
  4. Lifestyle — weight loss, exercise

2. SGLT2 Inhibitors (Class I, ESC 2021)

The only disease-modifying drug for HFpEF.

TrialDrugLVEF criteriaOutcome
EMPEROR-PreservedEmpagliflozin 10 mgLVEF >40%↓ CV death / HF hospitalisation
DELIVERDapagliflozin 10 mgLVEF >40%↓ CV death / HF hospitalisation
Works across the full EF spectrum (HFimpEF, HFmrEF, HFpEF), independent of diabetes status.

3. Diuretics

  • Loop diuretics (furosemide, bumetanide, torsemide) for symptomatic congestion
  • Thiazide (metolazone) added for diuretic resistance
  • MRA — spironolactone has modest benefit in HFpEF (TOPCAT) — symptom improvement, lower hospitalisation, but no clear mortality benefit
  • Avoid over-diuresis — patients are preload-dependent

4. Comorbidity Management

ComorbidityTherapy
HypertensionTarget <130/80; ACEi/ARB, CCB, thiazide
ObesityWeight loss (5–10% improves symptoms), SGLT2i, GLP-1 RA (semaglutide — STEP-HFpEF)
Atrial fibrillationRate or rhythm control, anticoagulation (DOAC)
Type 2 DMSGLT2i first, then GLP-1 RA
CKDSGLT2i (renoprotective), avoid NSAIDs
Sleep apnoeaCPAP (modest benefit, may not change outcomes)
Iron deficiencyIV ferric carboxymaltose (FAIR-HFpEF improved symptoms, 6MWD)
Coronary diseaseRevascularisation if symptomatic ischaemia
Pulmonary hypertensionTreat cause; sildenafil not routinely indicated

5. Drugs with Negative or Neutral Trials

  • ACEi/ARB — neutral in HFpEF (PEP-CHF, CHARM-Preserved, I-PRESERVE)
  • Beta-blockers — neutral (J-DHF, TOPCAT β-blocker subgroup)
  • Sildenafil — neutral (RELAX)
  • Neprilysin + ARB — ARNI trials in HFpEF did not meet primary endpoint
  • Ivabradine — not indicated (no role in HFpEF)

6. Lifestyle & Non-Pharmacological

  • Weight loss — bariatric surgery improves functional capacity
  • Supervised exercise training — improves peak VO₂, QoL
  • Cardiac rehabilitation — modest benefit
  • Salt restriction (<2 g/day)
  • Fluid restriction in decompensated patients
  • Smoking cessation, alcohol moderation

7. Phenotype-Specific Therapy

  • Amyloid (ATTR) — tafamidis (ATTR-ACT: ↓ mortality and hospitalisation)
  • Obesity/metabolic — SGLT2i + GLP-1 RA + weight loss
  • Pulmonary vascular disease — treat PH, avoid inotropes/vasodilators if PVR ↑↑
  • Cardiac sarcoid — steroids, immunosuppression, ICD
Pearls
- SGLT2i are class I for HFpEF (ESC 2021)
- Treat the phenotype — HTN, obesity, AF, iron deficiency, sleep apnoea
- Tafamidis for ATTR cardiac amyloid
- Diuretics for congestion only — not mortality benefit
- ACEi/ARB are NOT indicated purely for HFpEF
- GLP-1 RA (semaglutide) in obese HFpEF (STEP-HFpEF) — improved symptoms
CLINICAL SIGNIFICANCE — HFpEF treatment is phenotype-driven, not EF-driven. SGLT2i are the cornerstone, with comorbidity management. ATTR amyloid is treatable — always consider in elderly HFpEF with LVH. STEP-HFpEF (2023) supports semaglutide for obese HFpEF.

topics.title: Common Comorbidities Af Obesity Ckd Anemia Iron Deficiency
topics.html:
Topic: Comorbidities in Heart Failure — Cardiology
Style: Sea Knowledge study note
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

1. Why Comorbidities Matter

Comorbidities drive symptoms, hospitalisation, and mortality in HF. Treating them is part of GDMT.

2. Atrial Fibrillation (AF)

  • Prevalence: 30–40% of HF patients; more in HFpEF
  • Bidirectional relationship — AF worsens HF, HF worsens AF
  • Rate control — beta-blocker first; digoxin in HFrEF with AF
  • Rhythm control — amiodarone (safe in structural HD), DCCV
  • AV node ablation + CRT for refractory rate
  • Anticoagulation — DOAC preferred over warfarin in HF (no benefit for warfarin, more bleeding — no AFIB-VTE trial)
  • CASTLE-AF — catheter ablation in HFrEF + AF reduces mortality
  • CABANA — neutral overall, but HF subgroup benefit

3. Obesity

  • ~40% of HFpEF are obese; metabolic syndrome is a major driver
  • SGLT2i + GLP-1 RA (semaglutide, liraglutide) — weight loss, improved HF outcomes
  • STEP-HFpEF (2023) — semaglutide in obese HFpEF: improved symptoms, 6MWD, CRP
  • Bariatric surgery improves functional capacity
  • Obesity paradox — overweight HF patients have better short-term prognosis (caveat: cardiorespiratory fitness matters more)

4. Chronic Kidney Disease (CKD)

  • ~50% of HF patients have eGFR <60; cardiorenal syndrome is common
  • SGLT2i — renoprotective + cardioprotective (DAPA-CKD, EMPA-KIDNEY, CREDENCE)
  • Avoid nephrotoxins — NSAIDs, contrast
  • Diuretic resistance — sequential nephron blockade (loop + thiazide), ultrafiltration
  • Worsening creatinine on ACEi/ARB — accept up to 30% rise; reduce/stop if >30%
  • Hyperkalaemia — common on ACEi/ARB + MRA; novel binders (patiromer, sodium zirconium cyclosilicate) allow continuation

5. Anaemia

  • Prevalence: 30–50% in chronic HF
  • Causes: iron deficiency, CKD, chronic inflammation, haemodilution
  • Associated with: worse symptoms, hospitalisation, mortality
  • Workup: FBC, ferritin, TSAT, B12, folate, reticulocytes
  • Treatment: IV iron (FAIR-HF, AFFIRM-AHF, IRONMAN)
  • ESAs not routinely recommended in HF
  • Transfusion if severe/symptomatic

6. Iron Deficiency

  • Prevalence: 40–60% in HF — most common nutritional deficiency
  • Definition (ESC 2021): ferritin <100 μg/L OR ferritin 100–299 + TSAT <20%
  • Functional iron deficiency — ferritin normal/inflamed, TSAT <20%
  • IV iron — ferric carboxymaltose, ferric derisomaltose, iron sucrose
  • FAIR-HF — improved symptoms, functional class, QoL
  • AFFIRM-AHF — reduced HF hospitalisation post-acute HF
  • IRONMAN (2022) — IV ferric derisomaltose: reduced CV death + hospitalisation (COVID-truncated trial)
  • Avoid oral iron — poor absorption in HF (bowel oedema, hepcidin ↑)

7. Other Important Comorbidities

  • Sleep apnoea (CSA, OSA) — ~50% of HF; CSA carries worse prognosis; adaptive servoventilation (SERVE-HF) harmful in predominant CSA; OSA treated with CPAP
  • COPD — 20–30% of HF; caution with beta-blocker (use cardioselective, e.g. bisoprolol)
  • Diabetes — SGLT2i first
  • Depression — 20% of HF; SSRIs safe, screen routinely
  • Frailty, cognitive impairment, falls — common, especially elderly
  • Cachexia — BMI <22 with weight loss; carries very poor prognosis
Pearls
- Iron deficiency is the most common comorbidity in HF — screen and replace with IV iron
- SGLT2i treat HF, CKD, and diabetes simultaneously
- AF in HF — anticoagulate (DOAC); CASTLE-AF favours catheter ablation
- Worsening creatinine on ACEi/ARB — accept 30% rise; don't stop reflexively
- Hyperkalaemia — patiromer / SZC enable continuation of RAAS inhibitor + MRA
- Avoid NSAIDs in HF
CLINICAL SIGNIFICANCE — Comorbidity management is as important as the 4 GDMT pillars. IV iron for iron deficiency is one of the few interventions that improves symptoms AND hospitalisation. SGLT2i address HF, CKD, and DM in one drug.

topics.title: Acute Decompensated Hf Adhf Classification
topics.html:
Topic: ADHF — Cardiology
Style: Sea Knowledge study note
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

1. Definition

ADHF = rapid or gradual onset of HF symptoms/signs requiring urgent therapy. Most common cause of unplanned hospital admission in patients >65 y.

2. Clinical Presentation

  • Dyspnoea at rest (90%), orthopnoea, PND
  • Pulmonary congestion / oedema (crackles, CXR)
  • Peripheral oedema, ascites, hepatomegaly (right-sided)
  • Fatigue, ↓exercise tolerance
  • Cool peripheries, oliguria (low output)
  • Tachycardia, hypotension or hypertension

3. Classification by Clinical Profile

TypeFeatures%
Decompensated HFGradual worsening, mild-moderate~50%
Acute pulmonary oedemaSudden severe dyspnoea, pink frothy sputum~10–15%
Cardiogenic shockHypotension, hypoperfusion, oliguria~5%
Hypertensive HFSevere HTN, preserved EF, pulmonary oedema~10–15%
High-output HFWarm, vasodilated, low SVR (rare)<5%
Right HFPredominant venous congestion~5%

4. Precipitants of ADHF

Mnemonic — "FAILURE"

  • Forgetting medications / non-adherence
  • Arrhythmia (AF with rapid VR, VT)
  • Infarction (ACS) / ischaemia
  • Lifestyle (salt loading, fluid)
  • Uncontrolled hypertension
  • Respiratory infection / sepsis
  • Embolism (PE)
Other: anaemia, thyrotoxicosis, pregnancy, drugs (NSAIDs, steroids, CCB), renal failure.

5. Severity — Killip & Forrester (post-MI)

Killip classification:

  • I — no HF
  • II — bibasal crackles, S3
  • III — frank pulmonary oedema
  • IV — cardiogenic shock

Forrester classification (haemodynamic):

  • I — normal CI, normal PCWP
  • II — ↓CI, normal PCWP
  • III — normal CI, ↑PCWP
  • IV — ↓CI, ↑PCWP

6. Initial Assessment

  • Vitals, posture, JVP, lung bases, peripheral perfusion
  • ECG — ischaemia, arrhythmia
  • CXR — pulmonary oedema, effusion, cardiomegaly
  • Bloods — U&E (renal), FBC (anaemia), troponin, BNP, ABG
  • Point-of-care echo — EF, fluid status, valves
  • Urinary catheter — fluid balance

7. Management — Overview

ProfileTreatment
Warm & Wet (most)IV diuretic ± vasodilator
Cold & DryCaution — fluid challenge, consider inotrope
Cold & WetInotrope + diuretic ± vasopressor
Warm & DryReassess, optimise oral therapy
Pearls
- Decompensated HF = warm & wet → diuretic
- Cardiogenic shock = cold & wet → inotrope + vasopressor + mechanical support
- ADHF admission trigger — non-adherence in 30% of cases
- "Failure to cross the membrane" — diuretic resistance — switch to bumetanide/torsemide, add thiazide
- Pulmonary oedema + HTN — IV nitroglycerin (GTN)
- Kussmaul sign → constrictive pericarditis, not ADHF
CLINICAL SIGNIFICANCE — ADHF is the most common admission diagnosis in elderly. Treat the profile (warm/cold × wet/dry) — it's the fastest way to choose therapy. Always look for the trigger — non-adherence, AF, ACS, sepsis. ESC 2021 recommends early initiation of GDMT (SGLT2i in particular) during admission — STRONG-HF showed benefit of early up-titration.

topics.title: Hemodynamic Profiling Wet Dry Warm Cold
topics.html:
Topic: Hemodynamic Profiles in HF — Cardiology
Style: Sea Knowledge study note
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

1. Why Profile?

Stevenson's bedside 2×2 haemodynamic matrix guides initial therapy in minutes. Avoids blind diuresis in low-output patients.

Wet (congested)Dry (euvolaemic)
Warm (perfused)Warm & Wet — most common (~70%)Warm & Dry — compensated
Cold (hypoperfused)Cold & Wet — worst prognosisCold & Dry — uncommon

2. Bedside Assessment

Perfusion ("Warm or Cold")

WarmCold
Warm peripheriesCool / mottled peripheries
Normal pulse pressureNarrow pulse pressure (<25%)
Normal mentationConfused, drowsy
Normal urine outputOliguria
No ACEi/BB hypotensionSymptomatic hypotension

Congestion ("Wet or Dry")

WetDry
↑JVP, orthopnoea, bendopnoeaNo orthopnoea, flat JVP
Peripheral / sacral oedemaNo oedema
Hepatomegaly, ascitesNo congestion
Crackles, pleural effusionClear lung bases
S3 gallopNo S3

3. Profile-Based Therapy

Warm & Wet (most common)

  • IV loop diuretic (furosemide 20–40 mg IV stat, repeat or infusion)
  • Vasodilator if hypertensive (GTN)
  • Continue oral GDMT (BB, ACEi/ARB if BP allows)

Cold & Wet (cardiogenic shock spectrum)

  • Inotrope — dobutamine (β1), milrinone (PDE3), levosimendan (calcium sensitiser)
  • Vasopressor — noradrenaline (if SBP <80)
  • Diuretic — to decongest (use with inotrope)
  • Mechanical support — IABP, Impella, VA-ECMO if refractory
  • Right heart catheter to titrate

Cold & Dry

  • Cautious fluid challenge (250 mL NS over 15 min)
  • If no response → inotrope
  • Often early decompensation of advanced HFrEF

Warm & Dry

  • Optimise oral therapy
  • Discharge planning

4. Invasive Confirmation (Swan-Ganz)

ProfileCI (L/min/m²)PCWP (mmHg)
Normal2.5–4.06–12
Warm & Dry≥2.5≤15
Warm & Wet≥2.5>18
Cold & Dry<2.5≤15
Cold & Wet<2.5>18
Cardiac power output = (MAP × CO)/451 — most powerful predictor of mortality in cardiogenic shock. CPO <0.6 W ⇒ very high mortality.

5. Monitoring

  • Strict fluid balance, daily weight
  • U&E, K⁺, creatinine on diuretics
  • BP, HR, SpO₂, urine output
  • BNP/NT-proBNP for response
  • Reassess profile at least twice daily
Pearls
- Warm & Wet = diuretic ± vasodilator
- Cold & Wet = inotrope + diuretic ± MCS
- Cold & Dry = fluid challenge then inotrope
- Narrow pulse pressure = cold (low stroke volume)
- Bendopnoea (dyspnoea bending forward) — earliest sign of congestion
- S3 gallop is the most specific sign of elevated LV filling pressure
CLINICAL SIGNIFICANCE — Stevenson's 2×2 matrix is the bedside shortcut to ADHF therapy. Most admissions are Warm & Wet → IV diuretics. Cold & Wet requires inotropes and consideration of mechanical support. RHC may be needed in complex cases (ESC IIa).

topics.title: Iv Diuretics Vasodilators Inotropes Vasopressors
topics.html:
Topic: Acute HF Drug Therapy — Cardiology
Style: Sea Knowledge study note
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

1. IV Loop Diuretics

First-line for congestion (warm & wet profile).

DrugDoseNotes
Furosemide20–40 mg IV bolus, repeat or infusion 10–20 mg/hMost common
Bumetanide1–2 mg IVPredictable absorption, gut oedema-resistant
Torsemide10–20 mg IVOnce-daily, oral bioavailability >80% (TRANSFORM-HF neutral)
  • DOSE trial — high-dose vs low-dose: high-dose improved symptom relief with transient renal impairment (no long-term harm)
  • Bolus vs continuous infusion — equivalent; continuous may be smoother
  • Diuretic resistance — add thiazide (metolazone, chlorothiazide), acetazolamide (ADVOR), or switch to combination
ADVOR (2022) — acetazolamide IV in ADHF accelerates decongestion (no mortality benefit).

2. Vasodilators

For hypertensive ADHF (high PCWP, SBP >100) — venodilators ↓preload; mixed venodilator/arterial dilators ↓preload + afterload.

DrugDoseNotes
Nitroglycerin (GTN)10–200 μg/min IVFirst-line for hypertensive pulmonary oedema, titratable
Isosorbide dinitrate (ISDN)1–10 mg/h IVAlternative to GTN
Nitroprusside0.3–5 μg/kg/minArterial + venous; for afterload reduction; cyanide toxicity risk
Nesiritide (BNP)0.01 μg/kg/minNot widely used; modest symptom benefit, no mortality
Avoid in SBP <100 mmHg — vasodilators worsen hypotension.

3. Inotropes

Indicated in Cold & Wet / cardiogenic shock (low CO, congestion). Short-term bridge only — increased arrhythmia and mortality with prolonged use.

DrugClassDoseNotes
Dobutamineβ1-agonist2–20 μg/kg/min↑CO, ↓PCWP; tachyphylaxis, ↑HR
MilrinonePDE-3 inhibitor0.375–0.75 μg/kg/minInotrope + vasodilator (inodilator); avoid in renal failure (accumulation)
LevosimendanCa²⁺ sensitiser, K-ATP opener0.05–0.2 μg/kg/minInodilator; no ↑in O₂ demand; not available in many countries
DopamineDose-dependent2–20 μg/kg/minLow dose (renal) obsolete; mid (β1), high (α); more arrhythmias
Digoxin IVCardiac glycoside0.25–0.5 mg loadingSlow onset (hours), narrow therapeutic index

4. Vasopressors

Indicated for hypotension (SBP <80–90) with end-organ hypoperfusion.

DrugReceptorDoseNotes
Noradrenalineα1 >> β10.05–0.5 μg/kg/minFirst-line shock (cardiogenic, septic)
Adrenalineα + β0.05–0.5 μg/kg/minRefractory shock, anaphylaxis
VasopressinV10.03–0.04 U/minSeptic shock add-on
Dopamineα/β5–20 μg/kg/minMore arrhythmias than noradrenaline (SOAP II)
SOAP II — noradrenaline superior to dopamine in cardiogenic shock (less arrhythmia).

5. Vasoactive Strategy

  • Cold & Wet (SBP >90) → dobutamine or milrinone
  • Cold & Wet (SBP <80) → noradrenaline ± dobutamine
  • Refractory → mechanical circulatory support (IABP, Impella, VA-ECMO)

6. Adjuncts

  • O₂ only if SpO₂ <90% (avoid hyperoxia)
  • NIV (CPAP / BiPAP) for pulmonary oedema with respiratory distress (↓intubation, mortality)
  • Morphine — controversial; ↑mortality (ADHERE registry) — use sparingly
  • Thromboprophylaxis — LMWH (enoxaparin 40 mg sc daily) — all hospitalised HF
Pearls
- IV furosemide — first-line for congestion
- GTN — first-line for hypertensive pulmonary oedema
- Dobutamine / milrinone — Cold & Wet
- Noradrenaline — Cold & Wet with SBP <80
- Inotropes ↑arrhythmia + mortality with prolonged use — bridge, not destination
- DOSE trial — high-dose IV diuretic OK; transient renal dysfunction acceptable
- Acetazolamide (ADVOR 2022) — adjunct to loop for faster decongestion
CLINICAL SIGNIFICANCE — Tailor IV therapy to profile + BP: Warm & Wet → furosemide ± GTN; Cold & Wet → inotrope + diuretic + consider MCS. Inotropes are a bridge to recovery, transplant, or LVAD — not destination therapy. ESC 2021 recommends early SGLT2i during admission.

topics.title: Mechanical Support Iabp Impella Va Ecmo
topics.html:
Topic: IABP, Impella, VA-ECMO — Cardiology
Style: Sea Knowledge study note
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

1. Indications

  • Cardiogenic shock refractory to inotropes (SCAI C–E)
  • High-risk PCI (protected PCI)
  • Post-cardiotomy shock
  • Bridge to transplant, LVAD, recovery
  • Refractory ventricular arrhythmia

2. IABP (Intra-Aortic Balloon Pump)

Most common, lowest level of support. Counterpulsation — balloon inflates in diastole (↑coronary perfusion), deflates in systole (↓afterload).

AccessFemoral (8–9 Fr sheath)
Augmentation~0.5–1.0 L/min
LV afterload↓
Coronary flow↑
Best forCardiogenic shock, mechanical complications (VSR, acute MR)
ContraindicationsSevere AR, aortic dissection, severe PVD
IABP-SHOCK II — no mortality benefit in cardiogenic shock post-AMI. ESC downgraded routine use to IIb; still useful in mechanical complications.

3. Impella (Microaxial catheter pump)

Continuous axial flow pump across the aortic valve — unloads LV, pumps blood from LV → aorta.

DeviceFlow
Impella 2.52.5 L/min (high-risk PCI)
Impella CP3.5 L/min (cardiogenic shock)
Impella 5.0/5.55.0–6.0 L/min (more support)
  • Access: femoral or axillary (5.0/5.5)
  • LV fully unloaded — ↓LVEDP, ↓PCWP, ↓myocardial O₂ demand
  • Contraindications: LV thrombus, severe AS, mechanical AV, severe AR
  • Complications: bleeding, limb ischaemia, haemolysis, device migration
  • DanGer Shock (2024) — Impella CP in STEMI-related cardiogenic shock reduced 180-day mortality vs standard care (45% vs 58%)

4. VA-ECMO (Veno-Arterial Extracromial Membrane Oxygenation)

Highest support — both circulatory and oxygenation support. Venous drainage + arterial return + membrane oxygenator.

ConfigurationFem-fem (peripheral) or central
Flow3–7 L/min
ProvidesFull cardiopulmonary support
IndicationsRefractory cardiogenic shock, cardiac arrest (ECPR), bridge to recovery/transplant/LVAD
ComplicationsLimb ischaemia, bleeding, stroke, haemolysis, LV distension, "Harlequin" syndrome (differential hypoxia)
  • LV distension — must be vented (IABP, Impella, atrial septostomy)
  • Weaning — when LVEF recovers, ↓flow, assess with echo
  • Survival ~30–40% to discharge in cardiogenic shock

5. Other Devices

  • TandemHeart — LA → aorta (surgical, transseptal); used in refractory shock
  • CentriMag — extracorporeal, surgical, durable — bridge to recovery/transplant
  • Right ventricular support: Impella RP, ProtekDuo, VA-ECMO with RV venting

6. Choosing the Device

SeverityDevice
Borderline hypotension, awaiting recoveryIABP
Cardiogenic shock, adequate oxygenationImpella
Cardiac arrest, severe shock, hypoxiaVA-ECMO
Biventricular failureVA-ECMO + Impella RP / IABP

7. Complications (all MCS)

  • Bleeding (anticoagulation, access site)
  • Limb ischaemia (large-bore femoral access)
  • Infection, haemolysis
  • Device thrombosis / failure
  • Stroke (thromboembolism, air, haemorrhage)
  • Vascular injury requiring surgery
Pearls
- IABP — counterpulsation; modest support; great in mechanical complications
- Impella — LV-to-aorta microaxial; better LV unloading than IABP
- VA-ECMO — full bypass + oxygenation; ECMO is the most powerful but most complication-prone
- DanGer Shock (2024) — Impella CP benefit in STEMI shock
- ECMO + Impella (ECPella) — combines LV unloading with ECMO support
- Always exclude LV thrombus before Impella
CLINICAL SIGNIFICANCE — MCS is a bridge, not a destination. SCAI staging guides timing — early escalation (A → B → C) improves outcomes. Impella unloads the LV (DanGer Shock 2024), VA-ECMO provides full support but increases LV afterload (often paired with Impella). MCS decisions belong to the Heart Team.

topics.title: Ischemic Cardiomyopathy
topics.html:
Topic: Ischemic Cardiomyopathy — Cardiology
Style: Sea Knowledge study note
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

1. Definition

LV systolic dysfunction (LVEF ≤40%) caused by coronary artery disease — prior MI, chronic ischaemia, or hibernating/stunned myocardium. Most common cause of HFrEF in the Western world (60–70%).

2. Pathophysiology

  • Myocyte loss post-MI → fibrotic scar → adverse remodelling
  • Hibernating myocardium — chronic hypoperfusion → viable but dysfunctional (recovers with revascularisation)
  • Stunned myocardium — post-ischaemic dysfunction that recovers over time
  • Infarct expansion → ventricular dilation → functional MR → progressive HF

3. Diagnosis

  • History — prior MI, PCI/CABG, angina, risk factors
  • ECG — Q waves, LBBB, ischaemic changes, AF
  • Echo — regional wall motion abnormalities (RWMA), LVEF ≤40%, LV dilation, functional MR, viability assessment
  • Coronary angiography — gold standard; defines revascularisation strategy
  • CMR — gold standard for viability:
  • LGE (scar) — transmural scar >50% thickness → low recovery
  • Dobutamine stress CMR — contractile reserve predicts recovery
  • PET — viability with FDG/glucose uptake

4. Viability Assessment

  • CMR LGE — subendocardial scar (recoverable) vs transmural (>50% = poor recovery)
  • Dobutamine stress echo/CMR — contractile reserve = viability
  • PET — FDG uptake = viability (Hibernating Myocardium)
STICH trial — CABG + medical therapy vs medical alone in LV dysfunction. CABG improved long-term survival in patients with LVEF ≤35% + CAD amenable to surgery. Viability testing did not predict which patients benefit (STICH viability substudy).

5. Management

A. Medical (GDMT for HFrEF)

  • 4 pillars — ARNI/ACEi/ARB + BB + MRA + SGLT2i
  • Anti-ischaemic — beta-blocker (first-line), nitrates, CCB (amlodipine/felodipine only)
  • Antiplatelet — aspirin ± second antiplatelet (post-PCI/ACS)
  • Statin — high-intensity (atorvastatin 40–80 mg / rosuvastatin 20–40 mg)
  • Sacubitril/valsartan preferred over ACEi/ARB

B. Revascularisation

  • PCI — for 1–2 vessel disease, suitable anatomy, post-ACS
  • CABG — preferred for left main / 3-vessel disease with reduced EF (STICH, FREEDOM follow-up)
  • Heart Team decision (Syntax score, EuroSCORE, EF)
  • Revascularisation improves symptoms, EF (modestly), and survival in selected patients

C. Devices

  • ICD — primary prevention (LVEF ≤35%, NYHA II–III, GDMT ≥3 mo, ≥40 d post-MI)
  • CRT — if LBBB ≥150 ms
  • LVAD, transplant — for advanced disease

6. Specific Scenarios

  • Acute ischaemia → cardiogenic shock — immediate PCI (CULPRIT-SHOCK: culprit-only PCI)
  • Mechanical complications (VSR, papillary rupture) — emergency surgery + MCS
  • Ventricular arrhythmia — ICD, ablation, anti-arrhythmics (amiodarone)

7. Differentials

  • Non-ischaemic DCM — no regional pattern, younger, different RWMA pattern
  • Takotsubo — apical ballooning, normal coronaries, emotional trigger, recovers
  • Myocarditis — acute presentation, ↑troponin, oedema on CMR
Pearls
- Ischaemic CM is the most common cause of HFrEF in the West
- STICH trial — CABG in LVEF ≤35% + CAD improves survival
- CMR with LGE — gold standard for scar/viability
- Always revascularise if viable myocardium + suitable anatomy
- Heart Team for revascularisation decisions
- PCI for culprit lesion in shock (CULPRIT-SHOCK)
- Avoid verapamil/diltiazem (negative inotrope)
CLINICAL SIGNIFICANCE — Ischaemic cardiomyopathy carries the worst prognosis of all HF aetiologies. Revascularisation + GDMT + devices is the trifecta. STICH showed long-term benefit of CABG in LV dysfunction. CMR viability guides revascularisation, though STICHES showed benefit even without proven viability in selected patients.

topics.title: Non Ischemic Dilated Cardiomyopathy
topics.html:
Topic: Dilated Cardiomyopathy — Cardiology
Style: Sea Knowledge study note
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

1. Definition

LV dilation + systolic dysfunction (LVEF ≤40%) in the absence of CAD or abnormal loading conditions sufficient to cause the dysfunction. By definition, DCM is not due to ischaemia, valve disease, or hypertension.

2. Etiology

CategoryCauses
GeneticTTN truncations (25%), LMNA, PLN, RBM20, FLNC, DES, DMD
InflammatoryViral (parvovirus B19, HHV6, Coxsackie), autoimmune, lymphocytic, giant cell, eosinophilic
ToxicAlcohol (most common reversible), anthracyclines (doxorubicin, epirubicin), trastuzumab, cyclophosphamide, cocaine, methamphetamines
MetabolicThiamine deficiency, hypocalcaemia, hypophosphataemia, thyrotoxicosis, phaeochromocytoma
Tachycardia-mediatedChronic AF/SVT with rapid VR, RV pacing
PeripartumLate pregnancy / 5 mo post-partum
InfiltrativeAmyloid, sarcoid, haemochromatosis
EndocrineCushing, acromegaly
~30–40% of "idiopathic" DCM are genetic (TTN truncations most common).

3. Pathophysiology

Insult (genetic, viral, toxic) → myocyte dysfunction/loss → neurohormonal activation (RAAS, SNS) → adverse remodelling → dilation, ↓contractility → progressive HF.

4. Clinical Features

  • Symptoms of HFrEF — dyspnoea, fatigue, oedema
  • Embolic events — LV thrombus → stroke (↑ risk in DCM)
  • Arrhythmia — AF, VT/VF, bradyarrhythmia
  • Family history of SCD or HF
  • Examination: displaced apex, S3, MR, JVP, oedema

5. Diagnosis

  • ECG — LBBB, LVH, QRS widening, AF
  • Echo — LV dilation (EDD >58 mm men, >52 mm women), LVEF ≤40%, normal wall thickness, no regional pattern
  • CMR — gold standard for tissue: LGE (mid-wall, non-ischaemic pattern — distinguishes from ischaemic), myocarditis (Lake Louise criteria), amyloid, sarcoid
  • Coronary angio or CTCA — to exclude CAD
  • Endomyocardial biopsy — giant cell, eosinophilic, lymphocytic
  • Genetic testing — TTN, LMNA (high-risk SCD)
  • Viral serology / PCR — if active myocarditis suspected
  • Toxin screen — alcohol, drugs, chemotherapy
  • Family screening — 1st-degree relatives, ECG + echo

6. Management

  • 4 pillars of GDMT (ARNI, BB, MRA, SGLT2i) — up-titrate to target
  • Treat the cause:
  • Alcoholic — abstinence → recovery in 6 mo (LVEF improves ~10–15%)
  • Tachycardia-mediated — rate control / ablation → recovery
  • Peripartum — bromocriptine + GDMT
  • Thyrotoxicosis, thiamine — replace, recover
  • Anthracycline — stop if possible, dexrazoxane cardioprotection
  • ICD if LVEF ≤35%, NYHA II–III, GDMT ≥3 mo
  • CRT if LBBB + QRS ≥150 ms
  • LVAD / transplant for advanced disease
  • Avoid — alcohol, cocaine, anthracyclines

7. Family Screening

  • 1st-degree relatives — ECG + echo at presentation, then every 1–5 y
  • Genetic testing if pathogenic variant identified in proband
  • LMNA and FLNC — high-risk SCD regardless of EF (consider ICD at LVEF <45%)
Pearls
- TTN truncations are the most common genetic cause (~25%)
- Alcoholic CM is reversible with abstinence (6-month rule)
- CMR distinguishes ischaemic (subendocardial LGE) from non-ischaemic (mid-wall LGE)
- LMNA/FLNC = high SCD risk even at moderate EF — early ICD
- Peripartum CM = late pregnancy to 5 mo post-partum
- LVEF recovery possible in 30–40% of DCM (HFimpEF) — continue GDMT
- Bromocriptine in peripartum CM
CLINICAL SIGNIFICANCE — Non-ischaemic DCM is a heterogeneous group. CMR is the key — distinguishes ischaemic from non-ischaemic, identifies infiltrative and inflammatory causes. Treat the cause (alcohol abstinence, rate control) and the disease (GDMT + devices). LMNA and FLNC mutations warrant early ICD because of high SCD risk independent of EF.

topics.title: Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy Hcm
topics.html:
Topic: HCM — Cardiology
Style: Sea Knowledge study note
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

1. Definition

LV wall thickness ≥15 mm (or ≥13 mm with family history) unexplained by abnormal loading. Most common genetic cardiovascular disease (1:500). Sarcomeric disease in ~60% (autosomal dominant, MYH7, MYBPC3).

2. Pathophysiology

  • Sarcomere mutations → ↑Ca²⁺ sensitivity, energy depletion → myocyte disarray, hypertrophy, fibrosis
  • Dynamic LVOT obstruction (~70%): septal hypertrophy + SAM (systolic anterior motion of MV) → MR + gradient
  • Diastolic dysfunction (stiff, hypertrophied LV)
  • Microvascular ischaemia (capillary rarefaction)
  • Arrhythmia — AF 20–30%, NSVT, VF

3. Subtypes

  • Asymmetric septal (most common)
  • Apical (Yamaguchi, Asians) — giant TWI V3–V4, "spade-like" LV
  • Mid-ventricular (apical aneurysm, mural thrombus, SCD risk)
  • Concentric
  • Massive LVH ≥30 mm — high SCD risk
  • End-stage / "burned-out" — LVEF <50%, dilation (5–10%)

4. Clinical Features

  • Asymptomatic in most
  • Dyspnoea (diastolic dysfunction, MR, LVOT)
  • Chest pain (microvascular ischaemia)
  • Syncope (LVOT obstruction or arrhythmia) — exertional
  • Palpitations (AF, VT)
  • SCD (often first presentation, young athletes)
  • Examination: jerky pulse (spike-and-dome), double apex beat, harsh systolic murmur LLSB ↑Valsalva, S4

5. Diagnosis

  • ECG — LVH, deep T inversion, septal Q waves; giant TWI V3–V4 in apical HCM
  • Echo — wall thickness ≥15 mm, SAM, LVOT gradient (rest, Valsalva, exercise), MR, EF
  • CMR — gold standard (apical, RV, atypical cases, fibrosis/LGE, apical aneurysm)
  • Holter (24–48 h) — NSVT
  • Exercise test — BP response (flat/fall in <40 y = SCD risk), gradient
  • Family screening — 1st-degree relatives, ECG + echo every 1–5 y, age 12–18
  • Genetic testing — sarcomere panel

6. SCD Risk Stratification (ESC 2022 HCM Risk-SCD)

Variables: age, max wall thickness, LA diameter, LVOT gradient, NSVT, unexplained syncope, family SCD

  • <4% — low (no ICD)
  • 4–6% — intermediate
  • ≥6% over 5 y — high → ICD
Apical aneurysm: ~5%/y SCD. EF <50% (end-stage): ~1–2%/y. Massive LVH ≥30 mm: high risk.

7. Management

Asymptomatic / Low Risk

  • Monitor (echo 1–2 y), family screening, education
  • Avoid competitive sports (ESC 2020)

Symptomatic Obstructive (HOCM)

  1. Beta-blocker — first-line (bisoprolol, nadolol, propranolol, metoprolol)
  2. Non-dihydro CCB — verapamil, diltiazem (caution if severe gradient)
  3. Disopyramide — add-on (class IA, safe in structural HD unlike flecainide; monitor QT)
  4. Septal reduction therapy for refractory symptoms with LVOT gradient ≥50 mmHg at rest or with Valsalva:
  • Surgical myectomy (gold standard, gold-standard mortality)
  • Alcohol septal ablation (ASA) — alternative in elderly/comorbid

Symptomatic Non-Obstructive

  • Beta-blocker, verapamil, cautious diuretics

AF

  • Rate or rhythm (amiodarone)
  • Anticoagulate regardless of CHA₂DS₂-VASc

End-stage (EF <50%)

  • Standard GDMT, transplant evaluation, ICD for SCD

8. Avoid

  • Inotropes, afterload reduction (worsens gradient), diuretics (cautious), digoxin (worsens obstruction), ACEi/ARB (no benefit, may worsen)
Pearls
- HCM = wall ≥15 mm unexplained, sarcomere disease
- HOCM: gradient ≥30 mmHg, SAM, MR, harsh systolic murmur ↑Valsalva
- Apical HCM (Yamaguchi) — giant TWI V3–V4, spade-like LV (use CMR)
- HCM Risk-SCD ≥6% over 5 y → ICD
- Avoid inotropes, afterload reduction, diuretics in HOCM
- Disopyramide is class IA but safe in structural HD (unlike flecainide)
- ATTR amyloid mimics HCM — screen elderly with bone scintigraphy
CLINICAL SIGNIFICANCE — HCM is the most common cause of SCD in young athletes. Always screen family members. ESC 2022 HCM Risk-SCD calculator guides ICD. Septal reduction therapy (surgery or ASA) is reserved for refractory symptoms with severe gradient. Differentiate from ATTR amyloid — treatable mimicker in elderly.

topics.title: Restrictive Infiltrative Cardiomyopathies Amyloid Sarcoid Iron
topics.html:
1. Definition

Non-dilated ventricles with impaired diastolic filling (restrictive physiology), preserved systolic function (initially).

2. Causes

  • Amyloidosis — AL (light chain, plasma cell dyscrasia) or ATTR (transthyretin, wild-type or mutant)
  • Sarcoidosis — non-caseating granulomas
  • Hemochromatosis — iron overload (HFE C282Y)
  • Fabry disease — α-galactosidase A deficiency (X-linked)
  • Endomyocardial fibrosis — Löffler, tropical
  • Radiation, scleroderma

3. Diagnosis

  • Echo: ↑ wall thickness, speckled/gritty myocardium, biatrial enlargement, restrictive filling
  • CMR with LGE — key for tissue characterization
  • Bone scintigraphy (DPD/PYP scan) — ATTR (Grade 2–3 = highly specific)
  • Serum/urine immunofixation + FLC — to exclude AL amyloid
  • Genetic testing (TTR, Fabry)

4. Management

  • Treat underlying cause (chemo for AL, tafamidis for ATTR, iron reduction for hemochromatosis, agalsidase for Fabry)
  • Diuretics for congestion
  • Avoid digoxin, CCB, vasodilators in amyloidosis (hypersensitivity)
  • Standard HF drugs often poorly tolerated
Pearls — Suspect amyloid in HFpEF with ↑ wall thickness + low voltage ECG + carpal tunnel. AL must be excluded before ATTR diagnosis (cardiac biopsy or bone scan + negative monoclonal screen). Tafamidis 80/61 mg for ATTR-CM.

topics.title: Arrhythmogenic Right Ventricular Cardiomyopathy Arvc
topics.html:
Topic: ARVC — Cardiology
Style: Sea Knowledge study note
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

1. Definition

Inherited cardiomyopathy characterised by progressive fibrofatty replacement of the RV myocardium (and sometimes LV). Causes VT, VF, and SCD in young adults and athletes. Autosomal dominant — desmosomal gene mutations (PKP2 most common, also DSP, DSG2, DSC2, JUP).

Prevalence 1:5000. Most common cause of SCD in young athletes after HCM.

2. Pathophysiology

Desmosomal dysfunction → myocyte detachment → inflammation → fibrofatty replacement → RV dilation, aneurysm, arrhythmia.

3. Diagnosis (Modified Task Force Criteria 2010)

Six categories: Definite / Borderline / Possible.

  1. Global/regional dysfunction (echo, CMR, angiography)
  2. Tissue characterisation — fibrofatty replacement on biopsy / CMR LGE
  3. Repolarisation abnormalities — TWI V1–V3 (or beyond, in absence of RBBB)
  4. Depolarisation abnormalities — epsilon waves, late potentials on SAECG
  5. Arrhythmias — VT of LBBB morphology (RV origin), VF
  6. Family history — SCD <35 y, ARVC in 1st-degree relative, pathogenic mutation
Definite = 2 major, or 1 major + 2 minor, or 4 minor.

4. Clinical Features

  • Palpitations, syncope, SCD (often first presentation in young)
  • VT with LBBB morphology + superior axis (RVOT)
  • Exercise-induced — common trigger
  • Heart failure (late)
  • RV failure signs (JVP, hepatomegaly, oedema)

5. Investigations

  • ECG — TWI V1–V3 (minor V1, V2 in >14 y, no RBBB), epsilon waves (small deflection at end of QRS in V1–V3), QRS duration V1+V2+V3 >180 ms
  • Signal-averaged ECG (SAECG) — late potentials
  • Echo — RV dilation, RV wall motion abnormalities, RVOT dilation
  • CMR — gold standard: RV dilation, regional akinesia/dyskinesia, aneurysm, fibrofatty LGE
  • Holter / EP study — VT morphology
  • Genetic testing — desmosomal panel (PKP2, DSP, DSG2, DSC2, JUP)
  • Family screening — 1st-degree relatives (ECG, echo, CMR, Holter)

6. Management

Lifestyle

  • Avoid competitive sports / strenuous exercise (Class I, ESC 2020) — exercise accelerates disease

Arrhythmia

  • Beta-blocker — first-line
  • Antiarrhythmics — sotalol, amiodarone
  • Catheter ablation — for recurrent VT (high recurrence rate)
  • ICD — for secondary prevention (survived VF, sustained VT); primary prevention based on risk (syncope, NSVT, severe RV dysfunction, extensive LGE, family SCD)

Heart Failure

  • Standard GDMT (caution: limited trial data)
  • RV failure — diuretics, anticoagulation if severe
  • Heart transplant for end-stage biventricular failure

Family

  • Genetic counselling
  • Serial screening of 1st-degree relatives

7. Differentials

  • Biventricular ARVC (left-dominant, "ALVC" — DSP, FLNC)
  • Idiopathic RVOT VT — normal RV, no fibrosis
  • Sarcoidosis (RV involvement, systemic)
  • Congenital (Ebstein anomaly — displaced TV)
  • Dilated cardiomyopathy with biventricular involvement
Pearls
- ARVC = fibrofatty replacement of RV, desmosomal disease
- TWI V1–V3 in a young person with VT → think ARVC
- Epsilon waves — small deflections after QRS in V1–V3 (major criterion)
- LBBB-morphology VT with superior axis = RVOT
- Avoid competitive sports (accelerates phenotype)
- PKP2 is the most common mutation
- ICD for secondary prevention; primary prevention based on risk
- Catheter ablation — high recurrence rate, often palliative
CLINICAL SIGNIFICANCE — ARVC is a major cause of SCD in young athletes. The Modified Task Force Criteria integrate imaging, ECG, arrhythmia, family history, and genetics. Avoid exercise — it accelerates disease. ICD for secondary prevention is mandatory; primary prevention is individualised.

topics.title: Takotsubo Cardiomyopathy
topics.html:
Topic: Stress Cardiomyopathy — Cardiology
Style: Sea Knowledge study note
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

1. Definition

Transient LV systolic dysfunction with characteristic apical ballooning (or other patterns) in the absence of obstructive CAD, triggered by emotional or physical stress. Also called stress cardiomyopathy or broken-heart syndrome. Predominantly postmenopausal women (~90%).

2. Mayo Clinic Diagnostic Criteria

  1. Transient hypokinesis, akinesis, or dyskinesis of LV mid-segments ± apex
  2. Absence of obstructive CAD or plaque rupture
  3. New ECG abnormalities (ST elevation / TWI) or modest troponin elevation
  4. Absence of pheochromocytoma or myocarditis
InterTAK score — predicts probability of Takotsubo vs ACS (female sex, emotional trigger, psychiatric/neurological disorder, QTc prolongation, etc.).

3. Pathophysiology

  • Catecholamine surge → direct myocyte toxicity, microvascular spasm, epicardial spasm, Ca²⁺ overload
  • Oestrogen deficiency (postmenopausal) → loss of cardioprotection
  • Brain-heart axis — emotional stress → amygdala activation → sympathetic discharge

4. Patterns (AHA Scientific Statement 2018)

TypeApexBase
Apical (apical ballooning) — most commonAkinesisHyperkinesis
Mid-ventricularNormalNormal
Basal (reverse)HyperkinesisAkinesis
Focal / biventricularVariableVariable

5. Triggers

  • Emotional — grief, anger, surprise, fear
  • Physical — surgery, severe illness, ICU, SAH, stroke, sepsis, cocaine, adrenaline
  • No identifiable trigger in ~30% (primary)

6. Clinical Features

  • Chest pain (mimics ACS)
  • Dyspnoea
  • Syncope, arrhythmia, cardiogenic shock (rare but serious)
  • ECG: ST elevation (anterior), TWI, QTc prolongation
  • Troponin mildly elevated (disproportionate to wall motion abnormality extent)

7. Diagnosis

  • ECG — ST elevation (anterior) or diffuse TWI, QTc ↑
  • Troponin — modest rise (mismatch with extensive WMA)
  • NT-proBNP — markedly elevated (mismatch)
  • Echo — characteristic wall motion pattern, EF reduced
  • Coronary angio / CTCA — normal (mandatory)
  • CMR — gold standard: oedema (T2), no LGE, characteristic "apical ballooning"; excludes MI and myocarditis
  • Left ventriculogram — "octopus pot" (takotsubo = Japanese octopus trap)

8. Complications

  • LV outflow tract obstruction (~25% — basal hyperkinesis + SAM)
  • Cardiogenic shock
  • LV thrombus → systemic embolism
  • Ventricular arrhythmia (torsades, VF)
  • Apical aneurysm (rare)
  • Mortality ~5% in-hospital (similar to ACS)

9. Management

  • Supportive — most recover within days to weeks
  • Heart failure — diuretics, standard GDMT (controversial — beta-blocker may help)
  • LVOTO — beta-blocker (↓hyperkinesis), IV fluids, avoid inotropes, vasodilators
  • Cardiogenic shock — small fluid bolus, avoid inotropes (worsen gradient) — consider mechanical support (IABP, Impella)
  • Anticoagulation if LV thrombus or apical aneurysm
  • Avoid — inotropes, vasodilators if LVOTO
  • Recovery — EF normalises within 1–4 weeks in 90%
  • Recurrence rate ~1–2%/year
Pearls
- Takotsubo = "octopus pot" — apical ballooning, hyperkinetic base
- 90% postmenopausal women, often with emotional trigger
- Troponin-BNP mismatch — modest troponin, high BNP
- CMR is the gold standard (no LGE, distinguishes from MI and myocarditis)
- LVOTO in 25% — beta-blocker helps, inotropes harm
- Avoid inotropes, vasodilators if LVOTO present
- Cardiogenic shock — small fluid challenge, mechanical support
- Anticoagulation if LV thrombus
