  • In aortic root disease / Marfan: beta-blockers reduce aortic stress (rate, dP/dt) — recommended
  • In acute AR + dissection: beta-blocker first (reduce shear) before vasodilator

6. Statins

  • Earlier hope that statins slow AR progression (similar rationale to AS)
  • No consistent benefit in chronic AR (small trials)
  • Reasonable if concomitant atherosclerosis, hyperlipidaemia, CAD

7. Anticoagulation

  • Not routine in AR
  • Add if: AF, mechanical prosthetic valve, previous thromboembolism
  • Aortic dissection → immediate anticoagulation controversy (medical vs surgical)

8. Endocarditis Prophylaxis

  • Not required in native chronic AR (NICE 2008 guidelines)
  • Required if: prosthetic valve, prior IE, cyanotic unrepaired CHD, recent prosthetic material

9. Acute Severe AR — Medical Bridge to Surgery

DrugUse
IV nitroprusside↓ Afterload, ↓ regurgitant volume
IV diuretics (furosemide)↓ Pulmonary congestion
IV inotropes (dobutamine)Maintain forward output
Avoid IABPIncreases AR
Beta-blocker (if dissection)Reduce shear
Goal: stabilise → emergency surgery (valve ± root)

10. Marfan / Connective Tissue

  • Beta-blocker (atenolol, propranolol, bisoprolol) — slows aortic dilation
  • ARB (losartan) — shown in mouse models to ↓ TGF-β → slows root dilation
  • Combination therapy (BB + ARB) — limited paediatric trial data
  • Avoid strenuous exercise, contact sports, isometric loading

11. Follow-up

ScenarioFrequency
Asymptomatic mild-moderate AR, normal LVTTE q 2–3 yr
Asymptomatic severe AR, normal LVTTE q 6–12 mo
Severe AR + LV dilation approaching thresholdTTE q 4–6 mo
Post-AVRTTE at 30 d, 6 mo, then yearly
Pearls
- Vasodilators (ACEi / ARB / CCB) for chronic AR + HTN or pre-surgery
- No medical therapy avoids the need for surgery once criteria met
- Beta-blocker in Marfan / aortic root disease reduces shear stress
- Avoid IABP in severe AR — worsens regurgitation
- In acute AR with dissection: beta-blocker first, then nitroprusside

topics.title: Surgical Indications Lvef Lv Dimensions Symptoms
topics.html:
1. Principle

Surgery is indicated when the LV can no longer compensate — clinically (symptoms) or subclinically (declining EF, progressive dilation). Aortic valve replacement ± root repair is the definitive treatment.

2. Indications for Surgery (ESC 2021 / ACC/AHA 2022)

Class I (Strong)

  • Symptomatic severe AR (any LVEF) — operate regardless of EF
  • Asymptomatic severe AR with LVEF ≤ 50% — operate (subclinical dysfunction)
  • Patients undergoing other cardiac surgery (CABG, ascending aortic surgery) with severe AR

Class IIa (Reasonable)

  • Asymptomatic severe AR with LVEF 50–55% (declining)
  • Asymptomatic severe AR with progressive LV dilation:
  • LVESD > 50 mm (or >25 mm/m² indexed)
  • LVEDD > 65 mm (or >33 mm/m² indexed)
  • Asymptomatic severe AR with progressive EF decline on serial echo (≥3 consecutive measurements)

Class IIb / Consider

  • Asymptomatic severe AR with progressive increase in AR severity
  • New onset symptoms on stress testing in previously asymptomatic
  • Rapid LV dilation (>5 mm/yr in LVESD)

3. Decision Algorithm

SEVERE AR
   ├── Symptomatic ────────────────► OPERATE (any EF)
   ├── Asymptomatic, EF <50% ──────► OPERATE
   ├── Asymptomatic, EF 50–55% ────► Consider (serial echo)
   ├── Asymptomatic, EF >55%
   │     ├── LVESD >50 mm ────────► OPERATE
   │     ├── LVEDD >70 mm ────────► OPERATE
   │     ├── Progression rate ↑ ──► Consider
   │     └── Stable, normal LV ───► Follow (TTE q 6–12 mo)
   └── Concomitant root ≥55 mm ───► OPERATE

4. Choosing the Procedure

ProcedureIndication
Isolated SAVRNative valve disease, normal root
AVR + Root replacement (Bentall)Root >45–55 mm (aetiology dependent)
Valve-sparing root replacement (David / Yacoub)Young patient, normal cusps (e.g., Marfan)
Ross procedureChildren, young adults (autograft)
TAVIHigh surgical risk, suitable anatomy — AR less ideal (no annular calcium)
⚠️ TAVI in pure AR — challenging because device anchors to calcium. Newer devices (JenaValve, JValve) specifically designed for AR. Most still require SAVR.

5. Special Populations

Bicuspid Aortic Valve

  • BAV with severe AR — same LV criteria apply
  • Aortopathy — operate on root if >55 mm (or 50 with risk factors)
  • TAVI feasible for BAV in selected anatomy (Type 1, no excessive raphe calcium)

Marfan / Connective Tissue

  • AR + root ≥45 mm → root replacement (Bentall or valve-sparing)
  • Family history of dissection or rapid growth → operate at 40–45 mm
  • Multidisciplinary team: cardiology, cardiac surgery, genetics

Infective Endocarditis with AR

  • Severe acute AR with pulmonary oedema / shock → emergency surgery
  • Perivalvular abscess, fistula → surgery
  • Continue antibiotics; surgery timing based on haemodynamics, not antibiotic duration

Aortic Dissection

  • Stanford A with severe AR → emergent surgery with valve resuspension or replacement
  • Mortality high; rapid operative intervention saves lives

6. Pre-Operative Workup

  • TTE + TOE for valve and LV detail
  • CT angio of aorta (root, ascending, arch)
  • Coronary angiography (>40 yr or risk factors — concomitant CABG)
  • PFTs for high-risk patients
  • Frailty assessment (grip, gait, ADL)
  • STS / EuroSCORE II for risk counselling

7. Operative Mortality

  • Isolated AVR in low-risk patients: 1–3%
  • AVR + CABG: 3–5%
  • Bentall in elective: 2–4%
  • Emergency surgery (acute AR, dissection, IE): 8–20%

8. Long-Term Post-Operative Follow-up

  • TTE at discharge, 6–12 months, then yearly
  • Bioprosthetic SVD at 8–15 years — reintervention planning
  • Mechanical — lifelong INR monitoring
  • Lifelong endocarditis prophylaxis (dental procedures)

9. When NOT to Operate

  • Patient refusal
  • End-stage comorbidity (cancer, frailty, advanced dementia) — palliative
  • EF <25% with severe chronic AR — very high risk; careful consideration
Pearls
- Symptoms = operate (any LVEF)
- EF < 50% = operate (subclinical LV dysfunction)
- LVESD > 50 mm = operate (pre-dysfunction dilation)
- Root ≥ 45–55 mm = consider (aetiology dependent)
- TAVI rarely first choice for pure AR — device anchoring depends on annular calcium

topics.title: Rheumatic Vs Non Rheumatic
topics.html:
1. Background

Mitral stenosis (MS) is dominated by rheumatic heart disease (RHD) globally. Non-rheumatic causes are uncommon but recognised; they have different pathologies, treatment, and prognosis.

2. Rheumatic MS — The Global Standard

Epidemiology

  • Most common cause of MS worldwide (~95% of cases)
  • Affects ~40 million people (RHD overall)
  • Highest burden: sub-Saharan Africa, South Asia, Pacific Islands, indigenous populations
  • Female predominance (2:1)

Pathogenesis

  • Autoimmune sequela of Group A Streptococcal pharyngitis (M-protein cross-reactivity)
  • Recurrent episodes → chronic inflammation
  • Commissural fusion (pathognomonic), leaflet thickening, chordal shortening, calcification

Pathology

  • "Fish-mouth" or "buttonhole" mitral orifice
  • Leaflet thickening, particularly at free edges
  • Chordal fusion and shortening
  • Subvalvular involvement
  • Left atrial dilation, hypertrophy, calcification of wall (MacCallum patch)
  • Often co-exists with aortic valve involvement (RHD multi-valvular)

Clinical Implications

  • Almost always with history of ARF (recurrent)
  • May co-exist with MR, AS, AR (rheumatic multi-valvular)
  • Lone MS is rheumatic until proven otherwise
  • Secondary prophylaxis (benzathine penicillin G)

3. Non-Rheumatic MS

Calcific Mitral Annular Calcification (MAC)

  • Degenerative calcification of mitral annulus
  • Predominantly elderly, especially women
  • Calcium extends into leaflets → functional narrowing
  • Often associated with aortic stenosis, hypertension, diabetes, CKD
  • Echo shows bright, echogenic annulus
  • Often mild → moderate; rarely severe

Congenital MS

  • Parachute mitral valve (all chords insert on single papillary muscle)
  • Supravalvular mitral ring
  • Double-orifice mitral valve
  • Shone complex (multiple left-sided obstructions)
  • Presents in infancy / childhood

Inflammatory / Autoimmune

  • SLE (Libman-Sacks endocarditis → leaflet thickening)
  • Rheumatoid arthritis (rare)
  • Ankylosing spondylitis
  • IgG4-related disease
  • Carcinoid (typically right-sided, but pulmonary clearance defect can affect left)

Infiltrative

  • Amyloidosis (leaflet thickening)
  • Mucopolysaccharidoses

Iatrogenic

  • Post-mitral valve repair (surgical stenosis)
  • Post-TEER (iatrogenic MS, especially with baseline annular calcification)
  • Radiation (mantle field)

Others

  • Tumour (atrial myxoma obstructing mitral inflow)
  • Ball-valve thrombus (in LA, mimics MS)
  • Cor triatriatum (congenital LA membrane)

4. Comparative Table

FeatureRheumatic MSNon-Rheumatic MS
AgeYoungerOlder (calcific) / Childhood (congenital)
AetiologyPost-streptococcal autoimmuneDegenerative, congenital, inflammatory
Commissural fusionYes (pathognomonic)No
Subvalvular diseaseYes (chordal)No (mostly annular)
Co-existing valve diseaseAortic, tricuspidAortic stenosis (calcific)
LAA thrombusCommonRare
AF prevalenceHighLower
Wilkins scoreApplicableNot really
PMBVEffectiveNot effective
ProphylaxisBenzathine Pen GTreat underlying cause

5. Diagnosis

  • TTE — first-line
  • MVA planimetry (rheumatic: orifice at leaflet tips)
  • Pressure half-time for MVA (220/PHT)
  • Mean gradient (continuous wave Doppler)
  • Commissural fusion (parasternal short axis) — rheumatic hallmark
  • MAC — calcific deposits at annulus
  • TOE — pre-PMBV (exclude LA thrombus), assess MAC
  • Wilkins score (rheumatic) — leaflet thickness, mobility, calcification, subvalvular

6. Therapeutic Implications

  • Rheumatic MS → PMBV if suitable (Wilkins ≤ 8, no MR, no LA thrombus, pliable)
  • MAC MS → PMBV less effective (heavy calcification, no commissural fusion); surgery often needed
  • Congenital MS → surgical repair / replacement
  • Inflammatory → treat underlying condition

7. Global Health Note

  • RHD is the leading cause of MS worldwide and a disease of poverty
  • WHO, World Heart Federation, and RHD Action push for RHD control in endemic countries
  • Secondary prophylaxis with benzathine penicillin G prevents progression
  • Echocardiographic screening in schoolchildren in endemic regions picks up subclinical RHD
Pearls
- Rheumatic MS = commissural fusion (pathognomonic)
- Non-rheumatic MS = calcific MAC, congenital, inflammatory
- PMBV works for rheumatic MS — not for calcific MAC
- Rheumatic MS often co-exists with AR / AS / TR
- Lone MS is rheumatic until proven otherwise

topics.title: Hemodynamic Assessment Mva Mean Gradient Pht
topics.html:
1. Goals

Quantify severity of MS (MVA, gradient, PHT) and assess secondary consequences (LA enlargement, pulmonary HTN, RV function). Echo is the cornerstone.

2. Key Parameters

ParameterSevere ThresholdNotes
Mitral valve area (MVA)< 1.5 cm² (some guidelines <1.0 very severe)Planimetry or PHT
Mean transvalvular gradient> 10 mmHg at restCW Doppler
Pressure half-time (PHT)> 220 ms (some use >180 for severe)MVA = 220 / PHT
Pulmonary artery systolic pressure> 50 mmHgTR jet + RAP
Indexed MVA< 1.0 cm²/m² (in large patients)Adjusted for BSA

3. Methods of Measuring MVA

Planimetry (Anatomical Area)

  • Parasternal short axis at the leaflet tips (smallest orifice during diastole)
  • Trace the inner edge of the orifice
  • Gold standard for rheumatic MS
  • Errors: imaging plane (off-axis = over-estimate), calcification, atrial view
  • 3D TEE planimetry — most accurate

Pressure Half-Time (PHT)

  • MVA = 220 / PHT
  • Assumption: linear LA-LV pressure decay
  • Affected by LV compliance and LA pressure (over-estimates MVA in older / diastolic dysfunction)
  • Quick, reproducible, widely used

Continuity Equation (MVA)

  • MVA = (LVOT area × LVOT VTI) / MV VTI
  • Less flow-dependent than PHT
  • Useful when PHT / planimetry discordant

Proximal Isovelocity Surface Area (PISA)

  • PISA-based MVA = π r² × aliasing velocity / peak MV E velocity
  • Less commonly used (technical difficulty in MS)

4. Severity Grading

SeverityMVA (cm²)Mean grad (mmHg)PHT (ms)
Mild> 1.5< 5< 130
Moderate1.0–1.55–10130–220
Severe< 1.5 (or < 1.0 very severe)> 10> 220
Severe MS with symptoms = intervention. Severe MS asymptomatic with good compliance may be observed.

5. Associated Findings

Left Atrium

  • LA enlargement (AP diameter >40 mm, area >20 cm²/m²)
  • Spontaneous echo contrast (SEC) — precursor to thrombus
  • LA thrombus — most often in LAA; rule out by TOE

Pulmonary Circulation

  • Pulmonary hypertension (sPAP > 50 mmHg)
  • RV dilation, hypertrophy
  • Tricuspid regurgitation (functional)

Right Heart

  • RV systolic dysfunction
  • RA enlargement
  • Systemic venous congestion (hepatic congestion, peripheral oedema)

Other Valves

  • Co-existing rheumatic AR / AS
  • Functional TR (from PH)

6. Stress / Exercise Testing

  • Asymptomatic severe MS → exercise echo / right heart cath
  • Δ mean gradient > 15 mmHg or sPAP > 60 mmHg with exercise = consider intervention
  • Useful when symptoms are equivocal

7. Special Considerations

MS with AF

  • Loss of atrial "kick" → ↓ LV filling → ↓ cardiac output
  • Higher gradient at any given MVA
  • Higher LA thrombus risk → TOE before PMBV
  • Anticoagulation with warfarin (INR 2.0–3.0)

"Low-Flow, Low-Gradient" MS

  • Severe MVA on planimetry but low gradient (low output, e.g., concurrent LV dysfunction)
  • PHT unreliable in low cardiac output
  • Use planimetry + continuity equation

MacCallum Patch

  • Subendocardial fibrosis / calcification in LA from chronic RHD
  • Marker of advanced RHD
  • Increases LA thrombus risk

8. Diagnostic Algorithm

Suspected MS
   ├── TTE (parasternal SAX for planimetry, CW for PHT / gradient, RV / TR jet for sPAP)
   ├── LA / LAA (TTE limited; TOE for thrombus)
   ├── Severity determined?
   │     ├── Yes → manage
   │     └── No (discordant) → TOE, stress echo, 3D planimetry
   └── Treat / surveillance

9. When TEE is Needed

  • Pre-PMBV (exclusion of LA thrombus, assessment of MR, Wilkins score)
  • Suboptimal TTE windows
  • Discordant TTE measurements
  • Suspected prosthetic MS (valve thrombosis, pannus)
Pearls
- Severe MS: MVA < 1.5 cm², mean grad > 10 mmHg, PHT > 220 ms
- MVA = 220 / PHT (Emory formula) — quick estimate
- 3D planimetry is the most accurate MVA
- Always assess LA size, sPAP, RV function, LAA thrombus (TOE)
- Stress echo in asymptomatic severe MS to unmask symptoms / pulmonary HTN

topics.title: Percutaneous Mitral Balloon Valvuloplasty Pmbv Criteria
topics.html:
1. Background

PMBV (Inoue balloon technique) is the first-line intervention for symptomatic rheumatic MS with suitable anatomy. It splits the fused commissures, increasing MVA and relieving obstruction — without surgery or prosthesis.

2. Indications

  • Symptomatic severe MS (MVA ≤ 1.5 cm², NYHA II-IV)
  • Asymptomatic severe MS with:
  • High thromboembolic risk (prior embolism, AF with dense SEC, LA thrombus resolving on anticoagulation)
  • Haemodynamic decompensation on exercise (sPAP > 60 mmHg, MV gradient > 15 mmHg)
  • Plan for pregnancy (intervene before conception)
  • Restenosis after surgical commissurotomy (selected)

3. Contraindications

Absolute

  • LA thrombus (especially in LAA) — risk of systemic embolism
  • More than mild MR (≥moderate MR is a contraindication)
  • Severe concomitant aortic or tricuspid disease requiring surgery
  • Heavy mitral calcification with commissural calcification (Wilkins > 8)
  • Massive / extensive subvalvular disease (chordal fusion / calcification)

Relative

  • Severe pulmonary hypertension (still proceed with care; some operators avoid)
  • Pregnancy (radiation exposure — use echo guidance, abdominal shielding)
  • Unfavourable anatomy (Wilkins > 8, calcified commissures)

4. The Wilkins Echo Score (Anatomic Suitability)

ComponentScore 1Score 2Score 3Score 4
Leaflet mobilityHighly mobileReduced mid-portionRestricted basal motionMinimal / immobile
Leaflet thicknessNormal (4–5 mm)Mid-leaflet thickeningTotal leaflet thickeningMarked thickening
CalcificationNone / singleScattered at marginsAlong leaflet edgesExtending into subvalvular
Subvalvular diseaseNoneMinimal chordal thickeningChordal thickening ≤1/3Extensive to papillary muscle
  • Maximum 16 (4 + 4 + 4 + 4)
  • Score ≤ 8 → ideal for PMBV
  • Score > 8 → high risk of suboptimal result, MR
  • Score ≥ 12 → consider surgery

Other Scores

  • Reid's score (Cormier): group 1 (pliable, no calc, no subvalvular) → best
  • PACman (Predictors of Atrial Crest Commissural Mitral ANatomy) — emerging

5. Pre-Procedural Workup

  • TTE with detailed MV assessment, MVA, mean gradient, MR severity
  • TOE (mandatory):
  • Exclude LA / LAA thrombus (essential)
  • Confirm MR severity (≤mild)
  • Wilkins score
  • Assess commissural fusion pattern
  • Coronary angio if >40 yr or risk factors (concomitant PCI / CABG)
  • Pregnancy test in women of childbearing age (use echo / minimal fluoro)

6. The Inoue Technique

  • Balloon — stepwise inflation across the MV
  • Transseptal puncture (TOE / TTE / fluoro guided)
  • Inflation steps:
  1. Step 1 (small diameter) — check positioning
  2. Step 2 — main dilation
  3. Step 3 — final check (final MVA ≥ 1.5 cm², MR ≤ mild, LA pressure ↓)
  • Stop if: MR worsens ≥moderate, hemodynamics deteriorate, MVA acceptable reached

7. Outcomes and Success

  • Procedural success = MVA ≥ 1.5 cm², MR ≤ mild, gradient ↓ ≥ 50%
  • Echo score ≤ 8: success >90%
  • Echo score 9–11: success 70–80%
  • Echo score > 11: success <50% (consider surgery)
  • Restenosis rate: ~10–30% at 5 years, 50% at 10 years

8. Complications

ComplicationFrequency
Significant MR (≥moderate)2–10%
Cardiac tamponade (transseptal)1–2%
Stroke / embolism0.5–1%
Severe MR requiring surgery1–2%
ASD at puncture sitesmall, often closes
Death< 1% (experienced centres)

9. Post-Procedural Care

  • Bed rest 6–12 hours, monitor puncture site
  • TTE next day (MVA, MR, gradient)
  • Anticoagulation:
  • AF + MS → warfarin (INR 2.0–3.0) lifelong
  • Sinus rhythm, no LA thrombus → aspirin (some centres)
  • Endocarditis prophylaxis for 6 months (some recommendations)
  • Surveillance — TTE at 6 months, 1 year, then yearly

10. Special Populations

Pregnancy

  • Severe MS + pregnancy = high risk
  • PMBV in 2nd trimester feasible, often echo-guided (no / minimal fluoro)
  • Diuretics, beta-blockers as bridge

Children / Adolescents

  • PMBV feasible in rheumatic MS (RHD endemic areas)
  • Avoid in congenital MS (different anatomy — parachute valve)

Re-do PMBV

  • Restenosis after previous PMBV → consider re-do if anatomy still suitable
  • Higher complication rate; careful selection

MS with AF

  • Always TOE to exclude thrombus
  • Warfarin (not DOAC) — INRs may need 2.5–3.5
  • Some centres require 4–6 weeks of therapeutic INR before PMBV
Pearls
- PMBV is first-line for rheumatic MS with Wilkins ≤ 8, no LA thrombus, ≤mild MR
- Always TOE before PMBV — exclude LAA thrombus (Class I)
- LA thrombus + MR ≥moderate = absolute contraindications
- Procedural success = MVA ≥ 1.5 cm², MR ≤ mild, gradient ↓ ≥ 50%
- AF + MS = warfarin (INR 2.0–3.0), DOAC contraindicated in rheumatic MS

topics.title: Surgical Options Commissurotomy Replacement
topics.html:
1. Repair vs Replacement

  • Mitral: repair preferred when feasible (degenerative MR, ~95% repairable)
  • Tricuspid: repair (annuloplasty ring) preferred
  • Aortic: replacement standard (repair for select BAV, root aneurysm)
  • Pulmonary: repair in congenital

2. Commissurotomy

  • Closed (without bypass, historical) — for MS in developing world
  • Open (with bypass, direct vision) — for MS with calcified valves
  • Balloon valvuloplasty (BMV) — alternative to surgical commissurotomy in MS

3. Replacement Options

Mechanical (bileaflet, single tilting disc, caged ball)

  • Lifelong warfarin (INR 2.5–3.5)
  • <60y: durable, no degeneration
  • Bleeding/thrombosis risk

Bioprosthetic (porcine, bovine pericardial)

  • TAVI for older/high-risk
  • 10–15 y durability, no anticoagulation (after 3 mo)
  • Structural valve deterioration ~10y

4. Choice

  • <50y: mechanical (if no contraindication to warfarin)
  • 50–65y: shared decision
  • >65y: bioprosthetic
  • Pregnancy: bioprosthetic (avoid warfarin teratogenicity)
  • ESRD: bioprosthetic (avoid warfarin + dialysis)
Pearls — Mechanical → lifelong warfarin. Bioprosthetic in older, pregnancy, ESRD, contraindication to warfarin. TAVI is the new standard for older patients with AS.

topics.title: Primary Degenerative Rheumatic Vs Secondary Functional
topics.html:
1. Primary (Degenerative) MR

  • Leaflet/chordal pathology — prolapse, flail, Barlow's, fibroelastic deficiency
  • Most common cause of surgical MR in developed world
  • Repair preferred (95% success in expert centers)
  • Treat symptoms or LV dysfunction (EF 30–60%, ESD <40 mm)

2. Secondary (Functional) MR

  • LV remodeling/dilation causes annular dilation + leaflet tethering
  • Leaflet itself is normal
  • Cause: ischemic (post-MI) or non-ischemic DCM
  • GDMT first — reverse remodeling may improve MR
  • CRT can improve functional MR
  • Surgery: annuloplasty (recurrence high) or replacement
  • MitraClip (TEER) — for high-risk surgical candidates (COAPT, MITRA-FR trials)

3. Distinguishing Echo

  • Primary: leaflet pathology, eccentric jet, normal LV size
  • Secondary: normal leaflets, central jet, dilated LV, tenting

4. Timing

  • Primary: surgery when symptomatic, EF 30–60%, ESD <40, AF, or pulmonary HTN
  • Secondary: optimal medical therapy ± device first; consider TEER if persistent
Pearls — In secondary MR, treat the LV first. Mitral surgery for secondary MR alone has poor outcomes. COAPT showed MitraClip benefit in select patients; MITRA-FR did not. Patient selection matters.

topics.title: Hemodynamic Assessment Vc Width Eroa Regurgitant Volume
topics.html:
1. Definition

Echocardiographic assessment of MR severity uses qualitative, semi-quantitative, and quantitative parameters. Integration of multiple measures is essential; no single parameter is sufficient.

2. Key Features

  • Qualitative: colour jet area (central vs eccentric); pulmonary vein flow reversal (systolic = severe)
  • Semi-quantitative:
  • Vena contracta width (VC): <3 mm mild, 3–6.9 mm moderate, ≥7 mm severe
  • PISA radius (EROA calculated from radius × aliasing velocity / peak MR velocity)
  • CW Doppler intensity and contour (dense, triangular = severe)
  • Quantitative:
  • EROA (effective regurgitant orifice area): <0.20 cm² mild, 0.20–0.39 moderate, ≥0.40 cm² severe
  • Regurgitant volume (R Vol): <30 mL mild, 30–59 moderate, ≥60 mL severe
  • Regurgitant fraction (RF): <30% mild, 30–49% moderate, ≥50% severe
  • Primary vs secondary MR (mechanism matters for treatment):
  • Primary: leaflet pathology (prolapse, flail, endocarditis, rheumatic)
  • Secondary (functional): LV remodelling → leaflet tethering, annular dilation; HF-driven
  • Echo: TTE first; TOE for ambiguous cases, pre-surgical planning

3. Management

  • Mild-moderate primary MR: monitor
  • Severe primary MR + symptoms OR LV dysfunction (LVEF ≤60%, LVESD ≥40 mm): surgery (repair preferred over replacement)
  • Secondary MR: optimise GDMT; consider TEER (MitraClip) if HF + severe MR + appropriate anatomy (COAPT criteria)
  • TEER contraindicated if LVEF <20%, severe RV dysfunction, severe pulmonary HTN
  • Serial echo: annual for moderate, every 6 months for severe/asymptomatic
Pearls
- Severe MR: VC ≥7 mm, EROA ≥0.4 cm², R Vol ≥60 mL
- Primary MR → surgery; secondary MR → GDMT + TEER (COAPT)
- PISA is the workhorse for EROA calculation
- Pulmonary vein systolic reversal = severe MR

topics.title: P2 Flail Chordal Rupture Barlows
topics.html:
1. Definitions

  • Flail leaflet — leaflet reverses into LA during systole (chordal rupture)
  • P2 flail — most common (middle scallop of posterior leaflet)
  • Barlow's disease — diffuse myxomatous degeneration, multi-segment prolapse, redundant tissue, thick leaflets
  • Fibroelastic deficiency (FED) — focal, thin leaflets, single segment, elderly

2. Pathology

  • Myxomatous degeneration, chordal elongation/rupture
  • Posterior leaflet prolapse 2–3× more common than anterior

3. Clinical

  • Acute severe MR from chordal rupture → acute pulmonary edema
  • Mid-systolic click ± late systolic murmur
  • Holosystolic murmur if severe MR

4. Management

  • Acute severe MR — emergency surgery if unstable
  • Chronic severe primary MR — surgery at symptoms, EF 30–60%, ESD <40, AF
  • Repair technique — quadrangular/triangular resection, neochordae, annuloplasty ring
  • Repair success >90% in expert centers, mortality <1%
  • Percutaneous edge-to-edge (MitraClip) for high-risk
Pearls — P2 flail = most common cause of acute severe primary MR. Repair has lower mortality than replacement and avoids prosthetic complications. Refer to high-volume mitral surgeons for best outcomes.

topics.title: Transcatheter Edge To Edge Repair Teer Mitraclip
topics.html:
1. Definition

TEER = percutaneous transcatheter edge-to-edge mitral valve repair using a clip device (MitraClip, PASCAL) to approximate the free edges of the MV leaflets, reducing the regurgitant orifice. Used predominantly for secondary (functional) MR, also high-risk primary MR.

2. Key Features

  • Indications:
  • Severe secondary (functional) MR in symptomatic HF patients on GDMT
  • COAPT criteria: LVEF 20–50%, LVESD <70 mm, MR grade 3+/4+, PASP <70 mmHg, NYHA II–IV, no severe RV dysfunction
  • High-risk primary MR in non-surgical candidates
  • Anatomy: leaflet anatomy must be suitable — central A2-P2, sufficient leaflet tissue, no severe calcification
  • Procedure: transseptal, trans-THV or trans-jugular approach; clip deployed under TOE/fluoro guidance
  • Outcomes: COAPT showed mortality benefit and ↓ HF hospitalisation; MITRA-FR (negative) — patient selection critical
  • Complications: leaflet tear, partial clip detachment, residual MR, ASD from transseptal, stroke

3. Management

  • Heart team decision (cardiologist, surgeon, imaging expert)
  • Pre-procedure TEE to assess anatomy, severity
  • GDMT must be optimised first (≥3 months); only proceed if symptoms persist
  • Post-procedure: antiplatelet (aspirin ± clopidogrel 1 month); lifelong GDMT
  • Follow-up: clinical, TTE at 1, 6, 12 months then annual
  • Re-intervention: redo TEER or surgery
  • Emerging: tricuspid TEER, annuloplasty devices
Pearls
- COAPT, not MITRA-FR — patient selection is everything
- LVEF 20–50%, LVESD <70 mm = ideal candidate
- GDMT first (≥3 months) before TEER
- Heart team decision mandatory

topics.title: Surgical Repair Vs Replacement
topics.html:
1. Mitral

  • Repair preferred for degenerative MR (90–95% repairable)
  • Advantages: ↓ mortality, ↓ complications, no anticoagulation
  • Replacement for rheumatic, heavily calcified, failed repair

2. Aortic

  • Replacement standard for most AS/AR
  • Repair for select: BAV with regurgitation, root aneurysm (David, Yacoub procedures)
  • TAVI for older/high-risk AS

3. Tricuspid

  • Repair preferred (annuloplasty ring)
  • Replacement only if severe destruction, failed repair

4. Decision Factors

FactorFavors RepairFavors Replacement
AnatomyLocalized pathologyDiffuse/calcified
AgeYoungerOlder
Anticoag toleranceWant to avoidCan tolerate
Surgeon expertiseMitral/TV expert—
EtiologyDegenerative, prolapseRheumatic, endocarditis
Pearls — Mitral repair > replacement for degenerative MR (lower operative mortality, better long-term survival, avoids anticoag). Refer to high-volume valve centers. AS is mostly replacement territory; TAVI has revolutionized the field.

topics.title: Tr Primary Secondary Functional
topics.html:
1. Primary (organic, ~10%)

  • Endocarditis (IVDU), rheumatic, carcinoid, Ebstein anomaly, trauma, iatrogenic (lead/pacing)

2. Secondary (functional, ~90%)

  • Annular dilation from RV pressure/volume overload
  • Causes: L-sided heart disease (most common), pulmonary HTN, AF, RV dilation
  • Leaflets are structurally normal

3. Severity

  • Mild — normal-looking echo
  • Moderate — incomplete coaptation
  • Severe — wide jet, large VC, hepatic vein systolic reversal, RA/RV dilation

4. Management

  • Medical: diuretics, treat underlying cause (LV dysfunction, pulmonary HTN)
  • Surgical (TV repair, usually ring annuloplasty) at time of left-sided surgery OR isolated if severe + symptomatic
  • Transcatheter (TriClip) — for high-risk surgical candidates (TRILUMINATE trial)
Pearls — TR is usually secondary, treat the left side first. Isolated TV surgery high-risk. New TriClip for inoperable patients. Severe TR after left-sided surgery = poor prognosis.

topics.title: Tricuspid Stenosis Rare
topics.html:
1. Etiology

  • Rheumatic (most common, almost always with MS)
  • Carcinoid syndrome
  • Congenital
  • Prosthetic valve obstruction
  • Endocarditis with large vegetation

2. Clinical

  • Fatigue, peripheral edema, ascites
  • Diastolic murmur at LLSB, ↑ with inspiration
  • Giant a waves on JVP, abdominal JVP

3. Echo

  • Thickened/restricted leaflets
  • Mean gradient >5 mmHg
  • RV/RA enlargement

4. Management

  • Diuretics for congestion
  • Surgical (open commissurotomy or replacement)
  • Percutaneous balloon rarely feasible (large annulus)
  • Treat underlying cause (rheumatic, carcinoid)
Pearls — TS is rare, almost always rheumatic + MS. Don't confuse with TR (systolic murmur, not diastolic). Surgical replacement with bioprosthetic preferred (mechanical valves have high thrombosis risk in RV).

topics.title: Surgical Tricuspid Intervention Timing
topics.html:
1. Concomitant with Left-Sided Surgery

  • Severe TR at time of MV/AV surgery
  • Mild-moderate TR with annular dilation (>42 mm or >21 mm/m²) at time of left-sided surgery
  • Mild-moderate TR with pulmonary HTN at time of left-sided surgery

2. Isolated TR Surgery

  • Severe symptomatic TR despite medical therapy
  • Progressive RV dilation/dysfunction
  • Afterload (pulmonary pressures) not severely elevated

3. Timing is Key

  • Too early = unnecessary surgery
  • Too late = irreversible RV dysfunction → high mortality

4. Transcatheter

  • TriClip (TEER) for high-risk surgical candidates (TRILUMINATE 2023)
  • Expanding indications
Pearls — Don't wait too long — RV dysfunction + severe TR = high operative mortality. Repair (annuloplasty ring) preferred over replacement. New TriClip is changing practice for inoperable patients.

topics.title: Pulmonary Stenosis
topics.html:
Pulmonary valve disease in adults is most often residual or recurrent after childhood intervention (balloon valvuloplasty, surgical valvotomy), or seen in repaired TOF with transannular patch causing free PR.

1. Definition & Background

  • Pulmonary stenosis (PS) — obstruction at valve, subvalve, or supravalve level; in adults usually valvar, congenital (bicuspid/dysplastic pulmonary valve, Noonan syndrome).
  • Pulmonary regurgitation (PR) — retrograde flow into RV during diastole; most common cause in adults is post-TOF repair with transannular patch.

2. Etiology & Pathophysiology

  • PS: congenital bicuspid/dysplastic valve, Noonan syndrome, carcinoid (rare), rheumatic, extrinsic compression.
  • PR: iatrogenic (transannular patch in TOF repair), endocarditis, carcinoid, pulmonary hypertension (rare), congenital absence/dysplasia.
  • PS physiology: RV outflow obstruction → RV hypertrophy → ↓pulmonary flow → fixed low cardiac output; severe PS may cause R→L shunt via PFO.
  • PR physiology: volume overload of RV → RV dilation → TR → RV failure; chronic PR well tolerated for years then decompensates.

3. Clinical Features

  • Mild PS/PR: usually asymptomatic.
  • Severe PS: exertional dyspnoea, fatigue, chest pain, syncope (fixed output), RV failure (oedema, hepatomegaly, JVP).
  • Severe PR (post-TOF): progressive exercise intolerance, RV failure, atrial/ventricular arrhythmia, sudden death risk.
  • Signs: systolic ejection murmur LUSB (PS), early diastolic decrescendo at LUSB (PR), RV heave, single/soft P2, TR murmur, RV S3/S4.

4. Diagnosis & Investigations

  • ECG: RVH, RBBB (post-TOF), RAD, P pulmonale.
  • CXR: prominent pulmonary artery (PS), RV enlargement (PR), oligaemic lung fields (severe PS).
  • TTE/TOE: peak/mean gradient across PV (mild <36, moderate 36–64, severe >64 mmHg), PR severity (diastolic flow reversal in PA branches), RV size & function, TR severity, associated lesions.
  • CMR: gold standard for PR severity and RV volumes (RVEDV index >160 mL/m² or RVESV >80 mL/m² = severe).
  • Exercise test: symptom evaluation, arrhythmia.
  • Catheter: diagnostic ± balloon valvuloplasty.

5. Management

  • PS: balloon pulmonary valvuloplasty (BPV) first line for valvar PS (peak gradient >50 mmHg or symptoms); surgery (valvotomy) if BPV not feasible.
  • PR post-TOF: pulmonary valve replacement (PVR) — surgical (bioprosthetic) or transcatheter (TPVR, Melody/Edwards SAPIEN). Indications: symptoms, RVEDVi >160 mL/m², RVESVi >80 mL/m², RVEF <47%, progressive TR, sustained arrhythmia.
  • Medical: diuretics for RV failure, arrhythmia management, endocarditis prophylaxis not routinely indicated (see IE prophylaxis guidance).
  • Follow-up: lifelong — TTE/CMR every 1–3 yr; exercise testing.

6. Complications & Prognosis

  • PS: RV failure, arrhythmia, endocarditis, sudden death (severe), R→L shunt if PFO/ASD.
  • PR post-TOF: RV failure, VT (macroreentrant, isthmus-dependent — risk ↑ with RV dilation), atrial arrhythmia, sudden death.
  • Re-intervention: 10–15% need re-PVR within 10 yr (bioprosthetic degeneration).
  • Prognosis after timely PVR is excellent; long-standing severe RV dilation/failure is the main determinant of outcome.

topics.title: Pulmonary Regurgitation Post Tof Repair Pah
topics.html:
1. Etiology

  • Post-TOF repair (most common in adults)
  • PAH with annular dilation
  • Endocarditis (rare)
  • Carcinoid
  • Iatrogenic (post-PV intervention)

2. Clinical

  • Often asymptomatic for years
  • Progressive RV dilation → TR → RV failure
  • Exercise intolerance, AF, VT

3. Diagnosis

  • Echo: diastolic flow reversal in branch PAs, RV enlargement
  • CMR — gold standard for PR fraction (>25% severe) + RV volumes

4. Management

  • Severe symptomatic PR with RV dilation (RVEDVi >160 mL/m²)
  • Transcatheter pulmonary valve (Melody, Sapien) — preferred when feasible
  • Surgical PVR (pulmonary valve replacement) for others
  • Timing: before irreversible RV dysfunction
Pearls — Most common cause of PR in adults = post-TOF. CMR is gold standard for quantification. Transcatheter PV is revolutionizing management. Operate before RV dysfunction becomes irreversible.

topics.title: Transcatheter Pulmonary Valve Replacement
topics.html:
1. Indications

  • Severe PR post-TOF repair with RV dilation/dysfunction
  • Conduit dysfunction (RV-PA) — stenosis or regurgitation
  • Symptomatic or progressive RV dilation

2. Devices

  • Melody valve (Medtronic) — bovine jugular vein, 16–22 mm
  • Sapien valve (Edwards) — 20–29 mm

3. Procedure

  • Femoral or internal jugular access
  • Conduit preparation (prestenting)
  • Valve deployment

4. Outcomes & Follow-up

  • Procedural success >95%
  • Endocarditis risk 1–2% per year
  • Lifelong cardiology follow-up
  • Re-intervention rate ~10% at 5 years
Pearls — Melody for small conduits (≤22 mm), Sapien for larger. Endocarditis risk is real — prophylactic antibiotics. CMR follow-up is standard.

topics.title: Combined As + Mr
topics.html:
1. Hemodynamics

  • LV faces combined pressure (AS) + volume (MR) overload
  • Low-flow, low-gradient states common
  • AS severity may be underestimated by gradient alone

2. Assessment

  • Echo — careful measurement of both
  • AVA, mean gradient, dimensionless index (DI)
  • MR severity (EROA, regurgitant volume)
  • AS is more often the driver of symptoms

3. Management

  • Treat symptomatic severe AS first (dominant lesion)
  • If both severe, may need combined AVR + MV surgery
  • TAVI for AS — what about MR?
  • TAVI may improve MR in ~50% (LV remodeling)
  • Persistent severe MR after TAVI = high risk

4. Surgical Risk

  • Combined AVR + MVR: STS risk additive
  • Consider TAVI + future TEER (MitraClip) for high-risk
Pearls — AS usually the dominant lesion in AS+MR. TAVI may improve functional MR over time. Combined surgery for true severe dual valve disease. Low-flow AS common — measure with dobutamine stress if EF <50%.

topics.title: Mixed Valve Disease Management Priorities
topics.html:
1. Approach

  • Identify the dominant lesion (drives symptoms/prognosis)
  • Assess all valves (often multi-valve involvement in rheumatic)
  • Tailor therapy to the most severe component

2. Common Mixed Diseases

  • AS + AR — combined pressure + volume overload
  • MS + MR — rheumatic, common
  • MS + AR — rheumatic
  • TR + TS — rheumatic
  • Multiple valves in rheumatic heart disease

3. Management Principles

  • Treat the dominant lesion first
  • Surgery may need to address multiple valves
  • Percutaneous options for individual lesions (BMV, TAVI, MitraClip, TriClip)

4. Example

  • MS + MR: BMV may help if MV morphology suitable, but worsens MR if not
  • Consider MVR + TV repair instead of BMV
Pearls — Always identify the dominant lesion. BMV can worsen MR. Rheumatic often multi-valve. Consider surgery for multi-valve rheumatic disease in young patients.

topics.title: Mechanical Vs Bioprosthetic Valve Choice
topics.html:
1. Definition

Prosthetic valve choice = decision between mechanical (durable, requires lifelong anticoagulation) and bioprosthetic (tissue, limited durability ~10–15 y, no long-term anticoagulation). Patient age, lifestyle, surgical risk, ability to anticoagulate all factor in.

2. Key Features

  • Mechanical valves: bileaflet (St Jude, CarboMedics), single tilting disc (Medtronic Hall), caged ball (Starr-Edwards, historical)
  • Durable (essentially lifelong)
  • Lifelong warfarin (INR 2.5–3.5 depending on valve/position)
  • Higher bleeding risk; lower reoperation risk
  • Thrombosis risk higher if anticoagulation interrupted
  • Bioprosthetic (tissue) valves: bovine pericardial, porcine
  • Limited durability (10–15 y; faster in younger patients)
  • No lifelong anticoagulation (just 3–6 mo)
  • Reoperation at structural valve deterioration
  • TAVI for failed bioprosthetic ("valve-in-valve")
  • Decision factors:
  • Age <50: mechanical usually preferred (durability)
  • Age 50–65: shared decision-making
  • Age >65–70: bioprosthetic usually preferred
  • Life expectancy, bleeding risk, occupation/lifestyle, ability to comply with anticoagulation, reoperation risk

3. Management

  • Mechanical:
  • Warfarin (target INR per position: 2.5 aortic, 3.0 mitral)
  • Bridging for surgery
  • Aspirin 75–100 mg daily (some guidelines add)
  • DOACs contraindicated
  • Bioprosthetic:
  • Warfarin or DOAC for 3–6 months (per guidelines)
  • Aspirin long-term
  • Surveillance echo: baseline, 5 y, then every 1–3 y
  • Pregnancy: bioprosthetic preferred (avoid warfarin teratogenicity)
  • Valve-in-valve TAVI for failed bioprosthetic in high-risk reoperation candidates
  • Endocarditis prophylaxis: mechanical and bioprosthetic for dental/respiratory (per guidelines)
Pearls
- <50 y → mechanical, >65 y → bioprosthetic
- DOACs contraindicated in mechanical valves
- Mechanical = lifelong warfarin (INR 2.5–3.5)
- Bioprosthetic = degenerates ~10–15 y (faster in young)

topics.title: Anticoagulation Targets Inr Doacs Contraindicated In Mechanical
topics.html:
1. Mechanical Valves — LIFELONG Warfarin

  • Aortic mechanical, no risk factors: INR 2.0–3.0
  • Aortic mechanical + risk factors (AF, prior TE, LV dysfunction): INR 2.5–3.5
  • Mitral mechanical: INR 2.5–3.5
  • Tricuspid mechanical (rare): INR 3.0–4.0 (highest risk)
  • Risk factors: AF, prior thromboembolism, LVEF <30%, hypercoagulable state

2. Bioprosthetic Valves

  • First 3 months (any position): warfarin INR 2.0–3.0
  • After 3 months: aspirin alone (or no anticoagulation if no AF)
  • If AF: full anticoagulation per AF guidelines (DOAC OK)
  • DOACs are an option for bioprosthetic + AF (subgroup analyses from ARISTOTLE, ROCKET-AF)

3. Adding Antiplatelets

  • Mechanical + aspirin: controversial, ↑ bleeding (RECORD trial)
  • Routine low-dose aspirin reasonable if no bleeding history

4. DOACs — CONTRAINDICATED in Mechanical Valves

  • RE-ALIGN trial: dabigatran ↑ thromboembolism + bleeding vs warfarin
  • Never use in mechanical valves
Pearls — Mechanical → lifelong warfarin only. DOACs are contraindicated in mechanical valves. Bioprosthetic + AF → DOAC acceptable after 3 months. INR targets depend on position + risk factors.

topics.title: Valve Thrombosis Diagnosis Thrombolysis Vs Surgery
topics.html:
1. Definition

Prosthetic valve thrombosis (PVT) = thrombus formation on a prosthetic valve, leading to obstruction or systemic embolism. More common in mechanical valves, especially with subtherapeutic anticoagulation, but can occur in bioprosthetic (early post-op).

2. Key Features

  • Mechanical valve thrombosis (0.5–8%/y):
  • Risk factors: subtherapeutic anticoagulation, atrial fibrillation, hypercoagulable state, LV dysfunction, pregnancy
  • Symptoms: dyspnoea, HF, embolic event, valve click muffled/absent
  • Diagnosis: TTE (↓ opening, ↓ effective orifice area, ↑ gradients, mass on valve), TOE (gold standard, especially mitral position)
  • Cinefluoroscopy: stuck leaflet
  • Bioprosthetic valve thrombosis (early, <1 y):
  • Often subclinical; presents as ↑ gradients mimicking stenosis
  • Improved with short-term anticoagulation post-op
  • Types: obstructive (more severe) vs non-obstructive
  • Differential: pannus (chronic, slow progression), endocarditis, structural degeneration

3. Management

  • Stable, non-obstructive: optimise anticoagulation (IV UFH → warfarin); reimage
  • Obstructive (mechanical):
  • Thrombolysis (alteplase 10 mg bolus + 90 mg over 90 min) — first-line if high surgical risk; success 80%
  • Urgent surgery if low surgical risk, large thrombus (>10 mm), LA thrombus, failed lysis, bioprosthetic
  • Anticoagulation: UFH → warfarin; target INR 3.0–4.0
  • Bioprosthetic: anticoagulation (warfarin or DOAC) for 3–6 months; reimage
  • Adjuncts: antiplatelet, treat underlying cause
  • Endocarditis: must exclude
Pearls
- Mechanical valve thrombosis — urgent management
- Thrombolysis first-line if high surgical risk; surgery if low risk
- DOACs are contraindicated for mechanical valve anticoagulation
- Bioprosthetic thrombosis often subclinical — check gradients

topics.title: Prosthetic Valve Endocarditis
topics.html:
1. Epidemiology

  • 1–3% of prosthetic valves
  • Mechanical ≈ bioprosthetic
  • Early PVE (<60 d, nosocomial) vs Late PVE (>60 d, community)
  • Early: Staph aureus, coagulase-negative staph, GNR
  • Late: similar to native (Strep, Staph, enterococci)

2. Diagnosis

  • Modified Duke criteria — same as native IE
  • Echo: TTE sensitivity ~50%, TEE 90%+
  • Look for: vegetations, paravalvular leak, dehiscence, abscess

3. Management

  • Empiric: vancomycin + gentamicin + rifampin (covers Staph including MRSA)
  • Directed by cultures
  • Surgery indications:
  • Heart failure
  • Uncontrolled infection
  • Perivalvular extension/abscess
  • Large vegetations (>10 mm)
  • Prosthetic dehiscence
  • Relapse

4. Outcomes

  • Mortality 30–50%
  • Worse with Staph, root abscess, early surgery
Pearls — TEE is essential (TTE misses 50%). Surgery often needed. Staph aureus PVE mortality ~50%. Endocarditis team improves outcomes.

topics.title: Atrial Septal Defect Asd Types Closure Criteria Transcatheter Vs Surgical
topics.html:
1. Etiology & Pathophysiology

Etiology & Pathophysiology

The pathophysiology of Atrial Septal Defect - types, closure criteria, transcatheter vs surgical involves multiple cardiovascular mechanisms: ischaemic (atherosclerosis, plaque rupture/erosion → thrombosis), structural (hypertrophy, dilation, remodelling, fibrosis), electrical (re-entry, abnormal automaticity, triggered activity, ion channel dysfunction), neurohormonal (RAAS, SNS, BNP, vasopressin, endothelin), inflammatory (cytokines, immune ac

2. Classification

Clinical Features

Clinical presentation of Atrial Septal Defect - types, closure criteria, transcatheter vs surgical varies by acuity (acute vs chronic), severity, and patient factors. Cardinal symptoms: chest pain (typical: central, crushing, radiating to jaw/left arm, exertion-related; atypical more common in women, elderly, diabetics), dyspnoea (exertional, orthopnoea, PND, nocturnal), palpitations, syncope/pre-syncope, fatigue, exercise intolerance, perip

3. Clinical Features

Diagnostic Approach

Diagnostic approach to Atrial Septal Defect - types, closure criteria, transcatheter vs surgical includes: (1) History: SOCRATES for pain, NYHA/CCS for symptoms, SAMPLE for general, family/social history, drug history. (2) Physical examination: vital signs, targeted system examination. (3) ECG: 12-lead, serial if acute, right-sided leads for inferior MI, V7-V9 for posterior MI, comparison with prior.

4. Diagnosis & Investigations

Severity Assessment & Risk Stratification

Severity assessment uses: (1) Clinical scoring systems (GRACE/TIMI/HEART for ACS, NYHA/INTERMACS for HF, CHA2DS2-VASc/HAS-BLED for AF, Child-Pugh/MELD for liver, KDIGO for renal, EuroSCORE/STS for surgical risk). (2) Biomarkers (peak troponin, BNP, hs-CRP). (3) Imaging parameters (LVEF, valve area, regurgitation volume, PASP, RV function). (4) Functional capacity (6MWD, CPET with VO2 max, NYHA class).

Differential Diagnosis

Differential diagnosis of Atrial Septal Defect - types, closure criteria, transcatheter vs surgical includes related cardiovascular conditions and non-cardiac mimics. Cardiovascular: ischaemic heart disease (stable angina, ACS, microvascular angina, vasospasm), hypertensive heart disease (LVH, HFpEF, aortic dissection), valvular heart disease (AS, AR, MS, MR, TR, prosthetic), cardiomyopathy (DCM, HCM, RCM, ARVC, Takotsubo, infiltrative —

Investigations

Investigations for Atrial Septal Defect - types, closure criteria, transcatheter vs surgical: Bedside: ECG (12-lead, serial, rhythm strip), vital signs (BP, HR, RR, SpO2), JVP, urine output, glucometer, capillary refill.

topics.title: Pulmonary Valve Stenosis Regurgitation
topics.html:
1. Pulmonary Stenosis

  • Valvar (most common, usually congenital)
  • Subvalvular (RVOT obstruction, e.g. post-TOF repair)
  • Supravalvular (PA stenosis, often in syndromes)
  • Symptoms: dyspnea, fatigue; severe can cause RV failure
  • Echo: peak gradient >64 mmHg = severe
  • Treatment:
  • Balloon valvuloplasty for valvar PS (peak gradient >50 mmHg)
  • Surgical valvotomy if balloon not feasible
  • TPVR (Melody, Sapien) for dysplastic valve or conduit

2. Pulmonary Regurgitation

  • Most common cause: post-TOF repair (transannular patch)
  • Long asymptomatic period
  • Progressive RV dilation → TR → RV failure
  • Symptoms: exercise intolerance, AF, VT
  • Echo + CMR (gold standard for PR fraction)
  • Treatment: TPVR or surgical PVR before irreversible RV dysfunction

3. Follow-up

  • Lifelong cardiology follow-up
  • Annual echo for moderate/severe
  • CMR every 2–3 years for severe PR/RV volume
  • Endocarditis prophylaxis if prosthetic valve
Pearls — PS = treat with balloon valvuloplasty if severe. PR = treat with TPVR before RV dysfunction irreversible. CMR is gold standard. Lifelong follow-up.

topics.title: Endocarditis Prophylaxis
topics.html:
1. Etiology & Pathophysiology

Etiology & Pathophysiology

Pathogenesis: Endothelial injury (turbulent flow, jet lesion, MVP, degenerative valve, prosthetic, devices) → NBTE (non-bacterial thrombotic endocarditis) → bacteraemia (dental, GI, GU, skin) → adhesion (dextran, fibronectin-binding proteins) → colonisation → biofilm (S. epidermidis) → vegetation (fibrin, platelets, bacteria, inflammatory cells). Organisms: **S.

2. Classification

Clinical Features

Fever (90%, may be absent in elderly, IE, renal failure). New/changed murmur (50-80%, especially regurgitant). Septic emboli (stroke — 20%, systemic, splenic, renal, mesenteric, pulmonary in right-sided). Immunological: Osler nodes (tender, finger pulps, hands), Roth spots (retinal, pale, haemorrhagic), Janeway lesions (non-tender, palms/soles, septic emboli), splinter haemorrhages (subungual), glomerulonephritis,

3. Clinical Features

Diagnostic Approach

Modified Duke criteria (2000): Major — (1) positive blood culture (typical organism × 2, persistently positive), (2) imaging (echo vegetation, abscess, dehiscence, new partial dehiscence). Minor — predisposing heart disease, IVDU, fever >38, vascular phenomena (major emboli, mycotic aneurysm, intracranial, conjunctival, Janeway), immunological phenomena (Osler, Roth, glomerulonephritis, RF), positive blood culture not meeting major, e

4. Diagnosis & Investigations

Severity Assessment & Risk Stratification

Severity: severe (HF, embolic, large vegetation, abscess, prosthetic, fungal). Mortality: S. aureus 40%, fungal 50%, prosthetic 30-40%. Complications predict mortality. Vegetation size >10mm = embolic risk. Indication for surgery predicts outcome.

Differential Diagnosis

Culture-negative endocarditis (HACEK, Coxiella, Bartonella, fungi, lupus, malignancy — NBTE, Libman-Sacks). Atrial myxoma (mimics with emboli, fever). Vasculitis, SLE. Acute rheumatic fever (Jones). Marantic (NBTE) endocarditis (malignancy, SLE).

Investigations

Blood cultures (≥3 sets, different sites, before antibiotics), TTE, TOE, ECG (PR interval — abscess), CXR, bloods (FBC, ESR, CRP, RF, complement, U&E, LFT), urinalysis, CT/MRI (emboli, mycotic aneurysm), CT-PET, WBC-SPECT, colon (S. bovis).

5. Management

Management Algorithm

Antibiotics (bactericidal, prolonged 4-6 weeks, IV): Empirical (native valve, subacute, no haemodynamic compromise): ampicillin + (flu)cloxacillin + gentamicin (UK). Empirical (acute, severe, prosthetic, IVDU): vancomycin + gentamicin (or meropenem if HACEK/fungal). Specific: see regimens.

Special Populations

IVDU: S. aureus, right-sided (tricuspid), pulmonary emboli, often no surgery. Prosthetic: S. epidermidis, S. aureus, high mortality, surgery often needed. Elderly: enterococcus, S. gallolyticus (bovis) — colon cancer, GI malignancy. Pregnancy: rare, high mortality, urgent surgery. HACEK: difficult to culture, slow. Fungal: high mortality, surgery. Culture-negative: HACEK, Coxiella, Bartonella, fungi, prior antibiotics.

6. Complications & Prognosis

Complications & Prognosis

Heart failure (50-60%, valve destruction). Embolism (20-50%, stroke, systemic, pulmonary in right). Abscess (30-50% prosthetic, ring, conduction abnormalities). Mycotic aneurysm (2-10%, intracranial, hepatic, splenic). Glomerulonephritis (immune complex). Death (20-30% in-hospital, 30-50% at 1y). Relapse (5-10%). Recurrence (especially IVDU, prosthetic).

topics.title: Atrial Flutter Typical Atypical
topics.html:
1. Etiology & Pathophysiology

Etiology & Pathophysiology

Atherosclerotic narrowing of epicardial coronary arteries (≥70% stenosis, or ≥50% left main) → fixed supply limitation. Demand-supply mismatch: exertion (↑HR, ↑BP, ↑contractility) → ↑O2 demand → ischaemia (subendocardium first).

2. Classification

Clinical Features

Typical angina (3): (1) substernal chest discomfort (pressure, heaviness, squeezing, tightness), (2) precipitated by exertion or emotional stress, (3) relieved by rest or nitrates within minutes. Atypical (2 of 3, or non-cardiac — women, elderly, diabetics). Examination: usually normal.

3. Clinical Features

Diagnostic Approach

Resting ECG: usually normal (50%), may show Q waves (old MI), LVH, BBB, ST-T changes. Stress testing: (1) Exercise ECG (≥1mm STD, ≥80ms from J point, in ≥2 contiguous leads, at low workload/high risk — Duke Treadmill Score high-risk <−11), (2) Stress echo (RWMA, more sensitive/specific), (3) Stress CMR (perfusion, scar, viability), (4) Stress nuclear (perfusion, MUGA), (5) CTCA (low-intermediate probability, anatomical

4. Diagnosis & Investigations

Severity Assessment & Risk Stratification

CCS class (Canadian Cardiovascular Society): I (ordinary activity, no angina), II (slight limitation, angina with vigorous/rapid), III (marked limitation, angina with ordinary activity), IV (inability to perform any activity, angina at rest). Duke Treadmill Score: high-risk <−11, moderate −10 to +4, low ≥+5. Number of vessels: 1, 2, 3, left main (>50%). LV function (LVEF, regional WMA).

Differential Diagnosis

Oesophageal spasm, GERD, peptic ulcer, cholecystitis, MSK chest pain (costochondritis, Tietze), cervical radiculopathy, herpes zoster, anxiety, panic, PE, aortic dissection, pericarditis, myocarditis, Takotsubo, MS, AS, HCM, microvascular (INOCA), vasospasm, SCAD.

Investigations

Resting ECG, stress testing (exercise ECG, stress echo, stress CMR, stress nuclear), CTCA (low-intermediate), invasive coronary angiography (± FFR/iFR), echo (LV function, MR, structural), CMR (scar, viability), Holter (arrhythmia), CXR, bloods (FBC, U&E, LFT, TFT, lipid profile, hs-CRP, Lp(a), HbA1c), BP, BMI.

5. Management

Management Algorithm

Risk factor modification (ESC 2019): smoking cessation, BP <130/80, LDL-C <1.4 mmol/L (very high risk) or <1.8 (high), HbA1c <7%, weight (BMI <25), exercise ≥150 min/week, Mediterranean diet, alcohol <14 units/week. Anti-anginal (symptom control): (1) Beta-blocker (1st line, e.g.

Special Populations

Elderly: more comorbidities, less invasive, more GDMT, careful dosing. Women: atypical symptoms, more microvascular (INOCA), more abnormal stress test with normal angio, less revascularisation. Diabetes: more multivessel, smaller vessels, more diffuse, higher risk, SGLT2i benefit. CKD: more bleeding, more CV risk, dose adjust, more GDMT.

6. Complications & Prognosis

Complications & Prognosis

ACS (progression of plaque). MI. Heart failure (ischaemic cardiomyopathy). Arrhythmia (VT, AF, brady). Death (5-yr 5% with treatment, 15% without). Repeated hospitalisation (angina, revascularisation).

topics.title: Avnrt Avrt Wpw Syndrome
topics.html:
Catheter ablation is the first-line curative therapy for most AV nodal-dependent SVTs.

1. Definition & Background

  • AVNRT (AV nodal re-entrant tachycardia): re-entrant circuit within / around AV node — slow + fast pathways. Most common SVT (~60% of paroxysmal SVT).
  • AVRT (AV re-entrant tachycardia): re-entrant circuit uses AV node + accessory pathway. WPW = accessory pathway with antegrade conduction (short PR, delta wave).
  • Ablation uses RF (heating), cryo (freezing), or pulsed field (PFA) energy to destroy the arrhythmogenic tissue.

2. Indications for Ablation

  • Symptomatic AVNRT/AVRT refractory to or intolerant of drug therapy (or patient preference for cure).
  • Recurrent SVT impacting QoL, occupation (pilots, drivers), pregnancy planning.
  • WPW with high-risk features: shortest pre-excited RR interval (SPERRI) <250 ms, or accessory pathway ERP <250 ms, syncope, atrial fibrillation with rapid pre-excited conduction.
  • Documented tachycardia — provide 12-lead of SVT or documentation on device/loop recorder.
  • Pregnancy: consider ablation with minimal fluoroscopy / zero-fluoro (cryo or RF) in refractory cases.

3. Pre-Ablation Workup

  • 12-lead ECG in sinus rhythm (delta wave, short PR) and during tachycardia (RP interval, P-wave morphology).
  • Holter / event monitor to document arrhythmia.
  • EP study is essentially the procedure — diagnostic + therapeutic in one.
  • Echocardiogram — exclude structural heart disease (especially in WPW — Ebstein anomaly association).
  • Coagulation, pregnancy test, consent (risk of AV block, pacemaker).

4. AVNRT Ablation

  • Target: slow pathway — inferior septum between CS ostium and tricuspid annulus (Triangle of Koch).
  • Endpoint: non-inducibility of AVNRT with/without isoprenaline; residual slow-pathway echoes acceptable.
  • Energy: RF standard; cryo preferred near compact AV node (less AV block risk) — especially in children, athletes.
  • Acute success: >95%; recurrence: ~3–5%.
  • Risk of complete AV block: <1% (RF); <0.5% (cryo).

5. AVRT / WPW Ablation

  • Target: accessory pathway insertion site — mapped by earliest atrial or ventricular activation / pathway potential.
  • Sites: left lateral (most common), posteroseptal, right lateral, anteroseptal (near His — riskier), midseptal.
  • Approach: trans-septal (or retrograde aortic) for left-sided; inferior approach for posteroseptal.
  • Energy: RF standard; cryo for septal/parahissian.
  • Acute success: ~95% overall, lower for septal/anteroseptal pathways.
  • Complications: AV block (septal), cardiac tamponade (<1%), coronary artery injury (rare), aortic root damage (retrograde).
  • AVNRT: non-inducibility; single AV-nodal echo acceptable; no PR prolongation.
  • AVRT/WPW: loss of pre-excitation (delta wave absent), non-inducibility of AVRT/AF conduction over AP.

topics.title: Atrial Tachycardia
topics.html:
1. Definition

Atrial tachycardia (AT) = supraventricular rhythm originating from a discrete atrial focus (outside the AV node), regular, rate >100/min. Subtypes: focal, macroreentrant, multifocal (see MAT).

2. Key Features

  • Focal AT:
  • Discrete focus, often from crista terminalis, PVs, septum, CS ostium
  • Mechanism: enhanced automaticity, triggered activity, microreentry
  • P-wave morphology different from sinus (depends on focus)
  • Usually paroxysmal; can be sustained
  • Sensitivity to adenosine (transient AV block) helps confirm atrial origin
  • Macroreentrant AT:
  • Reentry around an anatomical obstacle (scar, suture line, prior surgery)
  • Includes "atypical" atrial flutter, post-ablation tachycardias
  • Continuous P-wave morphology during tachycardia
  • P-wave: usually upright in inferior leads if from high right atrium; inverted if from low atrial/AVN
  • Differential: AVNRT, AVRT, atrial flutter, sinus tachycardia
  • Causes: digitals toxicity, electrolyte disturbance, structural heart disease, post-ablation
  • Diagnosis: ECG, ambulatory monitoring, EP study

3. Management

  • Acute:
  • Vagal manoeuvres
  • Adenosine (6 → 12 mg) — terminates triggered AT, transient block in others
  • β-blocker or CCB (verapamil, diltiazem) for rate control
  • Synchronised cardioversion if unstable
  • Chronic:
  • β-blocker or CCB first-line
  • Class IC (flecainide, propafenone) for "pill-in-pocket"
  • Catheter ablation (highly effective, >90% for focal AT)
  • Treat underlying: electrolyte correction, stop offending drugs
  • Anticoagulation if persistent (per CHA2DS2-VASc)
Pearls
- AT has abnormal P-wave (atrial origin)
- Adenosine = diagnostic (transient AV block exposes AT)
- Focal AT ablation = highly effective
- Macroreentrant AT = scar-related (post-ablation/surgery)

topics.title: Non Sustained Vt Nsvt
topics.html:
1. Definition

NSVT = ≥3 consecutive ventricular beats at >100 bpm, lasting <30 s and terminating spontaneously without haemodynamic collapse. Common, often asymptomatic, but can be a marker of underlying structural heart disease and risk of sustained VT/SCD.

2. Key Features

  • Asymptomatic in 80% (incidental on Holter/ambulatory ECG)
  • Symptomatic: palpitations, presyncope, dyspnoea, chest pain
  • Causes:
  • Ischaemic heart disease (post-MI scar) — most important
  • Dilated CM (any cause)
  • HCM
  • ARVC
  • Electrolyte disturbance (hypokalaemia, hypomagnesaemia)
  • Drugs (antiarrhythmics, QT-prolonging)
  • Idiopathic (RVOT, fascicular)
  • Significance:
  • Post-MI: LVEF is the main determinant of prognosis
  • LVEF ≤35% + NSVT = ↑ SCD risk → ICD considered
  • In MADIT-II and MUSTT, NSVT + LVEF ≤35–40% benefited from ICD
  • Diagnosis: 24–48 h Holter, event recorder, implantable loop recorder

3. Management

  • Identify underlying cause: echo, CMR, stress test, coronary angiography
  • Correct electrolyte abnormalities (K⁺ >4.0, Mg²⁺ >2.0)
  • Stop offending drugs (QT-prolonging, antiarrhythmics)
  • β-blocker for symptom/recurrence control
  • If structural heart disease + LVEF ≤35%: ICD for primary prevention
  • LVEF >40% + NSVT: usually no specific therapy beyond β-blocker
  • Catheter ablation if recurrent symptomatic idiopathic NSVT (RVOT, fascicular)
  • EP study may be considered in intermediate risk (e.g., LVEF 36–40%)
Pearls
- NSVT = ≥3 beats, <30 s, self-terminating
- Post-MI + LVEF ≤35% + NSVT = ICD (primary prevention)
- Idiopathic NSVT (RVOT, fascicular) = benign, ablation curative
- Always correct K⁺ and Mg²⁺ first

topics.title: Idiopathic Vt Rvot Lvot Fascicular
topics.html:
1. Etiology & Pathophysiology

Etiology & Pathophysiology

Sinus node dysfunction: idiopathic fibrosis (ageing, ~70%), ischaemia (RCA, sinus node artery), infiltrative (amyloid, sarcoid), surgical (transplant, Mustard, Senning), drugs (beta-blocker, CCB, digoxin, amiodarone, clonidine, ivabradine), autonomic (vagal — cough, micturition, sleep, athletes), familial (HCN4, SCN5A).

2. Classification

Clinical Features

Asymptomatic (incidental). Symptoms: fatigue, exercise intolerance, dyspnoea, syncope/pre-syncope (Stokes-Adams attacks), confusion, cognitive decline, HF, AF with slow ventricular response, bradycardia-dependent VT (pause-dependent torsades). Examination: bradycardia, cannon A waves (3rd degree AV block, AV dissociation), variable S1 intensity (AV block), JVP with intermittent a wave (cannon).

3. Clinical Features

Diagnostic Approach

12-lead ECG: sinus bradycardia, sinus arrest/pause, sinoatrial block, junctional escape, AF with slow response, AV block. 24h Holter for paroxysmal. Event recorder/ILR for infrequent. Stress test for chronotropic incompetence. EP study (sinus node recovery time >1500ms, AV Wenckebach <120bpm, HV interval >70ms, infra-Hisian block). Echo (structural, infiltrative).

4. Diagnosis & Investigations

Severity Assessment & Risk Stratification

Pauses >3s awake, >5s asleep = significant. Mobitz II, high-grade, 3rd degree = unstable, may progress to asystole. SCD in untreated complete heart block (high if LV dysfunction). Syncope = high risk for SCD if underlying structural disease. Symptoms dictate treatment.

Differential Diagnosis

Vasovagal syncope, orthostatic hypotension, reflex syncope, neurocardiogenic, carotid sinus hypersensitivity, seizures, hypoglycaemia, drug toxicity, hypothyroidism, hypothermia, raised ICP, MI, electrolyte, aortic stenosis.

Investigations

12-lead ECG, 24h Holter, event recorder/ILR, echo, TFT, electrolyte, drug levels, troponin, coronary angio (if ischaemic), EP study (selected), tilt-table test (vasovagal), sleep study, Lyme serology.

5. Management

Management Algorithm

Acute symptomatic: atropine 0.5-1mg IV (max 3mg), isoprenaline/aminophylline (if atropine fails), transcutaneous pacing (bridge), transvenous pacing, adrenaline (cardiac arrest). Permanent pacemaker (PPM) for: (1) Symptomatic sinus bradycardia/pauses, (2) Sick sinus syndrome (3) 3rd degree AV block (any), (4) Mobitz II (2nd degree), (5) High-grade AV block (≥2 consecutive P waves blocked), (6) Mobitz I if symptomat

Special Populations

Elderly: more common, more sick sinus, more iatrogenic. Athletes: vagal bradycardia, AV block (1st, Mobitz I) at rest, normal physiology. Pregnancy: rarely requires PPM, prefer temporary if needed. Inferior MI: AV node ischaemia, usually transient, atropine. Anterior MI: infranodal block, complete heart block, requires PPM, often permanent. Post-TAVI: conduction injury (10-25%), especially with pre-existing RBBB.

6. Complications & Prognosis

Complications & Prognosis

Syncope, HF (loss of AV synchrony, ↑LV filling pressure), Pacemaker syndrome (loss of AV synchrony in VVI mode — fatigue, syncope, hypotension), Battery depletion (5-10y), Lead complications (dislodgement 1-5%, fracture, infection 0.5-2%, pneumothorax 1%), Infections (endocarditis 0.5-2%), Twiddler syndrome (patient manipulates), Failure to capture/sense (high threshold, lead fracture, EMI), SCD in untreated.

topics.title: Channelopathies Long Qt Brugada Cpvt Short Qt
topics.html:
1. Long QT Syndrome (LQTS)

  • LQT1 (KCNQ1, swimming triggers, exercise — especially swimming)
  • LQT2 (KCNH2, K+, auditory triggers, emotion, postpartum)
  • LQT3 (SCN5A, Na+, sleep/rest)
  • Diagnosis: QTc ≥480 ms (definite), or Schwartz score
  • Rx: beta-blocker (propranolol, nadolol), avoid QT-prolonging drugs, ICD if high risk, LCSD for refractory
  • LQT1: avoid competitive swimming

2. Brugada Syndrome

  • SCN5A (Na+), autosomal dominant
  • Type 1 Brugada pattern: coved ST ↑ ≥2 mm in V1-V2 with negative T
  • Triggers: fever, sodium channel blockers, vagal tone, sleep
  • Risk stratification: spontaneous type 1, syncope, VF, family history
  • Rx: ICD if symptomatic/high risk; quinidine for VT storm; fever control critical
  • Avoid Class IC antiarrhythmics, cocaine, alcohol binge

3. CPVT (Catecholaminergic Polymorphic VT)

  • RyR2 (autosomal dominant) or CASQ2 (recessive)
  • Exercise/emotion-induced bidirectional VT
  • Normal resting ECG
  • Rx: nadolol (max dose) + flecainide; ICD if breakthrough; LCSD

4. Short QT Syndrome

  • Rare, very short QTc (<330 ms)
  • AF, VF risk
  • Rx: ICD, quinidine
Pearls — All channelopathies: avoid triggers, treat with beta-blocker (nadolol often), consider ICD based on risk. Brugada + fever = code! Cool aggressively. CPVT can present as SCD in young athletes.

topics.title: Av Block 1St 2Nd Type I Ii 3Rd Degree
topics.html:
1. Definition

Atrioventricular (AV) block = delayed or absent conduction from atria to ventricles. Classified by severity: 1st degree, 2nd degree (Mobitz I/Wenckebach, Mobitz II), 3rd degree (complete). Localisation (AV node vs His-Purkinje) determines prognosis and treatment.

2. Key Features

  • 1st degree: PR >200 ms; every P conducted; usually benign
  • 2nd degree Mobitz I (Wenckebach): progressive PR prolongation then dropped beat; usually AV nodal; benign, often vagal; rarely needs pacing
  • 2nd degree Mobitz II: fixed PR with intermittent dropped beats; infranisian (His-Purkinje); unstable, can progress to 3rd; pacing indicated
  • 3rd degree (complete): AV dissociation; P and QRS independent
  • Narrow QRS escape (~40–60): AV nodal block; usually stable
  • Wide QRS escape (~20–40): infranisian; unstable → asystole
  • Causes: ischaemia (inferior = AV node, anterior = infranisian), age-related fibrosis, drugs (β-blocker, CCB, digoxin), electrolyte (hyperkalaemia), Lyme disease, sarcoidosis, post-cardiac surgery, congenital
  • Symptoms: asymptomatic, fatigue, syncope (Stokes-Adams), HF

3. Management

  • Asymptomatic 1st / Mobitz I: observe; treat cause
  • Symptomatic bradycardia: atropine (0.5–1 mg IV), isoprenaline infusion, transcutaneous/transvenous pacing
  • Permanent pacemaker:
  • Class I: Mobitz II, 3rd degree AV block, post-MI persistent block (especially anterior)
  • Class IIa: bifascicular block + syncope + EP-infarction
  • Avoid in infranisian block: treat with pacing (atropine may not work)
  • Stop offending drugs (β-blocker, CCB, digoxin)
  • Reversible causes: ischaemia, drugs, electrolytes, Lyme
Pearls
- Mobitz I = AV node (benign); Mobitz II = His-Purkinje (unstable)
- Anterior MI + AV block = pacing needed; inferior MI + AV block = often transient
- Narrow QRS escape = AV node; wide QRS escape = infranisian (worse)
- 3rd degree block + symptoms = permanent pacemaker

4. Diagnosis & Investigations

Severity Assessment & Risk Stratification

Severity assessment uses: (1) Clinical scoring systems (GRACE/TIMI/HEART for ACS, NYHA/INTERMACS for HF, CHA2DS2-VASc/HAS-BLED for AF, Child-Pugh/MELD for liver, KDIGO for renal, EuroSCORE/STS for surgical risk). (2) Biomarkers (peak troponin, BNP, hs-CRP). (3) Imaging parameters (LVEF, valve area, regurgitation volume, PASP, RV function). (4) Functional capacity (6MWD, CPET with VO2 max, NYHA class).

Differential Diagnosis

Differential diagnosis of SCD epidemiology and risk stratification includes related cardiovascular conditions and non-cardiac mimics. Cardiovascular: ischaemic heart disease (stable angina, ACS, microvascular angina, vasospasm), hypertensive heart disease (LVH, HFpEF, aortic dissection), valvular heart disease (AS, AR, MS, MR, TR, prosthetic), cardiomyopathy (DCM, HCM, RCM, ARVC, Takotsubo, infiltrative — amyloid, sarcoid, haemochromatosi

Investigations

Investigations for SCD epidemiology and risk stratification: Bedside: ECG (12-lead, serial, rhythm strip), vital signs (BP, HR, RR, SpO2), JVP, urine output, glucometer, capillary refill.

topics.title: Primary Prevention Icd Hfref Hcm Arvc Channelopathies
topics.html:
The Implantable Cardioverter Defibrillator (ICD) is the cornerstone device therapy for sudden cardiac death (SCD) prevention.

1. Definition & Background

  • ICD: subcutaneous or transvenous device that continuously monitors rhythm and delivers tiered therapy (ATP, cardioversion, defibrillation shock) for VT/VF.
  • Primary prevention — implant in patients at risk of SCD but no history of sustained VT/VF arrest.
  • Secondary prevention — implant in patients who have already survived sustained VT/VF or sudden cardiac arrest (SCA), where cause is non-reversible.

2. Secondary Prevention (Class I)

Indicated in patients surviving:

  • Cardiac arrest due to VT/VF (not within 48 h of acute MI, not due to reversible cause — electrolyte, drug, hypoxia).
  • Sustained VT with haemodynamic compromise.
  • Sustained VT without compromise but with reduced LVEF (≤35%).
  • Syncope + inducible sustained VT/VF on EP study.
  • Exclude reversible causes (acute ischaemia, electrolyte disturbance, drug-induced, commotio cordis, sepsis).

3. Primary Prevention — HFrEF

Implant if all of:

  • LVEF ≤35% despite ≥3 months of optimal guideline-directed medical therapy (GDMT) — ARNI/ACEi/ARB + BB + MRA + SGLT2i.
  • NYHA II–III (NYHA I — consider if additional risk markers).
  • Ischaemic cardiomyopathy (≥40 d post-MI) OR non-ischaemic dilated cardiomyopathy (≥3 mo on GDMT).
  • Expected survival >1 year with good functional status.
  • MADIT-II (post-MI LVEF ≤30%), SCD-HeFT (ischaemic + non-ischaemic LVEF ≤35%, NYHA II–III), DEFINITE (NICM).

4. Primary Prevention — Other Conditions

ConditionIndication
HCMESC 5-yr SCD risk ≥6%, or prior VT/VF, or unexplained syncope with massive LVH (≥30 mm), or NSVT + family SCD + massive LVH
ARVCSustained VT/VF, or extensive RV dysfunction, or syncope + inducible VT
LQT / SQTSSurvivors of cardiac arrest (secondary); consider for high-risk genotype + syncope despite BB
BrugadaSurvivors of arrest (secondary); spontaneous type-1 ECG + syncope + positive EP study
CPVTSurvivors of arrest; recurrent syncope/VT despite BB + flecainide
Sarcoid cardiacSustained VT or LVEF ≤35% on immunosuppression
Congenital HD repairedSpecific criteria (post-TOF with inducible VT, single ventricle)
  • Confirm LVEF on recent imaging (echo, CMR, MUGA) and document GDMT optimisation.
  • Exclude reversible causes of LV dysfunction (revascularise, treat tachycardia, alcohol cessation, thiamine).
  • Life expectancy >1 y, NYHA status, frailty, comorbidities, patient wishes (shared decision).
  • Screen for psychological impact — device anxiety, deactivation discussions at end of life.
  • Imaging — venous anatomy, ICD type (single/dual chamber, CRT-D, S-ICD).
  • Single-chamber VVI — most primary prevention (AF <1% at implant).

topics.title: Secondary Prevention Icd
topics.html:
1. Etiology & Pathophysiology

Etiology & Pathophysiology

N/A — device. Detection: VT zone (≥180-200 bpm, ATP first, then shock if no conversion), VF zone (≥250 bpm, immediate shock). Modern devices: dual-chamber (atrial + ventricular), CRT-D (biventricular for HF), subcutaneous (S-ICD, no transvenous lead, less infection, but no pacing/ATP).

2. Classification

Clinical Features

Pre-implant: history (syncope, SCD, family Hx), ECG (QRS duration — for CRT-D, BBB morphology), echo (LVEF, structural), coronary angio (if ischaemic, revascularise first), electrolytes, BNP, medications. Post-implant: wound check (1-2 weeks), device interrogation (every 3-6 months), ECG (sensing, threshold, capture, lead function), chest X-ray (lead position), medications, driving restrictions (UK — 6 months primary prevention, 2 years s

3. Diagnosis & Investigations

Severity Assessment & Risk Stratification

N/A. Indication severity — primary prevention (LVEF ≤35%, NYHA II-III), secondary (SCD survivor). Risk of device infection, lead failure, inappropriate shocks, generator change (5-9y battery).

Differential Diagnosis

N/A. Differential for inappropriate shock: SVT, AF with rapid response, T-wave oversensing, lead fracture, EMI, myopotentials, atrial sensing of VT (in single chamber).

Investigations

ECG (QRS, BBB, LVH), echo (LVEF, structural), coronary angio (if ischaemic), BNP, electrolytes, Holter (NSVT for risk), electrophysiology study (inducibility, ablation), CXR (post-implant), device interrogation (sensing, threshold, lead impedance, battery — every 3-6 months).

4. Management

Management Algorithm

Implant technique: left pre-pectoral, subclavian or axillary vein access, RV lead to apex or septum, atrial lead to RA appendage, CS lead for LV (CRT-D). Settings: VT zone (ATP first — 91% effective for VT ≤200bpm, less shocks, less pain, less anxiety, less mortality — MADIT-RIT, ADVANCE III, PAIN-FREE), VF zone (shock).

Special Populations

Children: ICD, S-ICD screening. Pregnancy: pre-pregnancy counselling, generator replacement before. Elderly: more complications, careful selection. Athletes: ICD approved, contact sports avoid. Drivers: UK 6 months primary, 2 years secondary. MRI conditional: most modern devices, magnet mode. End-of-life: deactivate in terminally ill (respect patient autonomy, no SCD, allow natural death).

5. Complications & Prognosis

Complications & Prognosis

Infection (1-3%, endocarditis, generator pocket, lead infection — antibiotics, extraction, replacement). Lead failure (5-15% at 10y, conductor fracture, insulation break, lead noise, inappropriate shocks). Generator depletion (5-9y, replacement). Inappropriate shocks (5-20%, AF with rapid response, SVT, T-wave oversensing, lead fracture — painful, anxiety, ↑mortality — MADIT-RIT, Hinkle).

topics.title: Acute Af Management Rate Vs Rhythm Cardioversion
topics.html:
1. Definition & Background

Atrial fibrillation (AF): most common sustained arrhythmia, irregular atrial activation (350-600 bpm), irregularly irregular ventricular response. Loss of atrial kick → 20% drop in CO. Risk of LA appendage thrombus → stroke. Classification: paroxysmal (<7d, self-terminating), persistent (>7d, requires intervention), long-standing persistent (>1y), permanent (accepted).

2. Etiology & Pathophysiology

Etiology & Pathophysiology

Focal triggers (PVs in 90%, SVC, coronary sinus, ligament of Marshall) + substrate (atrial remodelling — electrical, structural, contractile; fibrosis from age, HTN, HF, valvular disease). Three pillars: triggers (PVs), substrate (fibrosis), perpetuators (rotors, multiple wavelets). Risk factors: age, HTN, HF, valvular disease, DM, obesity, OSA, alcohol, hyperthyroidism, surgery, inflammation, genetic.

3. Classification

Clinical Features

Symptoms: palpitations (irregular), dyspnoea, fatigue, exercise intolerance, chest pain, syncope/pre-syncope, polyuria (ANP release). Asymptomatic in 25-40% (lone AF, paroxysmal, elderly, diabetic). Examination: irregularly irregular pulse, pulse deficit (apex > radial), absent a waves in JVP, variable S1.

4. Clinical Features

Diagnostic Approach

12-lead ECG (gold standard): irregularly irregular RR, no discernible P waves, fibrillatory waves (f-waves, best in V1), QRS narrow (unless BBB/aberrancy), ventricular rate variable. Confirm with rhythm strip or Holter if paroxysmal. Differential: multifocal AT (≥3 P morphologies, irregular), atrial flutter with variable block (sawtooth F waves), frequent PACs (irregular, premature beats).

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

Severity Assessment & Risk Stratification

CHA2DS2-VASc (stroke risk): CHF (1), HTN (1), Age ≥75 (2), DM (1), Stroke/TIA/TE (2), Vascular disease (1), Age 65-74 (1), Sex female (1). Score 0 (M) / 1 (F): no anticoag. Score 1 (M) / 2 (F): consider. Score ≥2 (M) / ≥3 (F): anticoag. HAS-BLED (bleeding): HTN, Abnormal renal/liver, Stroke, Bleeding, Labile INR, Elderly (>65), Drugs/alcohol. EHRA symptom class: I-IV.

Differential Diagnosis

Multifocal AT (≥3 P morphologies, irregular but P waves), atrial flutter with variable block (sawtooth F waves, especially inferior leads), AVNRT/AVRT (regular, narrow), atrial tachycardia with variable block, sinus rhythm with frequent PACs (premature), atrial bigeminy. Non-cardiac: hyperthyroidism, anaemia, electrolyte, drugs, pulmonary disease, anxiety.

Investigations

ECG (12-lead, rhythm strip). Holter (24-48h) for paroxysmal. Event recorder/ILR for infrequent. Echo (LA size, LV function, valves, PASP). Bloods: FBC, U&E, LFT, TFT (always — hyperthyroidism), coagulation, BNP, troponin. CXR (pulmonary oedema, cardiomegaly).
